title: English Composition I
description: A college-level introduction to effective written composition for academic, vocational, and occupational students, with emphasis on exposition. Formerly ENGL 101
prerequisites: Appropriate placement test score, or IE 045/046/047 (level 4) with a 3.0 or better, or IE 100/101/102 (level 5) with a 2.0 or better, or BSTEC 150 and 250 with a 2.5 or better, or ENGL 091 with a 3.3 or better, or ENGL 099 with grade of 2.00 or better, or English 101 instructor permission.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Principles of Accounting I
description: Accounting as an information system, the accounting cycle, accounting for a merchandising operation, cash, receivables, and inventories.

title: Principles of Accounting II
description: Includes accounting for fixed assets, liabilities, partnerships and corporations. Also includes the statement of cash flows as well as the underlying principles of accounting. Prerequisite: ACCT& 201 .

title: Principles of Accounting III
description: Development and analysis of accounting information for managerial decision-making. Prerequisite: ACCT& 202 and high school algebra or its equivalent.

title: Orientation to Adult Education
description: An introduction to Olympic College and its Adult Education Program. Students learn to set goals, make an educational plan, and assess their own progress.

title: Communication Skills 2
description: Students who complete this course will read for literal comprehension, find and interpret information from common references, write several related sentences, and use a computer for routine tasks.

title: Communication Skills 3
description: Students who complete this course can identify their goals, define and support a reading purpose, write clear narratives of a paragraph or more, and use a computer to perform routine tasks.

title: Communication Skills 4
description: Students who complete this course will be able to use reading and writing skills in personally relevant contexts, use resources to collect and interpret information, and use a computer to perform routine tasks.

title: Communication Skills 5
description: Students who complete this course will be able write clearly using standard grammar, usage, and punctuation; collect, interpret, and integrate information using multiple resources, and use a computer to complete routine tasks.

title: Math 5
description: Students who complete this course will further develop their skills to effectively communicate and use mathematical operations up to introductory algebra and geometry.

title: GED Prep 1
description: The GED Test is composed of 4 tests: Reasoning through Language Arts, Math, Social Studies, and Science. Students taking the GED Preparation course come in with a foundation in reading, writing, and math and need a refresh. If students need more detailed foundational instruction, then they will be registered in that subject specific TSABE course. Within GED Prep 1, 2, and 3 a student will move from refining basic reading, writing, speaking and listening, and math skills to proficient skills in each of these areas. Upon completion of GED Prep 1, 2, and 3, a student will be able to confidently pass the 4 tests that comprise the GED Test. GED Prep 1 focuses on interpreting fiction and non-fiction, conventions of English grammar, writing informative/explanatory text, and arithmetic. An overview of Civics and Earth Science will be contextualized within the Language Arts content. This course aligns with the Reading, Writing, Speaking/Listening standards as described in the WA SBCTC College and Career Readiness Standards, as well as some of the WA OSPI Civics and Earth Science standards.

title: GED Prep 2
description: The GED Test is composed of 4 tests: Reasoning through Language Arts, Math, Social Studies, and Science. Students taking the GED Preparation course come in with a foundation in reading, writing, and math and need a refresh. If students need more detailed foundational instruction, then they will be registered in that subject specific TSABE course. Within GED Prep 1, 2, and 3 a student will move from refining basic reading, writing, speaking and listening, and math skills to proficient skills in each of these areas. Upon completion of GED Prep 1, 2, and 3, a student will be able to confidently pass the 4 tests that comprise the GED Test. GED Prep 2 focuses on analyzing non-fiction, grammar, writing about read text, data, and Algebra basics. An overview of U.S. History and Life Science will be contextualized within the Language Arts content. This course aligns with the Reading, Writing, Speaking/Listening standards as described in the WA SBCTC College and Career Readiness Standards, as well as some of the WA OSPI Life Science and History standards.

title: GED Prep 3
description: The GED Test is composed of 4 tests: Reasoning Through Language Arts, Math, Social Studies, and Science. Students taking the GED Preparation course come in with a foundation in reading, writing, and math and need a refresh. If students need more detailed foundational instruction, then they will be registered in that subject specific TSABE course. Within GED Prep 1, 2, and 3 a student will move from refining basic reading, writing, speaking and listening, and math skills to proficient skills in each of these areas. Upon completion of GED Prep 1, 2, and 3, a student will be able to confidently pass the 4 tests that comprise the GED Test. GED Prep 3 focuses on honing critical thinking skills, writing skills, and interpreting written text. Algebra skills are applied to more advanced equations. An overview of Economics, Geography, World Issues, and Physical Science will be contextualized within the Language Arts content. This course aligns with the Reading, Writing, and Speaking/Listening standards as described in the WA SBCTC College and Career Readiness Standards, as well as some of the WA OSPI Physical Science, Economics, and Geography standards.

title: Biology
description: Students study an overview of scientific thinking, cellular biology, the fundamentals of genetics, and characteristics of plants. As students understand the nature of life, they are prepared to make informed decisions about health, medicine, genetics, and their environment. This course aligns with WA OSPI HS Biology Student Learning Outcomes, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D. Formerly ADABE 41, 51, 61,71 (Communications 2,3,4, and 5)
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Earth and Space
description: Students will study geologic structures and forces, the waters on our planet, the atmospheric forces that shape our world, the geosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, atmosphere, the geochemical cycles of the Earth, ecological interactions between living and nonliving components of the environment, the solar system, and the universe. This course aligns with WA OSPI HS Earth and Space Student Learning Outcomes, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D. Formerly ADABE 41, 51, 61, 71 (Communications 2,3, 4, 5)
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Chemistry
description: Students will study scientific thinking; the composition, properties and changes of matter; atomic structure and theory; periodicity; molecules; and chemical equations. This course aligns with WA OSPI Chemistry Student Learning Outcomes, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D. Formerly ADABE 41, 51, 61, 71 (Communications 2,3, 4, 5)
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Washington State History
description: Students study an overview of the history of Washington State as it applies to geographic, political, economic and cultural perspectives. This course aligns with WA OSPI Social Studies Student Learning Standards, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D.
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Civics
description: Students study an overview of government, democracy, structure of the United States Government, federal vs state, federal policies, civil rights, civil liberties, and civil responsibilities. This course aligns with WA OSPI HS Social Studies Student Learning Standards, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D.
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: US History
description: In U.S. History, students will study an overview of U.S. History from the birth of our country to current. Within the course, learners build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Level A - D. Formerly ADABE 86
prerequisites: Orientation/placement or permission of instructor

title: Contemporary World Issues
description: In Contemporary World Issues, the student will explore and discover happenings across the globe in the context of 9 different issues that are currently impacting our planet. The student will gain a wide understanding of Globalization and World Geography. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D. Formerly ADABE 087
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement or Permission of Instructor

title: Fundamentals of Reading and Writing 5
description: Formerly ADABE 073
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Fundamentals of Reading and Writing 6
description: Students develop strategies to find and interpret information from common reading activities. Students who complete this course will be able to fix standard English grammar, usage, and punctuation. Students will identify their writing goals, write clear narratives of two paragraphs in length, and use a computer to complete routine tasks. Formerly ADABE 075
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: ABE Transitions Reading and Writing 5
description: Students expand strategies to find and interpret information from common reading activities. Students who complete this course will be able to demonstrate understanding of standard English grammar, usage, and punctuation. Students will identify their writing goals, write clear narratives of three paragraphs in length, and use a computer to complete routine tasks. Formerly ADABE 088
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Transitions Reading and Writing 6
description: Students expand strategies to find and interpret information from common reading activities. Students who complete this course will be able to demonstrate understanding of standard English grammar, usage, and punctuation. Students will identify their writing goals, write clear narratives of three paragraphs in length, and use a computer to complete routine tasks. Formerly ADABE 089
prerequisites: Orientation & Placement

title: Arithmetic A
description: In Arithmetic A, students will develop in mathematical reasoning to outcomes described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Level C and D. These include whole number and decimal operations in addition, subtraction, multiplication and division; operations and practical applications of fractions; operations and practical applications of proportions; and operations and practical applications of percentages. Formerly ADABE 042
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement or Permission of Instructor

title: Arithmetic B - Pre-Algebra
description: In Arithmetic B - Pre-Algebra, students will continue development in mathematical reasoning to outcomes described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Level C and D including the review of operations with decimals; evaluating data; and instruction in operations with integers, algebraic expressions, introducing functions, combining like terms, and beginning factoring and distributing. Formerly ADABE 052
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement, Permission of Instructor, or ADABE 042 with a grade of 2.00 or better

title: Algebra 1
description: In Algebra 1, students will continue development in algebraic reasoning to outcomes described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Level D and E including the review of algebraic operations with integers, algebraic expressions, introducing functions, combining like terms, and beginning factoring and distributing; and instruction in solving equalities and inequalities (one, two and multi-step), coordinate grid, linear equations, operations with polynomials, and exponents. Formerly ADABE 062
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement, Permission of Instructor, or ADABE 052 with a grade of 2.00 or better

title: Algebra 2
description: Learners continue development in algebraic reasoning to outcomes described in CCRS’s Level D and E including linear equations, quadratic equations, other trinomial equations, rational equations, systems of equations, inequalities, operations with polynomials and operations with radicals.
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement, Permission of Instructor, or TSABE 085 with a grade of 2.0 or better

title: Geometry
description: Students continue development in mathematical and algebraic reasoning to outcomes described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Level C, D and E. Topics and principles will include coordinate and spatial geometry, angles, parallel lines, polygons and other figures, circles, surface area, volume, transformations, the Pythagorean Theorem, congruent and similar triangles, and introductory trigonometry. Formerly ADABE 082
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement, Permission of Instructor, or TSABE 085 with a grade of 2.0 or better

title: Physical Fitness
description: In Physical Fitness, the student will gain an understanding of the value of physical fitness and the variety of physical activities that can be used to achieve this fitness. Students will also maintain an exercise log throughout the course with the goal of establishing habits that will continue upon completion of the course. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Research Projects
description: In PE - Research Projects, the student will conduct research on topics related to physical fitness and healthy physical and mental habits that impact individuals, families and communities. Students will also maintain an exercise log throughout the course. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Health
description: In Health, the student will gain an understanding of what impacts physical, mental and emotional health both in positive and negative ways. Students will evaluate how they manage their time, eating habits, and habits that affect their mental and emotional health. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Biology
description: In Biology, students will study the scientific method, technological design, cells, plants, science vs pseudoscience, and the impact that humans have on the environment. This course aligns with WA OSPI Biology Student Learning Outcomes, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D.
prerequisites: Placement and orientation

title: Chemistry
description: In Chemistry, students will study scientific thinking; the composition, properties and changes of matter; atomic structure and theory; periodicity; molecules; and chemical equations. This course aligns with WA OSPI Chemistry Student Learning Outcomes, and within the course, learners also build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D.
prerequisites: Placement & Orientation

title: US History
description: In U.S History, students will study an overview of U.S. History from the birth of our country to current. Within the course, learners build their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.
prerequisites: Placement & Orientation or instructor permission

title: Art History
description: In Art History, the student will explore and analyze Pre-History Art, European Art, American Art and research into the life of a renown artist. This gained knowledge will be preparatory to an Art Museum visit. Additionally, students will learn about Movie Review and Critique. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Independent Art Projects
description: In Independent Art Projects, the student will learn about different mediums of art. Then, in collaboration with the instructor, the student may choose to experiment with a variety of mediums of art through multiple small projects through the quarter, or they may choose to focus on one medium of art to produce one “bigger” project over the course of the quarter. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Computer Skills
description: In Occupational Ed - Computer Skills, the student will master the digital skills needed to work, learn and participate fully in daily life. Instruction will include basic computer skills, internet basics, Windows OS, Excel, PowerPoint, and general information literacy. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.

title: Occupational Ed - College & Career Success Skills
description: In Occupational Ed - College and Career Success Skills, the student will gain an understanding of and practice personal skills and habits for success in both the academic and career worlds. Additionally, the student will study aspects of personal financial literacy and academic financial aid. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.
prerequisites: Placement & Orientation or instructor permission

title: Occupational Ed - Career Exploration
description: In Occupational Ed - Career Exploration, the student will explore resources that outline different career paths and develop a personal plan of action leading to a career of interest. Additionally, the student will develop a personal resume, cover letter, a concise introductory speech, and participate in a mock interview. Within the course, learners build on their skills in reading, writing, speaking and listening as described in WA College and Career Readiness Standards Levels A - D and meet the requirements of the WA OSPI.
prerequisites: Placement & Orientation or instructor permission

title: ESL Civics Education
description: Students are introduced to broad concepts and responsibilities of good citizenship while participating as active community members and building English language communication skills. 1-

title: ESOL 1 Speaking and Listening
description: Introduce students to basic survival English. It is designed for students who are true language beginners. 3-

title: ESOL 1 Reading and Writing
description: Introduce students to beginning English literacy skills. Designed for students who have just started to learn basic survival English. 2-

title: ESOL 2 Speaking and Listening
description: Built upon language skills beyond beginning English literacy; for students who are at a low beginning level of language learning. 3-

title: ESOL 2 Reading and Writing
description: Built upon language skills beyond survival English literacy; for students who are at a low beginning level of language learning. 2-

title: ESOL 3 Speaking and Listening
description: Built upon language skills beyond low-beginning English literacy; for students at a high-beginning level of language learning. 3-

title: ESOL 3 Reading and Writing
description: Built upon language skills beyond low-beginning English literacy; for students who are at a high-beginning level of language learning. 2-

title: ESOL 4 Speaking and Listening
description: Built upon language skills beyond intermediate English. It is designed for students who are at a high-intermediate/low-advanced level of language learning. 3-

title: ESOL 4 Reading and Writing
description: This course builds upon language skills beyond low-intermediate English literacy; for students who are at an intermediate level of language learning. 2-

title: ESOL 5 Bridge Speaking and Listening
description: Built upon language skills beyond low intermediate English for high intermediate level learners to prepare for advanced English classes. 3-

title: ESOL 5 Bridge Reading and Writing
description: Build upon high-intermediate language skills. It is designed for students with a high level of English fluency. 2-

title: ESOL 6 Bridge Speaking and Listening
description: Build upon language skills beyond high-intermediate English. Designed for advanced level learners to prepare for matriculating to college credit classes. 3-

title: ESOL 6 Bridge Reading and Writing
description: Designed for students at an advanced level of English. This course introduces students to the linguistic, cultural and study skills necessary for matriculating into college level classes at a community college. 2-

title: Beginning Pronunciation ESL
description: Improving pronunciation of American English for community, academic, and workplace settings for beginning students with basic English skills.
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement Testing

title: Intermediate Pronunciation ESL
description: Improve pronunciation of American English for community, academic, and workplace settings for students with intermediate English skills.
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement Testing; ADESL 085 - Advanced Pronunciation ESL

title: Conversational English
description: Students develop conversational skills in English by discussing self, family, work, community and current events.

title: Fundamentals of Grammar
description: A review of English grammar for non-native English speakers who have a high to intermediate to advanced vocabulary.
prerequisites: Orientation/Placement Testing

title: Bridge to College Success
description: Designed for non-native speakers of English, this course provides ESOL instruction in the context of transitioning into college level courses. Students will learn how to navigate the American college system from registration through graduation while improving their English skills in reading, writing, listening, and speaking. They will become familiar with the culture, systems, and processes necessary for successful transitions into American higher education.
prerequisites: ADESL 009 or ADABE 009, minimum level 4 ESOL placement (ADESL 060 or 061). Concurrent enrollment in a core ESOL class,

title: ESOL Foundations
description: This course is designed for beginning ESOL learners who want to build essential reading, writing, speaking, listening, and numeracy skills. Students will learn to recognize and write the alphabet, sounds associated with each letter, and read common sight words. Basic numeracy skills will be introduced. This course will help learners develop confidence in foundational literacy and numeracy to support daily life, work, and further education.
prerequisites: Placement into level upon completion of required ESOL Orientation (TSESL009) or by instructor permission.

title: Orientation to ESOL
description: An introduction to the English to Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) program at Olympic College. Students complete assessments to identify appropriate program level placement. Students will express personal learning goals and are introduced to campus and college resources that support their success. May be taken twice a year.

title: ESOL 1 - Integrated Skills
description: This course introduces students to basic English. It is designed for students with foundational English skills to begin developing additional reading, writing, listening, and speaking skills. Students in this level are moving from word production to complete sentences and questions.
prerequisites: Placement determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL001.

title: ESOL 1A - Integrated Skills
description: This course introduces students to basic English. It is designed for students with foundational English skills to begin developing additional reading, writing, listening, and speaking skills. Students in this level are moving from word production to complete sentences and questions.
prerequisites: Placement determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL001.

title: ESOL 1B - Integrated Skills
description: This course introduces students to basic English. It is designed for students with foundational English skills to begin developing additional reading, writing, listening, and speaking skills. Students in this level are moving from word production to complete sentences and questions.
prerequisites: Placement determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL001.

title: ESOL 2 - Integrated Skills
description: This course is designed for non-native English speakers at the high-beginner level. Students develop basic communication skills in this integrated English course with a focus on everyday language for speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Instruction is skill-based to support students’ personal language needs. Students will build comprehension and fluency around familiar topics.
prerequisites: Placement is determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL 010, 011, or 012.

title: ESOL 2A Integrated Skills
description: This course is designed for non-native English speakers at the high-beginner level. Students develop basic communication skills in this integrated English course with a focus on everyday language for speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Instruction is skill-based to support students’ personal language needs. Students will build comprehension and fluency around familiar topics.
prerequisites: Placement is determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL 010, 011, or 012.

title: ESOL 2B - Integrated Skills
description: This course is designed for non-native English speakers at the high-beginner level. Students develop basic communication skills in this integrated English course with a focus on everyday language for speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Instruction is skill-based to support students’ personal language needs. Students will build comprehension and fluency around familiar topics.
prerequisites: Placement is determined upon completion of TSESL 009 or by instructor permission, or successful completion of TSESL 010, 011, or 012.

title: Intro to American Ethnic Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to American Ethnic Studies. An interdisciplinary emphasis is placed on examining the knowledge traditions, cultural expressions, and resistance movements of Black, Indigenous, and people of color. By analyzing race, gender, sexuality, and other forms of social difference through their unique and shared contexts of meaning and power, students will draw connections across historical experiences to understand our contemporary moment and to uncover entryways for envisioning and enacting the future. Formerly ACES100
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Diversity and American Culture
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to the interdisciplinary study of diversity in American culture and society. An emphasis is placed on synthesizing and applying key concepts from literature, history, and sociology. Students will examine their own complex identities and experiences as they are constituted through sociohistorical contexts and relationships of power. By way of critical self-reflection and analysis of works by fiction writers, artists, activists, historians, and sociologists, students will explore the intersections of race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexuality, and ability in relation to lived experiences and larger social systems. Formerly ACES 101
prerequisites: No prerequisites, but recommended classes are: AEGS 100, ENGL& 101, or SOC& 101

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects) Formerly ACES 195
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Intro to Queer Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to Queer Studies. Students will examine how historical, political, and social forces shape gender and sexuality in relation to race and other forms of social difference. An emphasis is placed on the experiences and intellectual contributions of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, two spirit, intersex, asexual, and gender non-conforming communities (among others) for interrogating power. Topics may include queer theory, history, literature, politics, activism, and media and pop culture. Formerly AECS 102: The LGBTQ Experiences
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, AEGS 205, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Intro to African American Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to African American Studies. Students will examine how a revolutionary body of ideas emerged from African Americans and the broader African diaspora. An interrogation of ideas about “Negro,” “Black,” and “African American” identities and experiences will be explored in context. Emphasis is placed on the intellectual and creative contributions of a wide spectrum of leaders, scholars, artists, and activists. Topics may include perspectives from history, literature, politics, activism, and media and pop culture. Formerly ACES 201
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Intro to Latino Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to Latino Studies. An emphasis is placed on examining the cultural expressions, historical experiences, and contemporary social issues that gave rise to and continue to shape “Latina/o/x” identities and communities. We will also consider the political, economic, and historical linkages between the United States, Latin America, the Caribbean and other regions. Topics may include Latino and Latin American history, literature, art, politics, activism, and media and pop culture. Note: “Latinx” is used as a gender-neutral term for “Latinas” and “Latinos.” Formerly ACES 202
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Intro to Critical Indigenous Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to methods and research in Critical Indigenous Studies. Students will explore the worldviews, lived experiences, and theoretical standpoints generated within Indigenous communities. An emphasis is placed on Indigenous ways of being, knowing, and doing in constantly evolving contexts as they converge inside and outside of academia. Topics may include concepts and issues such as decolonization, sovereignty, cultural reclamation, environmental justice, tribalography, historiography, and intergenerational resistance to settler colonialism. Formerly ACES 203
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Intro to Asian American Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to the interdisciplinary field of Asian American studies. An emphasis is placed on examining the cultural expressions, historical experiences, and contemporary social issues that gave rise to and continue to shape Asian American identities and communities. Topics may include literature, history, politics, activism, pop culture, im/migration, citizenship, labor, gender, sexuality, coalition-building, war, imperialism, sovereignty, globalization, and post/colonialisms. Formerly ACES 204
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Intro to Women’s and Gender Studies
description: (H/DSJ) The Introduction to Women’s and Gender Studies investigates gender through interdisciplinary and intersectional lenses. We examine what gender is, how it differs from sex, sexuality, and gender identity, what sexism and heterosexism are, and how these concepts have material effects on U.S. and global societies. We consider how people of various genders, races, nationalities, and abilities have fought–and continue to fight–for gender equity and social justice. Topics may include critical gender theory, history, literature, politics, activism, and media and pop culture. Formerly HUMAN 220 and HUMAN 220/320
prerequisites: There are no prerequisites but recommendations would be: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL& 101 or SOC& 101

title: Intro to Oceania & Pacific Islander Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) This course is an introduction to Oceania and Pacific Islander Studies. An emphasis is placed on the people of the Pacific Islands across Polynesia, Micronesia, and Melanesia. We will examine the cultural expressions, historical experiences, and contemporary social issues that gave rise to and continue to shape Pacific Islander communities in the United States and the broader diaspora. We will also center Indigenous knowledge rooted in relationships to the land, to the seas, and to one another. Topics may include storytelling, tourism, colonialism, militarization, sovereignty, gender, sexuality, and resistance movements.
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but NOT required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL&101, or SOC& 101.

title: Introduction to Hip Hop Studies
description: (H/SS/DSJ) Hip Hop is a way of life. As a worldview and way of being, Hip Hop asks us to gain and create knowledge of our communities and our social, economic, and political contexts. In this course, we will explore the five elements of Hip Hop through African American, Native American, Asian American, Latino/x, and Pacific Islander histories and cultures. We will focus on each element of Hip Hop culture, including Rapping, DJing, Graffiti Art, Breakdancing, and Knowledge. Additional topics may include Hip Hop feminism, Homo Hop and LGBTQ+ representation, Krip Hop and disability justice, and Global Hip Hop Studies.
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but not required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL& 101, or SOC& 101.

title: Introduction to Transgender Studies
description: (H/SS) This course will explore the histories, identities, and lived experiences of transgender, non-binary, and gender diverse people. We will learn from diverse voices, including trans scholars, artists, and advocates, while analyzing and asking questions about how gender intersects with race, class, sexuality, disability, and more. What does it mean to be trans, non-binary, or gender diverse? How have trans communities shaped culture, politics, and society? How do different cultures and historical periods understand gender identity and expression? Additional topics may include media representation, healthcare access, legal rights, and activism.
prerequisites: Any of the following are recommended but not required: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, ENGL& 101, or SOC& 101.

title: Monsters & American Culture
description: (H/SS/DSJ) Monsters … and slashers … and ghosts! Oh, my! This course explores American culture and society through a history of monsters and horror in literary fiction, folklore, pop culture, and film. We will draw from ethnic, gender, and queer studies to examine monsters in context and to think about the ways in which they reveal broader power structures and cultural anxieties concerning race, class, gender, sexuality, ability, immigration, religion, and science. An eclectic, fun, and rigorous collection of monster and horror texts will be examined. Formerly ACES 110, ACES 210
prerequisites: No prerequisites, but recommended classes are: AEGS 100, AEGS 101, AEGS 205, ENGL& 101, or SOC& 101

title: Diversity & Social Justice in Leadership
description: (H) This course focuses on developing leadership visions and strategies for enacting commitments to diversity, equity, inclusion, and social justice. We will bridge scholarship in the field with wisdom from the experiences of Black, Indigenous, and people of color, including women, people with disabilities, and other communities who empower those around them. Emphasis is placed on the analysis of our values and sense of shared responsibility for building a more just society, as well as the application of tactics, tools, and evidence-based practices.
prerequisites: Admitted to OLTM Program.

title: Health Justice
description: (H/SS) ​​In this course, key frameworks and strategies for health equity will challenge us to think about the relationships between health and social justice. Is having access to healthy living a right or a privilege? How have stigmatization, criminalization, and other barriers impacted the health of marginalized groups? Such questions urge us to examine the forces leading to health disparities and to center health justice frameworks. We will draw from researchers and practitioners, and the wisdom, lived experiences, and artistic expressions of varying communities to develop culturally relevant approaches for achieving health equity and social justice. Formerly HUMAN 320 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101

title: Fingerspelling and Numbering
description: This course includes an introduction to historical an physiological aspects of fingerspelling and number use in American Sign Language (ASL). Specialized instruction will be given to develop and master their abilities of expressive spelling, reading fingerspelled words and numerical incorporation including focus areas involving: time signs, money, measurements, game scores, and other amounts. ,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: American Sign Language 1
description: (H) This course is an introduction to the basic skills in production and comprehension of American Sign Language (ASL). It includes the manual alphabet, numbers, and basic vocabularies along with the development of conversational ability, culturally appropriate behaviors, and it exposes students to ASL grammar. This course requires significant time learning this language inside and outside of class.

title: American Sign Language 2
description: (H) Continued study of ASL focused on expanding vocabulary and grammar to intermediate level with an emphasis on expressive and receptive skills. Further discussion of the deaf culture is also included. Prerequisite: ASL& 121 with 2.0 or better or permission of instructor. Students, who have taken ASL classes at their previous high schools/colleges in the past, need to be seen and evaluated by the ASL faculty to test their receptive and expressive signing skills to determine their skill level to be placed in the appropriate class.

title: American Sign Language 3
description: (H) Continued study of ASL focused on expanding vocabulary and grammar with emphasis on expressive and receptive skills. Further discussion of deaf culture. Prerequisite: ASL& 122 with 2.0 or better or permission of instructor. Students, who have taken ASL classes at their previous high schools/colleges in the past, need to be seen and evaluated by the ASL faculty to test their receptive and expressive signing skills to determine their skill level to be placed in the appropriate class.

title: American Sign Language 4
description: (H) First of the second-year sequence in studying ASL. Topics include enhancing receptive and expressive skills and fluency with comprehension and production skills emphasizing on complex grammar, short stories, narratives, and interactive use of the ASL. The student will continue a study in depth about the Deaf community and Deaf culture globally.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Environmental Anthropology
description: (SS/DSJ) Anthropological understanding of local and global environmental problems and sustainability. Human adaption to the environment. Globalization, ethnoecology, political ecology, environmental justice, history, theory, methods of Environmental Anthropology.

title: Archaeology Field School
description: Social Science Taught entirely in the field. Training given in archaeological field research methods and techniques, including survey, excavation, artifact analysis, report preparation, and museum curation of archaeological collections. ,

title: Death: A Comparative Perspective
description: Humanities/Social Science Course examines beliefs about death and afterlife, mourning rituals, mortuary practices, grief, medical/ethical issues, and images of death from diverse cultures. Includes the roles culture and society play in shaping the understanding, meaning, and experiences of death and dying, as well socio-cultural changes in beliefs and practices.

title: Culture/Health/Healing
description: Humanities/Social Science Introduces student to an anthropological perspective on disease, illness, and health. Examines cultural explanations of and responses to disease and illness, Different cultural approaches to treatment and curing, and factors that influences the distribution of disease, illness and wealth within and between cultures. Prerequisite: ANTH& 206 or ADN degree recommended.

title: Applied Anthropology
description: Social Science This course explores the practical application of anthropology’s methods and theories to address the needs of society, including the challenges and research needs of communities and organizations.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM program or BAS DF program or Instructor Permission.

title: Survey of Anthropology
description: (SS/DSJ) Survey of the subfields of archaeology, biological anthropology and linguistic and cultural anthropology; physical and cultural variation and change examined.

title: Archaeology
description: (SS/DSJ) The methods, techniques, principles, theories of archaeology used to uncover, examine, and interpret the past.

title: Biological Anthropology
description: (NS/SS/DSJ) Study of humans as biological organisms within the framework of culture. Attention is given to human variation and adaptation; genetics, primate studies, and fossil evidence for human evolution.

title: Cultural Anthropology
description: (SS/DSJ) Examination of cultural and social variation of humans. Comparisons of similarities and differences between human groups. Examples from diverse cultures around the world will be presented with an emphasis on the major elements of culture. Topics may include language and communication, belief systems, kinship, economic, social, and political systems, race and ethnicity, sex and gender, and sustainability.

title: Linguistic Anthropology
description: (H/SS/DSJ) The study of language in cultural and social contexts. This course offers tools and techniques to analyze media, everyday conversations, language diversity, and how language shapes our identities and perspectives of the world. The course offers insights into all aspects of language including the biological, psychological, social, cultural, and structural.

title: Indigenous Peoples of North America
description: (SS/DSJ) Explores Indigenous cultures of North America, their resilience and their diversity in social structures, languages, subsistence strategies, and value and belief systems in historical and contemporary contexts.

title: Art History/Ancient-Byzantine
description: (H) Major achievements in painting, sculpture, architecture, and the decorative arts in Europe, the Near East and North Africa from prehistoric times through the Byzantine Period.

title: Art History/Medieval-Renaissance
description: Humanities Major achievements in painting, sculpture, architecture, and the decorative arts in Europe, the Near East, and North Africa from Early Medieval through the 16th Century.

title: Art History/Baroque-Modern
description: Humanities Major achievements in painting, sculpture, architecture, and the decorative arts in Europe, The Americas, China, Japan, India and Africa from the Baroque Period to the present.

title: Drawing I
description: Humanities Drawing from still life and landscape, with an emphasis on observation, technique and design skills. ,

title: Drawing II
description:
Humanities Introduction to techniques in drawing the figure with emphasis on form, light, shadow, and Human anatomy Prerequisite: ART 106 .
,

title: Design I
description: Humanities Study of the relationship of form: the elements and the principle of art and organization as an understanding of two-dimensional art design. ,

title: Design II
description:
Humanities Introduction to digital image editing software using the elements and principles of art. Prerequisite: ART 110 .
,

title: Art History of Animation
description: (H) Art History of Animation introduces students to seminal works of animation across time and cultures. The course discusses the evolution of the art form through the lens of technical innovations, sociopolitical contexts, and aesthetic movements. Students will study works ranging from large productions to independent and experimental shorts, and the influence of different international productions on one another, including Asian and European works and creators. We will be learning about pivotal “players” in the animation field from the earliest days to the current 3D digital animation and AI generations.

title: Art History of the Northwest Coast
description: (Humanities/DSJ) Art History of the Northwest Coast introduces students to the Native Arts of the Northwest coastal region from Prehistory to the present.

title: Public Art Mural Painting
description: Humanities/Skills Performance Course will cover historical and cultural as well as the economic role of public murals. Students will work collaboratively in both the classroom and the field to create a large mural for an actual client during the quarter. Through coursework students will collaboratively experience design, presentation and construction of mural concept. ,

title: Ceramics I
description: Humanities/Skills Performance Study of clay as a material for art: emphasis on wheel throwing and hand building construction. Clay and glaze chemistry and glaze application introduced along with firing atmospheres of gas and electric. ,

title: Art for Teachers
description: Humanities Survey course that consider how children engage in the artistic process as a means of constructing their knowledge including children’s art and the development of the young child. Developmentally appropriate methods of planning and implementing creative activities in all areas of the ECE curriculum. Uses the elements of art and principles of design and how societal/cultural/historical factors give rise to art.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Drawing III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Continuation of ART 107 with further study of the figure and emphasis on techniques in drawing hands and portraiture. Prerequisite: ART 107 .
,

title: Design III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Introduction to digital illustration with emphasis on the elements and principles of art. Prerequisite: ART 111 OR DMA 120 with Grade of 0. 7 or better.
,

title: Ceramics II
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Continuation of ART 125 , with new emphasis on introduction of additional forms and construction methods. Introduction to Glaze calculation and mixing. Introduction to kiln firing. Prerequisite: ART 125 .
,

title: Ceramics III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Continuation of ART 225 , with further experimentation in porcelain, glazes and firing techniques. Prerequisite: ART 106 , ART 110 and ART 125 .
,

title: Watercolor I
description: Humanities/Skills Performance An introduction to the basic materials and techniques of watercolor painting. Emphasis will be on paint application, color theory and mixing, paper qualities, composition and stylistic possibilities of the medium. ,

title: Watercolor II
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Continuation of ART 230 , encouraging further development of personal imagery, technique and style. A variety of subject matter will be explored. Prerequisite: ART 230 .
,

title: Watercolor III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Emphasis on composition combining transparent and opaque watercolor, acrylic, pastels, ink, charcoal and collage. The development of painting within an historical and multicultural context through individual instruction. Prerequisite: ART 231 .
,

title: Painting I
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Introduction of fundamental techniques/ materials of acrylic painting. Emphasis on composition, color theory, and paint handling of image. Prerequisite: ART 106 strongly recommended.
,

title: Painting II
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Painting studio course in acrylic techniques which examines a variety of color, compositional and stylistic challenges encountered in personal artistic interpretation. Prerequisite: ART 240 .
,

title: Painting III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Students will continue development of their painting skills through the exploration of 20th Century color theory and practice, composition and materials. Prerequisite: ART 241 .
,

title: Sculpture I
description: Humanities/Skills Performance Introduction to materials. Consideration of form. Technical and compositional exercises in clay, plaster, wire, casting materials and found object materials. ,

title: Sculpture II
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Further practice in the fundamentals of additive, reductive, and constructive sculpture. Introduction to large forms, conceptually based artwork and alternative materials. A short presentation about a chosen artist will be required. Prerequisite: ART 266 .
,

title: Sculpture III
description:
Humanities/Skills Performance Intensive creative work in a variety of media including traditional and contemporary ideas and their relationship to personal expression. A presentation on Contemporary Art is required. Prerequisite: ART 267 .
,

title: Art Appreciation
description: Humanities Student finds personal meaning in visual arts, painting, sculpture, and architecture with emphasis on diversity of form, content, and comparative styles. ,

title: Introduction to Astronomy
description: Natural Science Modern concepts and theories from the science of astronomy; motions of night-time sky, history of astronomy, light and telescopes, solar system, stars, and galaxies. Particular emphasis on composition of our solar system. Prerequisite: MATH 094 (Elementary Algebra) or equivalent.

title: Stellar Astronomy
description: Natural Science Properties of stars, stellar evolution, the Milky Way and other galaxies, quasars, cosmology. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.

title: Life in the Universe-Astrobiology
description: Natural Science Introduction to Astrobiology, the study of the origin and evolution of life on Earth, and the search for microbial and intelligent life elsewhere in the Universe. Prerequisite: MATH 094 (Elementary Algebra) or equivalent.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Nursing Informatics
description: Analyzes information systems (IS) as they relate to clinical management, education, and research.
prerequisites: Enrolled in an ADN, TADN or BSN program or have instructor permission.

title: Advanced Clinical Reasoning
description: Examine clinical nursing phenomena and therapies from the perspective of Human physiologic response, including pathophysiologic, experiential and behavioral events. Includes life span and sociocultural factors.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the BSN program (RN-BSN or VL-BSN pathway)

title: Professional Writing for Nurses
description: The course focuses on the process of analytical reasoning in academic, scholarly, and professional writing for nurses. The course also introduces foundational concepts in the identification and analysis of supportive evidence relevant to nursing practice.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the BSN pathway.

title: Family & Genomics in Population Health Theory
description: This course explores the fundamental influence of genomics, social determinants of health, and concepts of family across the lifespan in applying population health nursing practice in the promotion and preservation of population health. Formerly BNURS 402 - Family/Population Health & Genomics Theory
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in RN-BSN or VL-BSN pathways.

title: Connecting Research to Nursing
description: Introduction to research methodologies and utilizing health care research to support evidence-based nursing practice.
prerequisites: Must be part of an approved BSN, ADN, TADN plan or student group to register for this class.

title: Perspectives on Diversity
description: This writing intensive course focuses on examining the human dignity, inherent worth, and uniqueness of individuals, families, groups, and populations. Ways that difference is defined, used, and experienced in society are explored.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the BSN program (RN-BSN or VL-BSN) or permission of the instructor

title: Family & Genomics in Population Health Fieldwork
description: Application of population health nursing theory in the implementation and evaluation of an interventional project. The course focuses on improving the health status of underserved and/or marginalized populations that include family and genomic influences on population health. Formerly BNURS 408 - Family/Population Health & Genomics Fieldwork
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in RN-BSN or VLBSN pathways.

title: Population Health Nursing Theory
description: Introduces foundational concepts, principles, and strategies in identifying, analyzing, and addressing factors relevant to improving population health. Emphasis is placed on collaboration with communities and other stakeholders to develop a plan of action with multicultural and diversity considerations in elevating the health status of underserved and marginalized populations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the BSN program (RN-BSN or VL-BSN pathway)

title: Contemporary Ethics in Nursing
description: Apply ethical theories and identify the influence of cultural, societal, professional and other sources of values on ethical decision making in nursing.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in BSN/VLBSN pathway

title: Population Health Nursing Application
description: Application of foundational concepts, principles, and strategies in identifying, analyzing, and addressing factors relevant to improving the health status of underserved and/or marginalized populations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in BSN program (RN-BSN or VL-BSN pathway)

title: Nursing Leadership in Health Systems
description: Basic organizational and system leadership for quality care and patient safety. Integration of Institute for Healthcare Improvement Standards. Prepares RN to lead change.
prerequisites: Admission to the Baccalaureate Nursing program or permission of the instructor.

title: Interactive Nursing Communication
description: Explores communication concepts related to populations, interdisciplinary teams, families, client education, media, and culture across the lifespan using motivational interviewing. Explores information systems (IS) related to clinical management, education, and research.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the BSN program (RN-BSN or VLBSN pathway).

title: Professional Development Seminar I
description: Transition to baccalaureate-prepared nursing with an introduction to the use of a portfolio as a measure of academic and professional progress and achievement.
prerequisites: Accepted enrollment in the BSN pathway or permission of the instructor.

title: Professional Development Seminar II
description: Evaluation to reflect personal growth and achievement of RN to BSN program outcomes. Prerequisite: Completion of all upper division general education and all BNURS courses prior to last quarter of study. Concurrent enrollment in BNURS 409 and BNURS 411 . Completion of BNURS 409 and BNURS 411 in spring quarter is required for one year program students.

title: Foundational Skills Theory - Online Module
description: This course provides students with a review of the foundational knowledge required for safe and effective nursing practice emphasizing clinical judgment, professional communications, and cultural competence in the application of nursing skills in diverse healthcare settings.
prerequisites: Acceptance and enrollment into the VLBSN pathway of the BSN program

title: Foundational Skills Lab
description: This course focuses on student application of theoretical concepts involved with the performance of safe and effective nursing skills in diverse healthcare settings.
prerequisites: Acceptance and enrollment in the VLBSN pathway of the BSN program

title: Dosages & Pharmacology Module
description: Examines the basics of pharmacology, including mathematics used in medication administration in the clinical setting.
prerequisites: Admission to the VL-BSN program

title: Foundations of Professional Nursing
description: Introduction of licensed practical nurses and veteran medics/corpsmen to the role of the RN, focusing on patient-centered care across the lifespan and incorporating social determinants of health.
prerequisites: Admission to the VL-BSN program

title: Nursing Therapeutics Module
description: Introduces concepts for promoting healthy physiologic responses in clients. A nursing process framework will be utilized to foster critical thinking in the nursing role. Same As NURSE 158
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program; Co-requisite: VL-BSN 142

title: Mental Health Theory
description: Prepares students to care for clients who, as a result of a mental illness, have alterations in mood, personal identity, and coping. Same As NURSE 172
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Mental Health Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of mental health clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance. Same As NURSE 174
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program; Co-requisite: VL-BSN 172

title: Nursing Care of Pediatric Clients
description: Prepares students to care for pediatric clients, focusing on the promotion and maintenance of family health related to the physical, psychosocial, and emotional development of children. Same As NURSE 176
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Nursing Care of Pediatric Clients Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of pediatric clients, focusing on the promotion and maintenance of family health. Same As NURSE 177
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program; Co-requisite: VL-BSN 176

title: Reproductive and Sexual Health
description: Explores physiologic, psychosocial, and pathophysiologic aspects of sexual and reproductive health across the lifespan.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Reproductive and Sexual Health Clinical
description: DSJ Application of knowledge of physiologic, psychosocial, and pathophysiologic aspects of sexual and reproductive health across the lifespan. Same As NURSE 179
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program, Co-requisite: VL-BSN 178

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing I - Theory
description: Prepares students to care for adult medical-surgical clients across the continuum of healthcare environments. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care. Covers common geriatric conditions. Same As NURSE 180
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing I - Clinical
description: (DSJ) Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of medical-surgical clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance. Same As NURSE 181
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program; Co-requisite: VL-BSN 180

title: Clinical Applications Lab III
description: Prepares students to perform advanced nursing care procedures based on critical analysis of patient needs in the management of clients with diverse therapies involving high-risk treatment modalities and equipment. A nursing process framework is utilized. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to apply critical thinking and theoretical content into practice. Same As NURSE 202
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in VLBSN pathway.

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing Theory II
description: Links pathophysiological changes related to disease entities and client care needed to facilitate positive adaptation in the client’s response. The course emphasizes a review of anatomy and physiology, pathophysiology, and management of acute and chronic conditions related to neurology, hematology, nephrology/urology, oncology, and shock. Same As NURSE 208
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing Clinical II
description: Integration of previous learning and application of theoretical concepts to clinical practice with an emphasis on critical thinking and the nursing process.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program; Co-requisite: VL-BSN 208

title: Case Management & Leadership Seminar
description: This course prepares students to incorporate case management concepts including intentional interprofessional collaboration and utilization of services and resources to optimize care outcomes.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in VL-BSN pathway

title: Case Management & Leadership Fieldwork
description: Clinical application of nursing leadership and management in complex healthcare environments to promote quality patient outcomes.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in VL-BSN pathway

title: Professional Development Seminar
description: Focused on group collaboration and topics to aid in the transition from LPN, medic, or hospital corpsman to the Bachelor-prepared RN role, reflecting on personal growth and demonstrating achievement of program outcomes.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN program

title: Professional Role Preceptorship
description: Utilizing a preceptorship program, this course prepares students to manage care for clients in the role of a professional nurse in a variety of direct patient care clinical experiences.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the VL-BSN Pathway.

title: Introduction to Marine Science
description: Natural Science Origin and extent of the ocean, its biological, chemical, geological, and physical aspects. Interactions of plants and animals in the sea and their use by Humans, includes field trips. ,

title: Plant Biology
description: Natural Science Basic content on plants, emphasizing diversity of structures, functions, economic importance, and function of plants in vegetation systems and Human communities.

title: Natural History of the Pacific NW
description: Natural Science Field, lecture, and laboratory course emphasizing the identification, habits, habitats, adaptations, and interrelationships of plants and animals that constitute the biomes of the Pacific Northwest. ,

title: Freshwater Biology
description: Natural Science Local freshwater organisms and basic biological, physical, and chemical factors of the inland water environment. Field trips to ponds, lakes, streams, and estuaries in the immediate area. ,

title: Local Flora
description: Natural Science An introduction to the native flowering plants of Western Washington. Emphasis on the use of taxonomic keys to identify the local flowering plants. For students majoring in forestry, game management, botany, horticulture, ecology, and those interested in learning more about their natural surroundings. ,

title: Ecology of the Northwest I
description: Natural Science The course applies basic ecological principles to fresh water ecosystems for the purpose of understanding how to best manage these systems for biological diversity and Human use. Laboratory includes extensive field work.
prerequisites: One year of Biology.

title: Ecology of the Northwest II
description: Natural Science The course applies basic ecological principles to the marine, fresh water and forest ecosystems for the purpose of understanding how to best manage these systems for biological diversity and Human use.
prerequisites: One year of Biology.

title: Ecology of the Northwest III
description: Natural Science The course applies basic ecological principles to the marine and fresh water ecosystems for the purpose of understanding how to best manage these systems for biological diversity and Human use. Laboratory includes extensive field work.
prerequisites: One year of Biology.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Medical Genetics
description: Seminar-style and online course examining various genetic conditions and their relationship to disease in the population. Discussion will be oriented toward healthcare professionals.
prerequisites: Ten credits of biological science or permission of instructor.

title: Pathophysiology
description: Natural Science A concise overview of the disordered physiological processes associated with disease or injury of the adult Human body. Prerequisite: BIOL& 242 grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Survey of Biology (with Lab)
description: (NS) Lab science course for non-Biology majors. An introduction to principles of biology and their practical applications. Major concepts include energy, cell structure and function, genetics and evolution, diversity of animals and plants, and interrelationships among living organisms, and ecology.
prerequisites: None

title: General Biology w/Lab: Cell Biology
description: Natural Science An introductory cell biology course for students preparing for health professions and non-Biology majors. Major concepts of cell biology will be introduced, including the chemistry, the structure, function, reproduction, and metabolism of cells, genetics.
prerequisites: Recent high school chemistry or introductory-level college chemistry is recommended before taking this course.

title: Human Biology w/Lab
description: Natural Science The structure and function of major systems and current health issues of the Human body. Includes gross anatomy and histology. Recommend for pre-professional programs. ,

title: Majors Cellular
description: Natural Science A general overview of life at the cellular and molecular level, including biochemical and cellular structures, cell division, metabolism, genetics, advances in DNA technology. First in a three-course sequence for science majors and pre-professionals . Formerly BIOL 201
prerequisites: None, however, to satisfy the prerequisite for upper division biology courses at some institutions, a year of general chemistry must be completed.

title: Majors Animal
description:
Natural Science A continuation of BIOL& 211 emphasizing evolution and zoology. For majors, prepares students for advanced biology courses and preprofessional programs. Prerequisite: BIOL& 211 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Majors Plant
description:
Natural Science A continuation of BIOL& 211 and BIOL& 212 emphasizing reproduction, growth, and homeostasis in plants as well as the major topics of ecology, e. g. , populations, communities, and ecosystems. For majors; prepares students for advanced biology courses and pre-professional programs. Prerequisite: BIOL& 211 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology 1
description: Natural Science This is a two-quarter course in the study of structure (anatomy) and function (physiology) of the human body. The course conforms to guidelines established by the Human Anatomy and Physiology Society. Both quarters are required for a complete survey of the human body and to ensure transferability.
prerequisites: BIOL& 160 or BIOL& 211

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology 2
description: Natural Science A continuation of BIOL& 241 with emphasis on blood, immunity, respiration, urinary function, digestion, and reproduction. Lab includes dissections and structure identification. Prerequisite: BIOL& 241 ,

title: Microbiology
description: Natural Science The structure, function, metabolism, genetics, control and cultivation of microorganisms, and their role in immunity and disease. For pre-professionals. Prerequisite: BIOL& 160 or BIOL& 211 and either CHEM& 121 or CHEM& 141 ,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Business Statistics
description: Natural Science Application of statistics in the fields of economics and business; descriptive statistics, inferential statistics, linear correlation and regression, probability, sampling, the Normal Distribution, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Business Finance
description: This course broadens the non-finance manager/student’s understanding of financial concepts and tools needed in financial management and decision making. Students will gain knowledge of finance terms, including time value of money, risk and return, securities valuation, risk management, and capital budgeting. Using basic accounting principles, students will be able to extrapolate data from financial statements for the purpose of financial analysis, goal setting, and budgeting.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the OLTM BAS program or permission of instructor.

title: Intro to Business
description: Social Science Business operations: management, marketing, finance, and Human resources. Business environments: global, economic, social, ethical, and political.

title: Business Law
description: Origin and development of business law, the legal system, and enforcement of individual legal rights; law of torts, crimes, and business contracts.

title: Introduction to Financial Planning
description: Explores money management, and wealth creation strategies to include a personal financial plan. Stocks, Bonds, Mutual Funds, Real Estate, Money Markets, Insurance needs, transportation options, and retirement planning. Consumer Credit, Predatory Lending, Identity Theft are also covered.

title: Business Management Capstone
description: A practical application course directing students in the development of an updated Resume, Cover Letter and Skill-Based Career Portfolio demonstrating individual competency in Business Management Program Outcomes. This course should be completed in the spring of a student’s final ATA year.
prerequisites: By permission of Instructor.

title: Introduction to International Business
description: This International Business course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the global business environment and the complexities of conducting business on an international scale. The course encompasses a broad range of topics, offering insights into the economic, cultural, legal, and managerial aspects of international business . By the end of the course, students will have a holistic understanding of international business concepts, enabling them to make informed decisions and contribute effectively in a globalized business environment. Formerly BMGMT 102

title: Business and Personal Mathematics
description: Solving practical business and personal financial situations in an applied context including the development, critique, and use of various financial models. Concepts covered include Banking, Credit, Loans, Wages, Taxes, Budgeting, Investments, Insurance, the Metric System, PV/FV Value, Discounting, Cost, and Markups. Formerly BMGMT 140 Prerequisite: MATH 091 with a grade of 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Improving Human Effectiveness
description: Effective leaders and managers are clear on their strengths and how they can be used in personal/professional settings. Explores how strengths can create a fulfilling career and life. Formerly OLRM 150

title: Introduction to Human Resources
description: This course examines policies and practices used in managing Human Resources to build and maintain an effective workforce. Topics include planning, recruitment, retention, and the HR legal environment. Formerly BMGMT 155

title: Marketing
description: Marketing has changed from one-way messaging to a conversation between companies and their customers. This course not only explores consumer buying behavior, target markets and trends, but it will assist you in better understanding your customer, so you know how to motivate them to engage with your business. In this course you will learn a variety or marketing strategies and tactics to establish and maintain long-term relationships with your current and future customers. Completing ENGL& 101 prior to taking this course is highly recommended due to the amount of research and writing that will be required during the course. Formerly BMGMT 180 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 recommended.

title: E-Business Strategies
description: E-Business Strategies is designed to equip students with the knowledge and skills needed to navigate the dynamic world of electronic business. The course focuses on the strategic use of digital technologies to enhance business operations, improve competitiveness, and capitalize on opportunities in the online marketplace.
