Students will explore key concepts and strategies related to electronic business, covering topics such as e-commerce, digital marketing, online presence, and technology-enabled business models. The course emphasizes practical applications and strategic decision-making in the context of the rapidly evolving digital landscape. Formerly BMGMT 185
prerequisites: A Marketing course is highly recommended.

title: Intro to Leadership & Business Management
description: Introduction to leadership and management within organizations. An exploration of the history of leadership & management theories, case studies, and understanding the relationship between management, leadership, motivation, and power. Formerly OLRM 201 and BMGMT 282

title: Introduction to Organizational Ethics
description: Introduction to organizational ethics, understanding the correlation between leadership/ management practice and the reflectiveness of moral philosophy, applying ethical decision making to ethical dilemmas. Formerly OLRM 202

title: Small Business Planning & Management
description: The Small Business Planning and Management course is designed to provide students with the essential knowledge and skills necessary for successfully launching and managing a small business. This course can assist in determining the feasibility of a small business venture, identifying start-up costs, comparing legal forms of ownership, and integrating a marketing strategy to meet business goals.
prerequisites: Completion of ENGL, ACCT and Marketing coursework recommended.

title: Systems Thinking for Leaders
description: Exploring how leaders impact and/or change organizations by understanding the patterns and relationships at work within their organizations. Formerly OLRM 218 Prerequisite: LBM 201 .

title: Human Relations in Organizations
description: (DSJ) This course is a “total person” approach to human relations. The focus is on the seven major themes of effective human relations: Communication, Self-Awareness, Self-Acceptance, Motivation, Trust, Self-Disclosure, and Conflict Resolution. An emphasis is placed on contributing to an inclusive workplace. Formerly OLRM 225

title: Conflict Resolution
description: Introduces students to the fundamentals of conflict resolution. Provides the knowledge and skills necessary to manage conflict, encourage cooperation, and create resolutions. Students will apply conflict identification, prevention, and resolution strategies to case studies in conflict resolution. Through reading, writing, and class participation; students will gain theoretical and practical knowledge for navigating personal and professional conflict. Formerly OLRM 260

title: Capstone Project
description: A practical application of theories, practices and concepts studied within the LBM program. This practical application can come through an internship, immersion program, a project within the workplace or other methods of experiential learning as agreed upon by student and instructor. Formerly BMGMT 299 and OLRM 299 Prerequisite: Successful completion of LBM 102 , LBM 202 , LBM 225 , and LBM 260 , or instructor permission.

title: Adaptive Keyboarding-One-Handed
description: Students will learn and develop skill in alphanumeric keyboarding and 20-key data entry using a one-handed keyboard.

title: Screen Magnification
description: Students will acquire the skills and knowledge to access and manipulate text using screen magnification.

title: Braille Translation and Printing
description: Comprehensive introduction to translating an ink-print document into Braille using a Braille translation program and printing in Braille. Prerequisite: BSTEC 104 .

title: Screen Reader Software Level 1
description: Introduction to the basics of voice-output software in a Windows environment. Instruction and use of basic keyboard commands to access and hear text voiced on the computer screen. JAWS or other screen reader technology.
prerequisites: Keyboarding skills.

title: Screen Reader Software Level 2
description: Instruction on producing, reading, and manipulating a word processing document using PC curser commands to access menu bars and icons using JAWS or other screen reader technology. Prerequisite: BSTEC 104 .

title: Screen Reader Software Level 3
description: The use of voice output to access and read graphic-based, mouse-driven environments, such as Windows desktop and web pages. Prerequisite: BSTEC 105 .

title: Voice Recognition Level 1
description: Introduction to DragonDictate. Use basic voice-activated input commands to build voice files, dictate a simple written document, and correct errors.

title: Voice Recognition Level 2
description: Learn to format and manipulate a document using intermediate voice-activated commands. Prerequisite: BSTEC 107 .

title: Doc. Processing with Speech Tec.
description: Basic Document Processing with speech technologies: Dragon, Windows Speech, etc. Substitutes for BSTEC 111 , when BSTEC speed requirement not met.
prerequisites: Computer competency recommended.

title: Beginning Keyboarding
description: Learn and develop skill in alphanumeric keyboarding, 10-key data entry, basic computer functions, and basic document formatting. ,

title: Intermediate Keyboarding
description:
Improve speed and accuracy of alphabetical and numerical data entry including business document formatting and 10-key pad skills using the touch system. Prerequisite: BSTEC 110 or equivalent.
,

title: Internet Basics
description: Introduction to tools and strategies to communicate, explore, and retrieve information using the Internet resources. Some computer skills required. Text required.

title: MS Outlook
description: Introduction to MS Outlook as an information manager. E-mail, files, contact lists, journal and calendar. Basic computer skills needed. Text required.

title: Electronic Communication
description: Write effective E-mail, use instant messaging, understand confidentiality and legal aspects, and use professional English to write, edit, and proofread before hitting send.

title: MS Word
description: Introduction to word processing with Microsoft Word for simple applications. Hands-on training. Textbook required. (Pass/No Credit or graded option)

title: MS Excel
description: Introduction to spreadsheets using Microsoft Excel for simple applications. Hands-on training. Textbook required. (Pass/No Credit or graded option)

title: MS PowerPoint
description: Understanding presentation software using Microsoft PowerPoint for simple applications. Hands-on training. Textbook required. (Pass/No Credit or grade)

title: MS Access
description: Microsoft Access database system, file structures and practical applications in the Windows environment. Computer skills suggested. Text required.

title: MS Transitions
description: Transition Microsoft Office skills using illustrated approach to most significant changes in terminology, features, and platform (Word, Excel, Access and PowerPoint).

title: MS Word Specialist
description:
Specialist approach to MS Word. Topics: formatting, editing, tables, columns, mail merge, graphics, Web pages. Use Word in business and help prep for the MOS Cert. test. Prerequisite: CIS 150 and keyboarding by touch, or permission of instructor.
,

title: MS Excel Specialist
description:
Specialist approach to MS Excel: formulas, logical functions, charts, hyperlinks, graphics, formatting, and managing data. Use Excel in business and help prep for the MOS test. Prerequisite: CIS 150 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Intro to MS Office PowerPoint
description: Use MS Office PowerPoint to create and edit a presentation, augment with design, graphics, audio/video, and other formatting, and enhance slideshow techniques. ,

title: Integration of Software Applications
description: Reinforce understanding and proficiency with MS Office, completing tasks in Word, Excel, Access and PowerPoint, and integrating between these applications. Prerequisite: CIS 150 or permission of instructor.

title: Practical Accounting
description: Introductory accounting course that includes accounting theory and practice as they apply to small business and service business situations.

title: Electronic Printing Calculators
description: Control and operation of electronic printing and display calculators. Emphasis on attaining minimum Office proficiency. ,

title: Computerized Accounting
description:
Hands-on, realistic approach to computerized, integrated accounting for students who have a fundamental knowledge of accounting practices and principles. Prerequisite: BSTEC 130 or ACCT& 201 , or permission of instructor.
,

title: Payroll Accounting
description: Designed to provide information and study regarding the benefits, taxes, payroll deductions, and employment accounting records incidental to the social security and tax program.

title: Accounting Simulation/Service Business
description: Simulated accounting application involving the accounting cycle for a service business. Prerequisite: BSTEC 130 or ACCT& 201 .

title: Accounting Simulation/Merchandising Business
description: Simulated accounting application involving the accounting cycle for a merchandising business. Prerequisite: BSTEC 130 or ACCT& 201 .

title: Accounting Simulation/Corporation
description: Simulated accounting application involving the accounting cycle for a corporation. Prerequisite: ACCT& 202 .

title: Payroll Simulation
description: Simulate the payroll accounting process using computer software to apply various workplace scenarios, including converting manual procedures to automated systems. Prerequisite: BSTEC 134 .

title: QuickBooks
description:
A hands-on, realistic approach to small business accounting using specialized QuickBooks accounting software and integration with Microsoft Word and Excel. Prerequisite: BSTEC 130 or ACCT& 201 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Business Writing/Grammar for the Workplace
description: A workplace-centered approach to improving writing skills by reviewing grammar, language usage, and punctuation, and using effective composition to write and revise basic workplace/business documents, including memos, letters, and reports. Prerequisite: Appropriate placement score to enter ENGL 098 ; or 2. 5 grade or higher in ENGL 091 ; or instructor permission.

title: Business English
description: A business-centered approach to improving writing skills by reviewing grammar, language usage, structure, English mechanics, editing, proofreading, and spelling. Prerequisite: Assessment test at college level reading and writing or ENGL 099 OR BSTEC 145.

title: MS Access Specialist
description: Specialist approach to MS Access. Design and development of databases. The creation and use of tables, various types of queries, forms and macros. Formerly CIS 154
prerequisites: Skill with using Windows and Files is recommended.

title: Communication Skills for Customer Service
description: Students will demonstrate communication competency while maintaining quality customer service. Students will develop technology skills and use research tools common to a customer service environment to locate and provide appropriate information. Emphasis on digital/electronic communications, including telephone skills, Internet research, writing effective email and understanding confidentiality and legal aspects of customer service. Formerly BSTEC 155 - Customer Service in the Information Age

title: General Office Procedures
description:
Introduction to the Office environment, receptionist duties, equipment and supply control, bank services, payroll procedures, mail and resume/job hunting skills. Prerequisite: CIS 150 and BSTEC 110 or equivalent skills with permission of instructor.
,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Business Computer Applications
description: Strategic use of software applications to support business activity with emphasis on tasks in business communications, accounting, business decision-making, and information management. Create professional documents, build effective business presentations, use problem-solving spreadsheet models and utilize database content to inform business decisions. Determine the appropriate software and integrate content to produce effective business projects.
prerequisites: Computer user familiar with the keyboard, browsing the internet, and common business software such as MS Office. Not a beginner level computer course. (Skills can be gained through personal use or formal coursework. ) This course meets a prerequisite for transfer to WWU’s bachelor in business program.

title: Individual Taxation
description: Study of Federal Income taxation to develop basic understanding of tax preparation requirements for individuals.

title: Practical Fund Accounting
description: Accounting and reporting concepts, standards and procedures applicable to state and local governments, the federal government, and not-for-profit institutions. Prerequisite: ACCT& 201 and ACCT& 202 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Taxation for Business
description: Study of Federal Income taxation and Washington State business taxation and its application to business entities. Prerequisite: BSTEC 130 or ACCT& 201 .

title: Taxation Simulations
description: Simulate tax filings for both individual and business entities. Demonstrate knowledge of tax laws and required forms. Prerequisite: BSTEC 229 and BSTEC 239 .

title: Business Correspondence
description: Effective composition for business letters, memos, and reports. Includes writing style, tone, grammar, punctuation, and vocabulary. Prerequisite: BSTEC 150 , or permission of instructor; keyboarding ability.

title: Document Formatting
description: Develop industry-standard formatting and production skills with MS Word software by keying and formatting various forms of standard industry documents.
prerequisites: BSTEC 111, 123, proof of 30 NWAM keyboarding requirement, or instructor permission

title: Records and Database Management
description: A study of the principles and practices of records storage and retrieval using manual and automated database systems; includes ARMA rules and introduction to Access. Prerequisite: CIS 150 , keyboarding proficiency at 25 WAM, or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Office Applications
description:
Integration of business-standard software skills focusing on MS Office Suite. Emphasis is on problem solving, collaboration, and independent thinking. Prerequisite: BSTEC 123 , BSTEC 124 , and BSTEC 255 or BSTEC 154 , keyboarding proficiency at 45 NWAM or permission of instructor.
,

title: Administrative Office Management
description: Designed for BSTEC or BMGMT students as capstone class, or for currently employed Office personnel desiring to expand their knowledge of administrative Office management. Prerequisite: BSTEC 160 or one year general Office support work experience.

title: Chemistry of the Environment
description:
Natural Science The basic principles of chemistry emphasizing how they apply to the Earth, its major components, and its ecosystems. Prerequisite: Completion of MATH 094 with a 2.0 or permission of the instructor.
,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Chemical Concepts w/Lab
description:
Natural Science Introduces chemical principles in nonmathematical format intended for the liberal arts student. Topics include food, energy, household chemicals, and drugs. Prerequisite: MATH 094 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Intro to Chemistry
description:
Natural Science Atomic structure, chemical bonding, quantitative chemical relationships, solutions, acids, bases, salts, buffers. An introduction to organic chemistry may be included. Primarily for ADN and Allied Health students. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 with a 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.
,

title: Intro to Organic/Biochemistry
description:
Natural Science Organic compounds including nomenclature and reactions of: hydrocarbons, alcohols, aldehydes and ketones, carboxylic acids, esters, amines. Biochemistry of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins and enzymes, nucleic acids, metabolism. Prerequisite: Completion of CHEM& 121 with a 2.0 or better.
,

title: General Chemistry Prep
description: Natural Science Prepares students for CHEM& 141 by introducing problem-solving techniques, the metric system, measurements, atomic structure, stoichiometry, solution chemistry, bonding, and molecular shape. NOT transferable for credit for science or engineering students. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: General Chemistry I
description: Natural Science Principles of chemistry including stoichiometry, enthalpy, atomic theory, gasses, periodicity, chemical bonding. Prerequisite: CHEM& 139 or CHEM& 121 with a 2.0 or above or successful completion of chemistry exam, and MATH 099 or MATHI 099 with a 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: General Chemistry II
description: Natural Science Principles of chemistry including organic chemistry, gasses, solid and liquid states, solutions, kinetics, equilibrium, thermodynamics, acids and bases. Prerequisite: CHEM& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: General Chemistry III
description: Natural Science Principles of chemistry relating to acid/base equilibrium systems, heterogeneous equilibrium systems, transition metal chemistry, electrochemistry and nuclear chemistry. Prerequisite: CHEM& 142 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: General Chemistry Lab I
description: Natural Science Principles of chemistry including organic chemistry, gases, solid and liquid states, solutions, kinetics, equilibrium, thermodynamics, acids and bases. Prerequisite: CHEM& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or higher or concurrent enrollment in CHEM& 141 .

title: General Chemistry Lab II
description: Natural Science Experiments illustrating general principles and quantitative relationships in chemistry. Prerequisite: CHEM& 151 , CHEM& 142 with a 2.0 or higher or concurrent enrollment in CHEM& 142 .

title: General Chemistry Lab III
description: Natural Science Volumetric and Gravimetric experiments in quantitative analysis using computer acquisition and treatment of data. Qualitative analysis of solutions containing selected metallic ions and polyatomic anions using wet chemical methods as well as a computer simulation. Prerequisite: CHEM& 152 , CHEM& 143 with a grade of 2.0 or higher or concurrent enrollment in CHEM& 143 .

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: Natural Science An introduction to the alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes. Includes discussions of structure, including stereochemistry, chemical and physical properties, and substitution/elimination reactions. Prerequisite: CHEM& 142 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: Natural Science Introduction to NMR, mass spectroscopy and free radical mechanisms. The structure, synthesis and reactions of alcohols, ethers, conjugated unsaturated systems, aromatics, aldehydes, and ketones. Prerequisite: CHEM& 241 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Organic Chemistry III
description: Natural Science Structure, nomenclature, synthesis and reactions of aldehydes and ketones, carboxylic acids and derivatives, B-dicarbonyl compounds, amines, aryl halides, carbohydrates, lipids, and amino acids/proteins. Prerequisite: CHEM& 242 .

title: Organic Chemistry Lab I
description: Natural Science Organic chemistry lab emphasizes mastery of techniques such as sample handling, filtration, measuring physical constants, recrystallization, extraction, GC, polarimetry, and refractometry. Prerequisite: CHEM& 241 or concurrent enrollment.

title: Organic Chemistry Lab II
description: Natural Science Organic chemistry lab emphasizes techniques such as simple, fractional, steam, and reduced pressure distillation; thin-layer, column, high-pressure liquid chromatography, and IR/ NMR spectroscopy. Prerequisite: CHEM& 251 , or CHEM& 242 or concurrent enrollment in CHEM& 251 .

title: Organic Chemistry Lab III
description: Natural Science Includes organic qualitative analysis, an oral presentation on a journal article, and an independent synthesis project. Prerequisite: CHEM& 252 , CHEM& 243 , or concurrent enrollment in CHEM& 243 .

title: Orientation to College
description: The orientation course aims to provide students with essential knowledge and skills to transition and navigate their college experience successfully. Students will become familiar with college terminology, identify and utilize campus resources, strategies to foster a sense of belonging, and explore the concept of diversity and inclusion in college. Formerly GEN-S 101
prerequisites: None.

title: College Success
description: Introduction to the Olympic College community, resources, and skills necessary for student success. Topics include goal setting, college resources, personal empowerment, career and academic planning, time management, study skills, and financial literacy. Formerly GEN-S 110
prerequisites: None.

title: Success for Student Cohorts
description: Intensive seminar to help prepare student cohorts for success at Olympic College and beyond, including identifying expectations in higher education, improving academic skills and self-awareness, and defining educational and career goals. Formerly GEN-S 121

title: Student Success Skills
description: Support in the learning and application of self-assessment and study skills for students new to higher education. Formerly GEN-S 131

title: Running Start and Beyond
description: This course provides Running Start students with the tools and strategies to navigate their educational and career pathways. Students will reflect on their personal interests, values, strengths, and goals. The course emphasizes developing individualized plans for college and career success. Students will research workplace and academic requirements, job qualifications, and financial aid opportunities. Formerly GEN-S 133

title: Career and Transfer Planning
description: Students will create an individualized degree plan, establish or change career goals, learn the college transfer process, develop essential job seeking and career development skills, research admission/major requirements, and identify resources for college adjustment issues. Formerly GEN-S 141

title: Photojournalism
description: Humanities The basics of digital photojournalism with special attention to news value and composition. Formerly JOURN 105

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Intro to Communication in Organizations
description: Humanities The purpose of this course is to help students understand communication theory and practice in organizations and how to take effective action in their organizations. Topics addressed in the course include organizational culture, organizational climate, diversity, and leadership in the context of organizational communication.

title: Intro to Popular Communication
description: (H/DSJ) The goal of popular communication studies is to investigate how popular culture artifacts reflect a lived cultural experience, and how lived experience is influenced by popular culture. The study of popular communication is rooted in studies of folklore, rhetoric, storytelling, media, and the culture industry. We study popular culture to understand how it reflects and is reflected by the values, ideologies, concerns, and public issues of the culture responsible for its production. Popular communication serves as both a tool for dividing people along ideological and cultural lines, and for unifying people across cultures. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Political Communication
description: Humanities/Social Science Exploration of political communication in historical and contemporary settings. Investigating political cultures as constructed through public rhetorics, traditional media, and digital communication practices. This course looks at the history and impact of political communication in local and global contexts. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 .

title: Sex and Gender in Communication
description: (H/DSJ) The purpose of this course is to help students understand and engage in dialogue, performance, and theory regarding sex and gender in communication. Sex and Gender in Communication centers on the role of gender and sexuality as communication practice in our everyday lives. This course is designed to increase students’ awareness of and sensitivity to other gender stereotypes, gendered biases, gendered communication practices, and the social, political, cultural, and historical contexts in which they live and communicate. This course will teach students to communicate effectively between and across multiple gender and sexuality divides as we reach out to diverse individuals and diverse audiences. Included are an introduction to major gender communication theories and principles, feminist and queer theory approaches to communication studies, and the application of these principles in local and global contexts. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Digital Cultures
description: Humanities Exploration of contemporary cultures as constructed through new media and digital communication practices. This course looks at the history and impact of creative digital communication in local and global contexts. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Ethical and Legal Principles of Media
description: Humanities Ethical and legal principles studied as they apply to media. Formerly Human 293

title: Strategic Communication for Leaders
description: (H) This course is designed to explore how strategic communication functions in leadership practice. Students will explore strategic communication literacies and as it relates to leading others in organizational and social contexts. Students will explore how ethics, authenticity, and cultural contexts influence the processes of strategic communication.
prerequisites: Admitted to the OLTM program

title: Introduction to Communication
description: Humanities The purpose of this course is to help students understand and engage in the principles of communication theory as it is practiced in everyday life. The course will cover skills and techniques needed for effective communication in a variety of contexts including interpersonal relationships, groups, multicultural and gender contexts, public communication, and mediated communication. Students will learn how to isolate and define how particular communication processes are related to power, modern life, and identity, and to recognize the different ways that communication impacts our understandings of the world and ourselves.

title: Intro to Mass Media
description: Humanities Students explore the ever-changing world of mass media and its impact on American Society. Formerly Human 290 and JOURN 290

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: Humanities Students will explore the complexity of communication in everyday life. This course will introduce students to major concepts and theories about face-to- face interaction. Students will learn how communication functions in a variety of contexts and relationships, and will improve their understanding of psychological, sociological, and relational factors that influence their communication with others.
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above

title: Public Speaking
description: Humanities The purpose of the course is to give students concentrated opportunities to communicate their ideas. Emphasis is on developing the speaker’s ability to present original subject matter. This is accomplished by the preparation and presentation of four informative and persuasive speeches, as well as various in-class assignments and exercises to develop speaking proficiency by skillfully using logic and reasoning.

title: Small Group Communication
description: Humanities The purpose of this course is to acquaint the student with the important roles played by various groups in which we all interact in our society, with special focus on assisting the student in understanding their own roles and behaviors within these groups. In addition to helping the student assess and improve their own communication skills this course will identify and familiarize students with techniques and procedures that will help the student become more skilled and effective in problem solving.
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above

title: Intercultural Communication
description: (H/DSJ) Intercultural Communication centers on the importance of culture in our everyday lives and communication practices. This course is designed to increase students’ awareness of and sensitivity to other cultures, their own cultural backgrounds, and the social, political, cultural, and historical contexts in which they live and communicate. This course will teach students to communicate effectively in the increasingly culturally diverse and interdependent society. Included content areas are an introduction to major intercultural communication theories and principles, and the application of these principles in local and global contexts. Formerly CMST 153 CMST 253 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Computer Literacy for Online Learning
description: This class will help prepare students for success in online learning by focusing on basic computer literacy and eLearning environments.

title: Computer Literacy Assessment
description: Demonstrate mastery of basic computer use, file management, word processing, spreadsheets, the World Wide Web, and email, through assessment tests. (Pass/No Credit).

title: Introduction to Personal Computers
description: Introduction to personal computers for first time users. Computer terminology, PC hardware options, windows operating systems, basic software techniques and basic Internet use.

title: Information Systems Concepts
description: Explore the fundamentals of information processing. Topics include: hardware, software, networking, the Internet, programming, and databases.
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of Microsoft Windows XP or later. Competent keyboard skills.

title: Introduction to Operating Systems
description: An technical introduction to operating system theory and common operating systems found in business environments, like Windows and Linux, with hands-on activities. Prerequisite: CIS 110 or concurrent enrollment or permission of instructor. ,

title: Introduction to HTML
description: Learn to use HTML tags to create web pages in accordance with XHTML specifications. Create links, format text, create bulleted and numbered lists, insert images and background color/images, produce image maps, create forms, and understand multimedia possibilities. (Pass/No Credit)
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of Microsoft Windows 95 or later. Competent keyboarding skills.

title: Intro to MS Visio
description: Introduction to Microsoft Visio to create flow diagrams, basic organizational charts, and network diagrams. Knowledge of basic computer skills suggested. Text required.

title: Logic and Pattern Matching
description: Students will evaluate mathematical expressions in computer numbering systems, evaluate propositional logic using sets and Boolean circuits, and search and manipulate strings using patterns with regular expressions to support programming and network environments. Formerly CIS 202 Prerequisite: MATH 091 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Exploring Coding
description: An exploration of computer programming for non-programmers presented in a fun, engaging, and visual format. ,

title: Programming Concepts
description: An introduction to programming concepts.

title: Java I Introduction to OOP
description:
An introduction to applications development for Windows and the Web using Java applications and applets. Prerequisite: CIS 141 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.
,

title: Java II Fundamentals of OOP
description:
Develops fundamental concepts and techniques for analysis, design, and implementation of computer programs using an object-oriented language. Includes graphical user interfaces, event-driven programming, recursive techniques, and data structures. Prerequisite: CIS 142 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.
,

title: Introduction to C Language
description:
Writing C programs utilizing programming concepts obtained from CIS 141 . Introducing C syntax for program control, functions, arrays, pointers, and string manipulation. Prerequisite: CIS 141 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.
,

title: Survey of Computing
description: Learn basic concepts of word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, email management, and the Internet. Students without sufficient prior computer skills might benefit from taking CIS 100 first. To self-assess, please take this survey: https://bit.ly/cis-placement-survey ,

title: Web Development I
description:
Students are introduced to HTML5 elements and CSS3 to develop simple Web pages that integrate responsive (mobile) design, media, tables, and forms to produce consistent, navigable Web sites. Prerequisite: CIS 110 or concurrent enrollment, or instructor permission
,

title: Multimedia for the Web
description: Students will explore how digital multimedia is optimized to integrate into a HTML5 web page based on current standards and technologies. Managing and manipulating fonts, images, audio, video, and animation formats and codecs, and issues with hosting and legal considerations will also be covered.
prerequisites: None, though proficiency with HTML is helpful.

title: Technical Support Skills
description: Fundamentals of PC hardware and peripherals, mobile device hardware, networking and troubleshooting hardware and network connectivity issues. Installing and configuring operating systems including Windows, iOS, Android, Apple OS X and Linux. Also introduces security, the fundamentals of cloud computing and operational procedures. This course helps prepare students for the CompTIA A + 220-1001 and 220-1002 exams. Both exams are required for CompTIA A + certification. ,

title: Networking Concepts
description: This course is designed to introduce LAN/WAN terminology, design, topologies, protocols, various network hardware components, the TCP/IP network protocol, software, cabling and connectivity.

title: Information System Project Management
description: The student will learn, apply, analyze and evaluate significant information technology projects from initiation through closing. ,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: SQL
description: Introduction to structured query language (SQL) used by most relational databases. Students will create, manipulate, and query data using DDL and DML. This class is intended for students with fundamental skills in computer programming. Students are strongly encouraged to contact faculty before enrolling in this class to review the prerequisite skills and knowledge needed for a successful experience. The prerequisite skills may be obtained by taking CIS 141 . ,

title: Windows for Professionals
description: Students will learn how to deploy, configure, secure, manage, and monitor device and client applications in a Microsoft enterprise environment. Skills covered include: deploying, maintaining, and updating Windows; configuring connectivity; managing policies and profiles; managing and protecting devices; and managing applications and data. This course helps prepare students for the MD-100 Windows 10 and MD-101 Management Modern Desktops exams to achieve their Microsoft 365 Certified: Modern Desktop Administrator Associate certification. ,

title: Introduction to Data Analytics
description: Analytics is at the heart of modern businesses. Data analysts are those professionals which possess the skills and knowledge needed to perform this vital work. This course prepares students for this type of work and for the vendor-neutral CompTIA Data+ (DA0-001) certification. This course is not restricted to those in the Information Technology (IT) field. The skills and knowledge learned along with the CompTIA Data+ certification can be used in any business environment; Healthcare, Business, Data Security and Privacy, and any other consumer area where data is collected and used. ,

title: Introduction to ASP. NET
description:
Students create interactive and dynamic database-driven Web applications using ASP. NET, C# and the .NET Framework. This class is intended for students with fundamental skills in computer programming, HTML, and SQL. Students are strongly encouraged to contact faculty before enrolling in this class to review the prerequisite skills and knowledge needed for successful experience. Prerequisite: CIS 142 , CIS 155 , and CIS 210 , all with a 2.0 or higher, or instructor permission.
,

title: Information System Security I
description: In this course, we will take an in-depth look at information systems security concepts and techniques. We will examine theoretical concepts that make the world of IS security unique. Also, this course will adopt a practical, hands-on approach when examining information systems security techniques. Along with examining different strategies for mitigating security threats, this course will explore the advancement of information assurance as well as timeless problem-solving strategies.
prerequisites: CIS 110 or concurrent enrollment or permission of instructor.

title: Cybersecurity Analyst
description: Students learn how to apply behavioral analytics to networks and devices to prevent, detect and combat cybersecurity threats through continuous security monitoring and IT regulatory compliance. Helps prepare students for the CompTIA CySA+ certification. , 2 hours Guided Practice

title: Applications Server Support
description: Introduces the skills necessary to be a server administrator. Concepts covered include server architecture, administration, storage, security, networking, troubleshooting, and disaster recovery. This course helps prepare students for the CompTIA Server+ SK0-004 certification exam. ,

title: Java III
description:
Review the details of the Java language as covered in the Java Certification Oracle Certified Associate Exam 1Z0-808 and Java Certification Oracle Certified Professional Exam 1Z0-809. Topics covered include Core Java APIs, Class Design, Exceptions, Generics, Lambdas, Streams, I/O, Concurrency. Prepares students for the Oracle Certification Exams 1Z0-808 and 1Z0-809. Students are required to take and pass one of the two exams. Prerequisite: CIS 143 OR CS 143 with grade of 2.0 or better, or Instructor Permission.
,

title: Pen Testing
description: Provides intermediate skills for cybersecurity professionals who are tasked with hands-on penetration testing to identify, exploit, report, and manage vulnerabilities on a network. Prepares students for the CompTIA PenTest+ certification. ,

title: Computer Hacking Forensic Investigator
description:
Students learn to use current computer forensic procedures and tools. Students will learn the techniques and processes for detecting cybercrime intrusions, implement the steps to properly extract and evaluate evidence, and conduct system audits to prevent future cybercrimes. Prerequisite: CIS 111 and CIS 182 with a 2.0 or higher, or instructor permission.
,

title: Web Scripting
description: Students build upon the skills of Web Development I to introduce JavaScript, libraries, and simple server side processing within the HTML5 framework. Prerequisite: CIS 141 and CIS 155 .

title: Content Management Systems
description: Students will focus on managing and developing Content Management Systems, such as Wordpress. Students will set up a content management system to run on the internet. Formerly CIS 258 Web 2.0
prerequisites: CIS 155

title: User Interface Design
description: N/A Students will be introduced to designing for screens and developing user interfaces based on design principles and design elements. User experience, and useability will be the focus as students create working prototypes of their interfaces using a variety of programs. Formerly CIS 160 User Interface Design (2 credits)
prerequisites: CIS 155 - OR - ART 110 - OR- DMA 120 - OR - Permission of instructor

title: Linux I
description:
An introduction to the Linux operating system and Linux system administration. This course helps prepare student for the CompTIA Linux+ exam. Prerequisite: CIS 111 and CIS 141 with a grade of 2.0 or above or permission of instructor.
,

title: Cisco I
description: The student will be introduced to and understand the development in the design and installation of local area networks to ensure optimal throughput. ,

title: Cisco II
description:
An introduction to Cisco switching and routing and delves into wireless local area networks (WLANs) and security concepts. Prerequisite: CIS 270 .
,

title: Cisco III
description:
This course will enable the student to implement a switched network and a basic wireless network. Prerequisite: CIS 270 .
,

title: Cybersecurity Operations
description:
Students learn the key components of cybersecurity operations, including security concepts, security monitoring, host-based analysis, network intrusion analysis, and security policies and procedures. Helps prepare students for the Cisco CBROPS certification. Prerequisite: CIS 236 with a minimum grade of 2.0 or instructor permission.
,

title: Object Oriented Programming with C
description: Writing object oriented programs utilizing C. Introduces concepts of data abstraction, data classes, and polymorphism. Prerequisite: CIS 142 or CIS 145 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.

title: CIS Practicum
description: A capstone course providing in-depth hands-on experience in one of the seven areas of computer information systems: networking, hardware, security, web, project management, database, or programming.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Introduction to Computing and Computer Ethics
description: (NS/DSJ) An exploration of the fundamentals of computer science. In this course students will gain practical programming skills: converting natural language problems into formal algorithms and computer programs, developing debug strategies, and verifying solutions meet requirements. We will also consider the foundations of computer ethics, particularly the impact of software and information technology on our global society, communities, and individuals.
prerequisites: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 (Intermediate Algebra) with a 2.0 or better

title: Computer Science II Java
description: Natural Science Solving problems using object-oriented programming techniques and basic data structures; design and analysis of algorithms particularly in the context of searching and sorting. Formerly CS 177/MATH 177 Prerequisite: CS& 141 with grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Linear Algebra for Data Analysis
description: (NS) This course uses a high-level programming language as a vehicle to discuss those aspects of linear algebra that are most important in data analytics. Students will develop intuition and an understanding of how to use linear algebra, rather than on proofs. Topics include basic matrix operations, linear transformations, ranges, linear combinations and spans, systems of linear equations, symmetric matrices, inverses, determinants, triangular matrices, trace, eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Formerly N/A
prerequisites: Math&151 and CS& 141 with a 2.0 or better

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Web Application Programming
description: (NS) Students will build and deploy a web application using an industry standard 1) backend server programming language, 2) frontend UI framework, and 3) relational database technology. Students will learn to organize a large codebase with multiple subsystems and how to decompose business logic and engineering requirements into database tables, migrations, code, and user interfaces. They will learn to design and build representational state transfer application programming interfaces (REST APIs) to decouple their frontend and backend stacks. They will also learn to leverage industry standard testing frameworks for both frontend and backend code. Prerequisite: CS& 141 or CIS 142

title: Software Development Tools
description: (NS) Students will learn the tools of modern software development from the ground up. This begins by learning to use an industry standard editor, along with its efficiency-boosting AI-powered capabilities. Students will learn to organize their code and development processes with a version control system. They will also learn to work on code hosted on remote Linux boxes via (Secure Socket Shell)SSH and how to configure continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipeline to deploy code to production environments. In short, students will learn a variety of tools for working with code, managing it, moving it from place to place, and for automating such processes. Prerequisite: CS& 141 or CIS 142

title: Computer Science Career Seminar
description: This course reviews a variety of computer science careers and reflects on their associated skill requirements and professional responsibilities, mapping these to educational requirements and ethical implications. Students create or refine a professional persona including resume and online portfolio, identify local professional networks and networking events, and investigate graduate education, community-based learning, internship, and mentorships. The course will cover all life stages of searching for and obtaining employment in the field of computer science, including how to prepare for a technical interview, how to negotiate your first salary, and how to keep the job you just landed. Prerequisite: CS& 141 or CIS 142

title: Foundations of Computer Science
description: (NS) This course is an introduction to computer science in the context of other scientific disciplines, such as the physical and biological sciences. Students will practice object-oriented programming and apply elementary data structures and algorithms to medium-scale applications. Students will also gain exposure to the theory of computing and computing machines. Topics include: programming in Java, hardware and software systems, algorithms and data structures, fundamental principles of computation, and machine architecture.
prerequisites: Admitted to the BSCS Program

title: Computer Organization and Architecture
description: (NS) An introduction to computer organization and architecture. Students will learn to write programs that run on “bare metal” without any services provided by the Operating System such as time, input/output (I/O), and other events in C and assembly language. Additionally, the course covers the different components of a modern microprocessor and the fundamentals of the fetch/decode/execute cycle and pipelining. Formerly N/A
prerequisites: CS 243

title: Databases and Information Management Systems
description: (NS) Exploration and practice with relational and non-relational databases, including cloud databases. Students will differentiate between various types of databases (e.g., relational, NoSQL, graph) and their use cases. They will understand and apply the principles of data modeling using Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams and normalize database designs to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity. They will write complex Structured Query Language (SQL) queries to retrieve, insert, update, and delete data from a database. They will design a database system and implement a database-driven application.
prerequisites: CS 143 Computer Science II Java

title: Data Structures and Algorithms 1
description: (NS) This course thoroughly examines how algorithm design impacts performance and growth rate of data structures and algorithms from CS 143. Students also implement and compare self-balancing trees including AVL, Red-Black, and B-Trees. Additionally, students will traverse undirected graphs and find the shortest path and minimum spanning tree. Lastly, learn to apply greedy algorithms and concepts of dynamic programming. Formerly N/A
prerequisites: CS 143 - Computer Science II with a 2.0 or better

title: Data Structures and Algorithms 2
description: (NS) Students will solve problems using algorithms relating to Path Finding, Network Flow, and Computational Geometry. Also, this course introduces constraints solving using Linear Programming and problem solving with Parallel Programming. Formerly N/A
prerequisites: CS 333

title: Software Engineering & Capstone Project I
description: (NS) Fundamentals of software engineering including analysis of system requirements, evaluation of appropriate engineering compromises, and application of collaborative software development practices and tools, such as Scrum, Kanban and other Agile practices. Students will also utilize agile methodologies to build a system for real stakeholders, which means this course will also serve as a hands-on context for devops, testing, and team collaboration techniques learned in previous courses.
prerequisites: CS 243 Software Development Tools, CS 233 Web Application Programming

title: Software Development Practicum
description: (NS) Students will apply their programming and software development skills to a hands-on, real-world project of their choosing. (The course will be taken 4 times for 4 credits total.)
prerequisites: Entry into Bachelor of Science in Computer Science (BSCS) program

title: Algorithmic Problem Solving
description: (NS) Apply understanding of complex algorithms to real-world scenarios in mock interview settings. This course helps to prepare students for technical interviews, a critical step in the application process for many computer science positions.
prerequisites: CS 333 Data Structures and Algorithms 1

title: Statistical Methods for User Research
description: (NS) How to use statistics to solve common quantitative problems that arise in the development and execution of user research studies. Themes include summarizing data and computing margins of error, determining statistical significance, finding appropriate sample sizes for a study, and investigating relationships among variables.
prerequisites: Entry into Bachelor of Science in Computer Science (BSCS) program

title: Theory of Computation
description: (NS) The Theory of Computation course delves into the foundational concepts that govern the capabilities and limitations of computation. It investigates what computers can and cannot compute, even with unlimited resources. Through formal models like automata and Turing machines, students will explore the formal definitions of algorithms, computability, and computational complexity. This course provides the theoretical underpinnings that every computer scientist should understand to grasp the boundaries of what is computationally feasible.
prerequisites: CS 143 Computer Science II Java, CS/MATH 210 Discrete Math

title: Principles of Computer Systems
description: (NS) Exposure to the design, implementation, and evaluation of computer systems, including operating systems and networking
prerequisites: CS 243 Software Development Tools with a 2.0 or better

title: Programming Languages
description: (NS) This course provides an in-depth exploration of the fundamental concepts, paradigms, and design principles underlying programming languages. Students will gain hands-on experience with several families of programming languages and will develop a deep understanding of how and why various programming languages are designed the way they are.
prerequisites: CS 143 Computer Science II Java

title: Foundations of Artificial Intelligence
description: (NS) This course will introduce students to the fundamentals of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students will cover topics including Search, Logic, Planning, Uncertainty, Reasoning, Learning and Applications of Artificial Intelligence (AI)
prerequisites: CS 333 Data Structures and Algorithms 1, CS 402 Statistical Methods for User Research

title: Capstone Project 2
description: (NS) This course is a continuation of CS 350. Students will continue to work on the project devised in the previous course and will bring the project to completion (delivering it to stakeholders) by the end of this course.
prerequisites: CS350: Software Engineering & Capstone Project 1

title: Computer Science I Java
description: Natural Science Problem solving methodology and basic programming abilities and concepts in JAVA. Formerly CS 176/MATH 176 Prerequisite: MATH& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or higher ( MATH& 142 with a grade of 2.0 or higher is recommended).

title: Introduction to Construction Industry
description: This is a combination/lecture and lab course which includes, introduction to the construction industry, occupations and careers in the industry, construction and worksite safety, knowledge, skills and attitudes required, trade unions, apprenticeships, and building codes. The lab portion includes measuring and layout, hand and power tool identification, use, care, maintenance and safety, building materials and supplies, PPE, job site safety and working conditions, safely working at heights, along with supervised student practice and course project assignments.

title: Residential Print Reading
description: Introduction to reading and interpretation of architectural prints for planning and building residential and light construction.

title: Foundations & Floor Framing
description: This is a combination knowledge and skills based course dedicated to the study of residential and light construction techniques and materials used in foundation forming and floor framing.

title: Wall & Roof Framing and Construction
description: This is a combination knowledge and skills based course dedicated to the study of residential and light construction techniques and materials used in wall and roof framing.

title: Interior and Exterior Finishing
description: This is a combination knowledge and skills course dedicated to interior and exterior finishing materials and construction techniques.

title: Stairway Design and Construction
description: This is an introduction to the design and construction of residential and commercial stair framing. Topics will include design factors, building codes, terminology used in stair construction, layout, cutting and installation of stringers, risers, treads and skirt boards.

title: Introduction to Cosmetology
description: Students are exposed to Washington State Cosmetology laws, rules and regulations, career opportunities, business skills, professional image, communication and the history of Cosmetology. Awareness of self-care, diet, hydration, body mechanics, exercise, goal setting, ethics, and positive attitude.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Cosmetology General Sciences
description: Emphasis is placed on the skills and knowledge of the general sciences necessary for the field of cosmetology. Introducing basics of anatomy & physiology with attention to the body organs and systems and focus on scalp, nail, skin health to include diseases and disorders.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Hair Care, Hairstyling & Haircutting
description: Introduction to haircare services, treatments for scalp care, the effects of growth patterns, density, and texture. Students will learn the technical aspects of haircutting using elevation, angles, and over direction, along with proper shear, razor, and clipper usage. The foundations of hairstyling to include consultation, hair types, styling aids, creating/pinning and updo basics.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Chemical Texture Services
description: This course will cover chemicals used for cosmetology services to change hair structure. Topics will include safe use, chemical interaction and end result of chemical texture services to include perming, chemical relaxing, curl reformation, and smoothing treatment options.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Basics of Haircolor
description: Introduction to basic color theory and various techniques utilizing the law of color and design. Study of hair structure as it pertains to the chemical interaction of artificial pigments deposited into the hair using semi, demi, permanent haircolor. Introduction of removing color with various lighteners while maintaining hair integrity.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Intermediate Haircutting
description: The technical elements of intermediate haircutting needed to provide additional knowledge and skills. Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in COS 103 and COS 123 .

title: Advanced Chemical Texture Services
description: Introduction to basic color theory and technique utilizing the law of color to artificially pigment the hair or remove color pigment. Prerequisite: Completion of: COS 104 or concurrent enrollment in COS 104 .

title: Intermediate Hair Color
description: Building on basic hair color techniques to increase skill level of dimensional color and creative color placement and to introduce color correction. Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in COS 105 and COS 225 .

title: Cosmetology Skin Care & Services
description: Principles of esthetics including skin analysis, care of the skin and temporary hair removal. Introduction to basic makeup application techniques implementing cosmetic color theory concepts and face shapes.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Facial Makeup
description: Basic makeup application techniques implementing cosmetic color theory concepts.
prerequisites: None.

title: Advanced Haircutting
description: Advanced haircutting styles combining an array of haircutting tools to include razor, texturizing tools, and clippers. These styles and techniques are designed to increase skill level in subject matter mastery. Introduction to basic electricity as it pertains to cosmetology.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Cosmetology Nail Care & Services
description: Fundamental principles of nail care to include structure, growth, diseases and disorders to safely perform basic manicure and pedicure services. Gain understanding of gel nails, acrylic and other artificial nail products and procedures.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Wigs, Braiding and Extensions
description: Introduction to wig fitting, styling and care of wigs and braiding styles. Exposure to artificial hair applications to include box braids and various extension types such as clip-in, tape-in, sew-in, and micro bead with proper safety and removal techniques.
prerequisites: Instructor approval

title: Cosmetology Lab Clinic I
description: Students perform hands on skills with supervision, in the areas of haircutting, chemical procedures and hairstyling services to include draping, shampooing, thermal styling, wet styling such as fingerwaves and roller sets. Once introduced to the techniques in the live lab, students will further their knowledge and techniques with online video training for the hybrid portion of this class. This hybrid course includes a minimum of 70 face to face hours with the remaining hours up to 70, required online.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Cosmetology Lab Clinic II
description: Students continue to perfect hands on skills with supervision, in the areas of customer service, client protection, hairstyling, haircutting, chemical procedures while expanding their skills to include more advanced techniques.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Cosmetology Lab Clinic III
description: Students continue to perfect timing and execution of hands on skills with supervision, in the areas of customer service, client protection, hairstyling, haircutting, chemical procedures, manicuring, and basic esthetic services. Prerequisite: COS 152 and Instructor permission

title: Cosmetology Lab Clinic IV
description: Students are self-correcting while still refining skills and executing hands on techniques with less supervision and improved timing , in the areas of customer service, client protection, hairstyling, haircutting, chemical procedures to include permanent waving, relaxers, color and lightening, manicuring, and basic esthetic services. Prerequisite: COS 152 or COS 153 . Must have instructor permission.

title: Cosmetology Lab Clinic V
description: Students work independently with minimal supervision and more collaboration with instructor about the execution plan for services performed. Work is increasingly performed on clients as they near completion of the program. Refinement of skills, timing and execution of hands on techniques continues in the areas of customer service, client protection, hairstyling, haircutting, chemical procedures to include permanent waving, relaxers, color and lightening, manicuring, and basic esthetic services. Prerequisite: COS 154 and Instructor permission

title: Introduction to Esthetics
description: Student is exposed to Washington State Esthetics laws, rules and regulations, Life Skills including Ethics, Professional Image, Communicating for Success, Career Opportunities and the History of Esthetics. Students will earn the Dermalogica Meaningful Connections Certification.
prerequisites: part of first-term curriculum, corequisite with COS 161, 171, and 181

title: Esthetics General Sciences I
description: Emphasis is placed on the skills and knowledge of the general sciences necessary for the field of Esthetics, Safety and Sanitation, Infection Control, Disorders and Diseases of the Skin. Students will earn Barbicide and Barbicide Covid 19 Certifications.
prerequisites: instructor permission required – part of first term curriculum, corequisite with COS 160, 171, and 181

title: Esthetics General Sciences II
description: Instruction in Nutritional needs to become a Healthy Professional, Chemistry & Chemical Safety, Electricity & Electrical Safety, Anatomy, Physiology and Histology of the Skin.
prerequisites: COS 160, 161, 171, 181 – second term corequisite with COS 172 and 182

title: Esthetic Skin Care I
description: Instruction in Skin Analysis, Skin Care Products including Chemistry, Ingredients and Selection, Treatment Room preparation, Facial treatments, and beginning Hair Removal. Weekly product research focusing on professional products. Students will earn Dermalogica Clean Touch Certification, and complete the d.n.a. courses to become Dermalogica Certified.
prerequisites: instructor permission – corequisite of COS 160, 161, and 181

title: Esthetics Skin Care II
description: Instruction of Facial Massage, Facial Devices and Technology, clinic operations, advanced Hair Removal, lash & brow enhancements. Weekly product research focusing on product ingredients. Students earn Dermalogica Acids, Peels and Exfoliants Certification and Meet Pro Power Peel Certification.
prerequisites: COS 160, 161, 171, 181 – corequisite part of second term with COS 162 and 182

title: Esthetics Skin Care III
description: Instruction on basic Make-up application, Advanced Topics and Treatments, clinic operations, and review body treatments, chemical exfoliation, laser, light therapy, microcurrent, and equipment for medical esthetics.Weekly product research focusing on retail products and sales. Students earn Dermalogica Makeup Artist Clean Touch Certification and Advanced Pro Power Peel Certification.
prerequisites: COS 162, 172, 182 – corequisite with COS 180 and 183

title: Business Practices & State Board Prep
description: Instruction for Washington State written and practical skills exam and the licensing process. Instruction on Career Planning, On-the-Job Operations, the beauty business including resume writing, retail sales, business outlines, booth rental, ownership and marketing strategies. Student will demonstrate skill and proficiency prior to completion of the program.
prerequisites: COS 162, 172, 182 – corequisite with COS 173 and 183

title: Esthetics Lab Clinic I
description: Students perform esthetic services with supervision on both mannequins and models, gaining hands on practical knowledge and experience in Skin analysis, Product Selections, Preparing the treatment Room, Basic Facial Treatments and Beginning Hair Removal. Students will also review training videos online for the hybrid portion of this class.
prerequisites: Instructor permission – corequisite with COS 160, 161, 171

title: Esthetics Lab Clinic II
description: Students perform esthetic services with supervision, gaining hands on practical experience in Advanced Skin Analysis and Product Selections, Facial Treatments with Massage, Facial Devices, Technology, Hair Removal, Lash and Brow Enhancements, while perfecting their guest services and timing. Students will also watch training videos online for the hybrid portion of this class to further knowledge and techniques.
prerequisites: COS 160, 161, 171, 181 – part of second term, corequisite with COS 162 and 172

title: Esthetics Lab Clinic III
description: Students perform esthetic services with supervision, gaining hands on practical experience in all areas of Esthetic services including Basic Makeup application, clinic operations and Advanced Treatments. Students will also prepare for the practical portion of the State Board Exam. Students will also watch training videos online for the hybrid portion of this class to further knowledge and techniques.
prerequisites: COS 162, 172, 182 – corequisite with COS 173 and 180

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Methods of Teaching & Learning
description: This course for career education instructors covers teaching methods and classroom preparation to include: Qualities of the career education instructor, teaching plan and learning environment, teaching study and testing skills, basic learning styles and principles, methods of teaching and communicating confidently.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Classroom Management & Supervision
description: This course for career education instructors covers effective presentations, effective classroom management and super vision and achieving learner results.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Program Development & Lesson Planning
description: This course for career education instructors covers program and curriculum development, lesson planning, educational aids and technology in the classroom, assessing progress and advising students.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Basic Teaching Skills
description: This course covers the student salon, performance goals, teamwork and record keeping requirements.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Professional Development
description: This course covers educator relationships, conditions for learning, integrating humor in the classroom and creativity in instruction, teaching success strategies for a successful career, teamwork, and evaluating professional performance.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Braiding and Extension Techniques
description: Designed to introduce a variety of methods of hair additions and extensions.
prerequisites: Proof of Current Cosmetology License or Proof of Cosmetology Student Enrollment in a Licensed School.

title: Advanced Hair Coloring
description: Expand the mastery of haircolor to problem solve color correction. Build to understand advanced hair color methods to improve skill level and ability to combine multiple hair color applications while incorporating color into hairstyling to enhance the overall design.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Business Skills for Cosmetology
description: Preparing for and seeking employment by creating a resume, cover letter and practicing interviewing skills to assist in obtaining a position in the field of cosmetology. Understanding salon business and professionalism, business planning, clientele building & marketing and retail.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Business Skills II
description: Salon business and professionalism, business planning, marketing and retail. Prerequisite: Completion of: COS 231 .

title: State Board Preparation
description: Preparation for Washington State written and practical exams through book review, viewing preparatory testing videos, taking mock written test as study enhancements, detailing the state website highlighting details of laws, rules and expectations. Preparing physical kit for practical exam.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Cadet Clinic Lab I
description: Student performs application of teaching methods while performing student teaching. Student will gain hands on practical experience working in a training salon/spa environment using knowledge and skills achieved from related instruction.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Cadet Clinic Lab II
description: Student performs application of teaching methods while performing student teaching. Student will gain hands on practical experience working in a training salon/spa environment using knowledge and skills achieved from related instruction.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Cadet Clinic Lab III
description: Student performs application of teaching methods while performing student teaching. Student will gain hands on practical experience working in a training salon/spa environment using knowledge and skills achieved from related instruction.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Cadet Clinic Lab IV
description: Student performs application of teaching methods while performing student teaching. Student will gain hands on practical experience working in a training salon/spa environment using knowledge and skills achieved from related instruction.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Culinary Techniques
description: Working in the commercial kitchen: equipment, knife skills, and food product identification.
prerequisites: Advisor signature and Food Handler’s Permit.

title: Food Production I
description: Prepare meats, seafood, poultry, soups, vegetables, starches and basic desserts for restaurant and commercial food service.
prerequisites: Kitsap Food Workers Health Card/ advisor signature.

title: Dining Room Service
description: How to properly serve food to guests in a restaurant. For potential and actual waiters/waitresses and also managers or supervisors who train the servers.
prerequisites: Instructor signature.

title: ServSafe Food Safety Training
description: The ServSafe course provides accurate up-to-date information for all levels of employees on all aspects of handling food; from receiving and storing to preparing and serving.
prerequisites: Instructor signature.

title: Sustainable Food System of Kitsap County
description: Sustainable Kitsap County is a comprehensive tour of food establishments and their practices as related to the food system of Kitsap County: The family farm/homestead, Barner property on Olympic College campus, bringing food/ related items to market, Farmers Markets, grocery/Co-op stores, local certified kitchens, Kitsap Health District, Kitsap Poultry Grocers Co-op, Puget Sound Meat Producers Co-op, local seafood purveyors, foraging organizations, and gleaning programs.

title: Food Production II
description: Classic food preparation technique: sauces, soups, fabrication of poultry, seafood and meat.
prerequisites: Cooks Helper Certificate.

title: Garde Manger
description: Develop skills producing a variety of cold food products. Prepare items appropriate for buffet presentation including decorative pieces.
prerequisites: Certificate/Prep Cook.

title: International Cuisine
description: History of various countries’ cuisine covering major food sources, cooking methods and influences on cuisine.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Applied Food Service Computation
description: This course is designed to prepare students to perform basic applied math needed in the field of culinary arts. Topics will include factions and percentages, estimating food costs, scaling recipes, recipe yield conversions, and food purchasing strategies.

title: Commercial Baking I
description: Applying fundamentals of baking science to the preparation of a variety of products.
prerequisites: Certificate/Prep Cook.

title: Food Production III
description: This course will cover creation of a menu from start to finish, breakfast to dinner.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Quantity Food Purchasing
description: The operations of purchasing and receiving including invoicing, pricing, product costing, and inventories to promote fiscal controls.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Nutrition for Culinary Professionals
description: For students in the culinary program; this course is for those needing to use nutritional principles in menu and recipe planning.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Food Production IV
description: Create menus, buffets, and specialty dishes for fine dining from preparation to order (including ala carte) and determine entire cost.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Culinary Management
description: The chef as a supervisor, trainer, and as a manager in the day to day working of a food service operation.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Culinary Internship
description: This is an unpaid six week work experience related to the Culinary/Hospitality field of study. 18 hours Clinic

title: Clinical Dental Hygiene I
description: Students will perform basic dental hygiene skills and procedures including infection control and the use of probes, explorers, mouth mirrors, air/water syringe, and suction in a clinical setting.
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene II​
description: ​​​​Students apply knowledge of assessment, planning, diagnosis, implementation, evaluation and documentation while providing dental hygiene preventive and therapeutic services to patients in a clinical environment. Patient care includes oral assessment, oral prophylaxis and periodontal maintenance, fluoride treatment, and oral hygiene education.​
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene III​
description: Students apply knowledge of assessment, planning, diagnosis, implementation, and documentation while providing dental hygiene preventive and therapeutic services to patients in a clinical environment. Patient care builds off DHYG 302 and includes treating patients with periodontal diseases and moderate deposits.
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene IV​
description: Continued clinical activities with increased difficulty in the type and number of cases. Students will continue to build confidence in assessment, diagnosis, planning, implementation, evaluation, and documentation.
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene V​
description: ​​Continues clinical activities to include treating moderate periodontal patients and patients with moderate/heavy deposits; activities will correlate to theory lecture course. Students will begin to rotate through assigned community externship sites. ​
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene VI​
description: Continues clinical activities including treatment of patients having moderate to severe periodontal disease with varying levels of calculus. Students will also begin clinical activities that will help prepare them for boards and private practice. ​
prerequisites: Acceptance into and continued enrollment in the dental hygiene program

title: Clinical ​Dental Hygiene VII​
description: A culmination of all previous clinical courses moving through the dental hygiene process of care. Clinical activities include treatment of patients with varying levels of disease and calculus.
