title: Intermediate Digital Photography
description:
Intermediate instruction and practice in digital photography, including background, equipment, shooting techniques, lighting, scanning, manipulation, and output (web or print). Formerly DMA 137 Prerequisite: DMA 136 or permission of instructor.
,

title: DMA Practicum
description: A capstone course providing in-depth hands-on experience in Digital Media. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credits.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Intro to Theatre
description: (H) An overview of theatre arts, including the nature of theatre, its role in society, activities of playwrights, directors, designers, and performers. Attendance at two outside performances is required.

title: Professionalism and Ethics in ECE
description: Examine personal philosophy, professional qualifications, ethical practices and the development of interpersonal skills necessary in the early learning workplace.

title: Practicum II
description:
Intermediate level practical application in the working world of theories and methods studied in the Early Childhood program. Formerly ECE 151/151A Prerequisite: ECED& 120 , ECED& 160 , and ECED& 190 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Mathematics for Early Childhood Ed
description: Math for early learning environments. Addresses how children learn and understand mathematical concepts including whole numbers, fractions, geometry, measurement, data analysis and problem solving. Formerly ECE 164 Prerequisite: MATH 090 with a grade of at least 2.0 or placement test score.

title: Environmental Evaluation
description: Evaluating the early childhood environment using an industry standard tool (the ECERS) to ensure a quality experience for children and to optimize learning and development. Formerly ECE 166

title: Music and Movement for Young Children
description: This course introduces teachers to the sequence of physical and motor development of young children as well as activities and equipment to promote optimum movement and physical education to the young child. In addition, musical concepts such as body rhythms, songs, sounds, instruments, records, and musical environments suitable for the early learning environment are introduced. Formerly ECE 176 and 182

title: Science for Young Children
description: The role of science in the education and development of the young child, including an overview of cognitive characteristics, appropriate materials and activities. Formerly ECE 177

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Practicum III
description:
Students apply cumulative knowledge to practice skills with children and professional interactions with families and staff in a developmentally appropriate early childhood setting. Formerly ECE 201 Prerequisite: ECED& 120 , ECED 151 , or permission of instructor.
, 12 hours Clinic

title: ECE Professional Portfolio
description: A seminar to evaluate individual professional ePortfolio documentation of key assessments from coursework in the early childhood education program and to plan short and long term professional improvement goals. Formerly ECE 215

title: History & Theory of Early Childhood Education
description: This course will provide students with an overview of philosophical, psychological, behavioral, educational, and contemporary influences in the field of early childhood education. Further, students will examine notable theorists, trends, and the theories influencing child development.

title: Issues and Trends in ECE
description: Current issues and trends impacting ECE field. National/international developments, concerns facing teachers, families, children and society today. Formerly ECE 225

title: Child Care Basics
description: Designed to meet licensing requirements for early learning lead teachers and family home child care providers, STARS 30 hour basics course recognized in the MERIT system. Topics: child growth/development, cultural competency, community resources, guidance, health/safety/ nutrition and professional practice. Formerly 2-credit ECE 100

title: Introduction to Early Childhood Education
description: Social Science Overview of the foundations of early childhood education. Examine theories defining the field, issues and trends, best practices, and program models. Observe children, professionals, and programs in action.

title: Health/Safety/Nutrition
description: Develop knowledge and skills to ensure good health, nutrition and safety of children in group care and education programs. Recognize the signs of abuse/neglect and reporting and available community resources. Formerly Replaced 3-credit ECE 184

title: Practicum-Nurturing Rel
description: Introductory level application of theories of best practice in an early learning setting. Focus on developing supportive relationships while keeping children healthy and safe. Formerly Replaced 3-credit ECE 101 ,

title: Infants/Toddlers Care
description: Examine the unique developmental needs of infants and toddlers. Study the role of the caregiver, relationships with families, developmentally appropriate practices, nurturing environments for infants and toddlers, and culturally relevant care. Formerly ECE 163

title: Family Child Care
description: The basics of home/family child care program management. Topics include: licensing requirements; business management; relationship building; health, safety, & nutrition; guiding behavior and; promoting growth & development. Formerly ECE 189

title: Outdoor Learning for Young Children
description: This course is an exploration of nature-based, outdoor learning experiences for young children. Students will identify the benefits of outdoor learning for children, learn about the teacher’s role in supporting learning in the outdoor learning environment, consider adult comfort levels with outdoor experiences, and examine risk-taking as a part of learning.

title: Home Visitor/Parent Engagement
description: Plan and provide home visits and group activities that promote secure parent-child relationships and support families to provide high-quality early learning experiences that are embedded in everyday routines and experiences.

title: Administration of Early Learning Programs
description: Focuses on developing administrative skills required to develop, open, operate, manage, and assess early childhood education and care programs. Explore techniques and resources available for licensing and NAEYC standard compliance. Formerly ECE 191

title: Curriculum Development
description: Investigate learning theory, program planning, and tools for curriculum development promoting fine/gross motor, social-emotional, cognitive and creative skills and growth in young children. Formerly Includes content formerly in 3-credit ECE 165

title: Environments-Young Child
description: Design, evaluate, and improve indoor and outdoor environments which ensure quality learning, nurturing experiences, and optimize the development of young children. Formerly ECE 167

title: Language & Literacy Development
description: Develop strategies for language acquisition and literacy skill development at each developmental stage through the four interrelated areas of speaking, listening, writing, and reading. Formerly ECE 179

title: Observation/Assessment
description: Collect and record observation and assessment data in order to plan for and support the child, the family, the group and community. Practice reflection techniques, summarizing conclusions and communicating data. Formerly Includes content formerly in 2-credit ECE 171 ,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Micro Economics
description: Social Science Supply and demand; cost and revenue analysis; pure competition; agriculture; monopoly; imperfect competition; antitrust policy; regulation; factor incomes; unions. Prerequisite: Completion of MATH 098 or MATH 099 or above with a minimum grade of 2.0 within the last 6 years, or satisfactory placement into any college-level MATH course. Satisfactory placement into ENGL& 101. Business transfer students are required to take MATH 099 as a prerequisite.

title: Macro Economics
description: Social Science Fundamentals of a private-enterprise economy; national income; employment; inflation; growth; money; the monetary system; Keynesian Economics; Monetarist Economics; international trade.
prerequisites: Completion of MATH 098 or MATH 099 or above with a minimum grade of 2.0 within the last 6 years, or satisfactory placement into any college-level MATH course. Satisfactory placement into ENGL& 101. Business transfer students are required to take MATH 099 as a prerequisite.

title: Instructional Strategies
description: This is a course designed to give students a foundation of the instructional process, from planning, implementing, and evaluating instruction. Students will gain an understanding of the role of the learning process, best educational practices, the use of on-going assessment, and modifying instruction to meet the needs of all learners.

title: Trauma Informed Teaching
description: Course addresses using social-emotional learning strategies and content for recognizing, understanding, and responding to trauma and toxic stress, developing trauma-sensitive classroom spaces and behavioral practices, and using trauma-informed approaches to build positive student, teacher, and family relationships.

title: Introduction to Special Education
description: Survey course that provides both an historical and a contemporary overview of the field of special education and how it is utilized in K-12 schools. Social inequity is a key theme in this course. Students will gain an increased understanding of the marginalization of differently abled students and the ramifications of institutionalized academic discrimination. In addition, the course focuses on the various types of exceptionalities including learning challenged students, gifted students, second language learners, the characteristics of such learners, the legal requirements and laws connected to special education, the teacher’s role in accommodating the needs of exceptional learners, the role of the family in an exceptional learner’s education, inclusive education,and instructional best practices including Universal Design for Learning (UDL), Response to Intervention (RTI), differentiated instruction, among others. Formerly Not applicable.
prerequisites: None.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Practicum
description: This course is a practical application course in the working world of the basic theories and methods studied in the Education pathway.
prerequisites: ECED& 160 Methods of Curriculum Development

title: Classroom Management
description: This course is designed to provide prospective teachers with the foundational knowledge and practical skills necessary to effectively manage K-12 classrooms. Classroom management is essential for creating a positive learning environment where students can thrive academically, socially, and emotionally. Students will learn various strategies for establishing routines, fostering positive relationships, addressing challenging behaviors, and promoting student engagement. Formerly EDUC 123
prerequisites: EDUC& 130 is recommended.

title: Paraeducator Basics
description: An introduction to roles and responsibilities of the Paraeducator in the K-12 educational system. Students will explore techniques supporting instruction, professional and ethical practices, positive and safe learning environments, effective communication and teamwork.

title: Child Development
description: Build a functional understanding of the foundation of child development, prenatal to age eleven. Observe and document physical, social, emotional, and cognitive development of children, reflective of cross cultural and global perspectives.

title: Child Development I: Birth to 8
description: (SS) Provides an overview of typical developmental sequences for children from birth to age 8, the conditions impacting development and the history and theories of child development.

title: Child Development II: 8-Teen
description: Social Science Survey of the development of children from middle childhood through adolescence. Includes social, emotional, physical, motor, intellectual, moral and language characteristics. Prerequisite: EDUC& 121 .

title: Guiding Behavior
description: Examine the principles and theories promoting social competence in young children and creating safe learning environments. Develop skills promoting effective interactions, providing positive individual guidance, and enhancing group experiences. Formerly ECE 185

title: School Age Care
description: Develop skills to provide developmentally appropriate and culturally relevant activities and care, specifically: preparing the environment, implementing curriculum, building relationships, guiding academic /social skill development, and community outreach. Formerly ECE 160

title: Child/Family/Community
description: Integrate the family and community in which a child develops. Explore cultures and demographics of families in society, community resources, strategies for involving families in the education of their child, and tools for effective communication. Formerly ECE 210

title: Introduction to Education
description: (SS/DSJ) Introduction to the role of education in society, the sociological and psychological aspects; an orientation to the personal, academic, and professional requisites that contribute to success. Formerly EDUC 101

title: Introduction to Inclusive Education
description: (SS) Exploring trends, resources, and strategies for including children with disabilities, and their families, in the educational and the wider communities.

title: Diversity in Education
description: (DSJ) This course focuses on the need to recognize and understand the similarities and Differences among people and develop a respect for all individuals and groups. Assists teachers to recognize the learning needs of children from Different racial, ethnic, cultural and socioeconomic groups and to encourage teachers to integrate multicultural/diversity teaching into the early learning environment. Formerly ECE 190, ECED 240

title: Direct Current
description: Fundamentals of direct current from Ohm’s Law through network theorems. Prerequisite: MATH 094 or equivalent.

title: Alternating Current
description: Principles of inductance, capacitance, impedance, resonance, and filters. Prerequisite: ELECT 101 or equivalent.

title: Introduction to Solid-State
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of diode and bipolar transistor theory. Prerequisite: ELECT 102 or equivalent.

title: Introduction to Ship Systems
description: An introduction to shipboard work, the course will cover foundational terminology, hand tools, safety and compartmentation.

title: Electronic Fabrication
description: Basic skill development through hands-on practice is emphasized covering such topics as soldering techniques and circuit board assembly.

title: Marine Electronics for Maritime Vessels
description: Focuses on theory of installation, service and repair of marine electronic systems. Includes control systems, navigation, RADAR, GPS, HF, VHF, satellite communications, lightning and corrosion protection systems.

title: Direct Current Circuit Laboratory
description: Laboratory practice and experimentation in elementary circuitry using basic electronic instrumentation. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 101 .

title: Alternating Current Circuit Lab
description: Practice in the application of AC concepts: Techniques in using electronic instruments, such as oscilloscopes, digital multimeters, frequency counters, and Z meters. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 102 .

title: Basic Solid-State Laboratory
description: Applications of diodes and transistors in electronic circuits. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 103 .

title: Introduction to Maritime Careers
description: Discover education and career opportunities in the field of maritime technology and apprenticeships. Students will learn about college resources and short and long-term career options. The course will culminate in each student developing a personal educational pathway to reach individual career goals.

title: Computer Applications I
description: Practice in the application of typical data processing operations for solving direct current problems. Prerequisite: Must be taken concurrently with ELECT 101 .

title: Introduction to Digital Logic
description: Introduction to the theory, practices and application of digital electronics. Prerequisite: ELECT 102 .

title: Introduction to Digital Logic Lab
description: Introduction to the theory, practices and application of digital electronics. Theoretical concepts and trouble-shooting techniques are demonstrated through lab experiments. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 165 .

title: Computer Applications II
description: Course helps familiarize the student with the use of personal computers, the school’s computer labs, and using computers to solve electronic-related problems. Prerequisite: Must be taken concurrently with ELECT 102 .

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Basic Electronics Theory & Assessment
description: This course reviews fundamental theory associated with the first year electronics program and assesses students’ preparation for advanced instruction.
prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Solid-State Devices
description: Continuation of solid-state theory, use of approximation techniques in circuit analysis, development of parameters, evaluation of circuit potentials and applications. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 211 .

title: Advanced Solid-State Devices
description: Continuation of analysis in using equivalent circuit concepts. Various types of solid-state components and introduction to analog integrated circuits. Prerequisite: ELECT 201 and concurrent enrollment in ELECT 212 .

title: Special Circuits
description: Solid-state devices/integrated circuits in industry; active filters, phase locked loops, SCRs, Triacs, and other power control semiconductors. Prerequisite: ELECT 202 and concurrent enrollment in ELECT 213 .

title: Solid-State Laboratory
description: Laboratory practice in the construction, analysis, and trouble shooting of bipolar transition circuits.
prerequisites: Completion of first-year core program or equivalent.

title: Advanced Solid-State Circuit Lab
description: Development of and experimentation with transistor amplifiers and analog integrated circuits. Prerequisite: ELECT 201 , ELECT 211 .

title: Special Circuits Laboratory
description: Laboratory practice in analysis and troubleshooting of active filters, phase locked loops, and solid-state power control circuits. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 203 .

title: Advanced Digital Circuits
description: A continuation of basic digital circuits, with emphasis on counters, decoders, and registers. Course also includes an introduction to microprocessors. Prerequisite: ELECT 165 or equivalent.

title: Microcontrollers
description: Digital circuit types used in industry for machine control such as microprocessors and microcomputers.

title: Advanced Microprocessors
description: Theory and applications of interface systems used in the control of microprocessors. Prerequisite: ELECT 225 or equivalent.

title: Advanced Digital Circuits Laboratory
description: A continuation of the basic digital circuits laboratory, with an emphasis on counters, decoders, registers, and an introduction to microcomputers. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 225 .

title: Microcomputer Laboratory
description: Introduction to the use of machine/assembler language programming to control microprocessors for problem solving or A/D and D/A interfacing. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 227 .

title: Advanced Microprocessor Lab
description: This class gives hands-on experience constructing, testing and evaluating a microprocessor control project. Prerequisite: ELECT 225 or equivalent. Concurrent enrollment in ELECT 228 .

title: EMT Fundamentals
description: This course covers the operational and medical knowledge required for Washington State and National Registry Emergency Medical Technician certifications. Content areas align with national EMS education standards.
prerequisites: Admission to the EMT program pathway

title: EMT Skills Lab
description: This course integrates assessment with anatomy and physiology for students to learn, demonstrate, verbalize, and document fundamental EMT skills used during patient treatment.
prerequisites: Admission to the EMT program pathway

title: EMT Clinical
description: This course allows students to implement and evaluate evidence-based interventions aimed at relieving symptoms and promoting overall patient health across the lifespan.
prerequisites: Admission to EMT program pathway

title: Introduction to Engineering
description: Introduction to fields and careers of engineering. How does one become an engineer All engineering majors should take ENGR 100 early in the curriculum. (Pass/No Credit)

title: Engineering Problems
description: Introduces students to engineering problem solving techniques, including using calculators and computers. Students will be introduced to MATLAB as a problem solving tool. Prerequisite: MATH& 142 or MATH 143 with 2.0 or better or co-enrollment in MATH& 142 with instructor permission.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: CAD Applications for Engineering Design
description: Advanced CAD applications for engineering design; surfaces, sheet metal, weldments, molds, multibody parts, advanced assembly modeling, CAD FEA, CFD, motion studies and CAD documentation. Prerequisite: ENGR& 114 and ENGR& 225 each with a grade of 2.0 or above or instructor permission.

title: Design of Logic Circuits
description:
Design and application of combinational logic circuits with exposure to modern methods and design tools; introduction to sequential logic circuits. Formerly N/A Prerequisite: CS& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.
,

title: Applied Numerical Methods for Engineering
description:
Natural Science Numerical solutions to engineering problems using MATLAB programming. Application of mathematical judgment in selecting computational algorithms and communicating results. Prerequisite: MATH& 163 with 2.0 grade or higher required. Co-enrollment in MATH 250 desired. CIS 141 recommended.
,

title: Fundamentals of Materials Science
description: Elementary principles underlying the structure and properties of materials used in engineering practice. Relation of microstructure to physical properties. Formerly ENGR 170 Prerequisite: CHEM& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or higher AND ENGR& 225 with a grade of 2.0 or higher AND co-enrollment in ENGR 271 .

title: Materials Sciences Laboratory
description: Laboratory experience in various material testing and experimental stress analysis methods, engineering data analysis and report writing. Formerly ENGR 171 Prerequisite: ENGR& 225 with 2.0 or higher and co-enrollment in ENGR 270 .

title: Introduction to Design
description: Social Science Revolutionary technologies and how they have shaped the world. Introduction to design and communication principles through engineering project approach. Formerly ENGR 120 ,

title: Engineering Graphics
description: Usage of graphics (sketching and parametric modeling software) in engineering design. Up to two team design projects.

title: Electrical Circuits
description:
Introduction to electrical engineering. Basic circuit and systems concepts. Solution of first and second order linear Differential equations associated with basic circuit forms. Laboratory activities illustrate principles explored in lecture. Formerly ENGR 215 Prerequisite: MATH 221 and PHYS 255 , both with 2.0 grade or higher.
,

title: Statics
description: A study of the forces and loads acting on objects at rest using vector applications. Formerly ENGR 210 Prerequisite: ENGR 111 and MATH& 152 (each with a grade of 2.0 or higher) or ENGR 111 (grade of 2.0 or higher) and co-enrollment in MATH& 152 with instructor permission.

title: Dynamics
description: Studies of motion using vector calculus, central force motion, Newtonian mechanics, energy, and impulse momentum methods. Formerly ENGR 230 Prerequisite: ENGR& 214 and MATH 221 with a grade of 2.0 or higher or ENGR& 214 with a grade of 2.0 or higher and co-enrollment in MATH 221 .

title: Thermodynamics
description: Introduction to energy conservation topics with application to engineering design; including energy transformation and maximum efficiency. Formerly ENGR 260 Prerequisite: MATH& 163 and PHYS 254 both with 2.0 grade or higher or MATH& 163 with 2.0 grade and co-enrollment in PHYS 254 with instructor permission.

title: Mechanics of Materials
description: Introduces the concepts of stress, deformation, and strain in solid materials; design implications are explored. Formerly ENGR 220 Prerequisite: ENGR& 214 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Reading & Writing in Life & College
description: This intro course helps students develop strategies for reading, writing, reflection, and problem solving. Assignments focus on individual and group processes for personal/academic writing.
prerequisites: Assessment test score or instructor permission.

title: Reading/Writing Academic Disciplines
description:
This course develops students’ critical strategies for reading, writing, problem solving, and handling academic life. As an acceleration class with additional lab time, ENGL 099 offers students the opportunity to earn both ENGL 099 and ENGL& 101 credit in one quarter. Prerequisite: Assessment test score, or ENGL 091 with grade of 2.00 or better, or ENGL 092 with grade of 2.00 or better, or instructor permission.
4 ,

title: Literature of Comic Books and Graphic Novels
description: (H/DSJ) A survey of the rhetoric used in graphic novels and comic books with an emphasis on its representations of social issues and their place in history. The texts will include multiple authors who use comics as a medium to tell powerful stories, some of which are a very personal retelling of their own history. We will be look at the superhero genre as it is the dominant narrative used in comic books/graphic novels but will move beyond this genre towards texts that use this medium to provide powerful narratives and ideologies.

title: The Short Story
description: Humanities The nature and development of short fiction.

title: Introduction to African American Literature
description: (H/DSJ) What is the Black literary tradition, and how does it represent the African/Black American experience? This course surveys African American literature with particular attention to historical and contemporary social contexts. Emphasis is placed on literature and perspectives from a wide spectrum of African American and Black authors, leaders, artists, and activists. Texts may include fiction, poetry, nonfiction, music from hymns to hip hop, cultural artifacts from quilts to fashion, food culture, and other forms of storytelling.

title: Diversity in Contemporary Literature
description: (H/DSJ) Through textual and cultural analysis, this course will cover the different ways in which Contemporary Literature, post 1950, represents struggles for social justice. This course explores how some Contemporary Literature depicts resistance against ideological norms. Contemporary Literature aims to understand how diverse authors negotiate power and privilege through personal, local, and/or global literary works. Texts may include novels, short stories, poetry, drama, graphic novels, social media, and pop culture.

title: Special Topics in Literature
description: Humanities An in-depth study of a single author’s work or of selected works by two or more authors related by theme, time period or cultural milieu. This course may be repeated for up to 15 credits.

title: Fantasy Literature
description: Humanities/DSJ From epic hero quests to magical worlds, fantasy represents the power of the imagination. Fantasy’s appeal resides in more than just its whimsical fairies, daring protagonists, and frightening monsters; it offers tools for coping with reality and a way to reimagine society. In this class, students will develop close reading and literary analysis skills while exploring how this enduring genre reflects and influences changing ideas about identity, the human condition, utopian dreams, and more. By confronting, reflecting, and subverting our deepest fears and desires, fantasy imagines alternative ways to live.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: Humanities This course focuses on entry-level creative writing practices. We will study introductory craft elements of three genres, including short fiction and poetry. The course will use a workshop model to draft, share, and revise texts. Prerequisite: ENGL 099 with grade of 2.0 or better, or any College Level English Course.

title: Children’s Literature
description: (H) This course explores the genres, authors, and illustrators of literature for children from birth through age eight. Methods and criteria for evaluation and curriculum use are also explored. Formerly ECED 178

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Science Fiction Literature
description: (H/DSJ) A survey of the literature of science fiction, with an emphasis on the theme of diversity, including the representation of gender, race, social class, sexuality, and culture in the 20th and 21st centuries, as well as the role of diversity in the authorship of science fiction short stories and novels. Authors and works will vary, but may include Shelley, Wells, Bradbury, Asimov, Le Guin, Delaney, Atwood, Butler, and Okorafor. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with Grade of 2.0 or better OR ENGL& 111 with grade of 2.0 or better OR AEGS 101 with grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Asian American Literature
description: (H/DSJ) This course surveys Asian American and Pacific Islander American literature from the mid-20th century to the present, with particular attention to the stylistic elements, thematic concerns, and contexts from which the works emerge. Key questions will be asked. With its definitions and boundaries always changing, what exactly does “Asian American literature” or “Pacific Islander American literature” mean? How do histories of war, empire, and colonialism influence such literature? What insights can be learned about Asian American and Pacific Islander empowerment, community formation, and the intersections between race, gender, sexuality, and other forms of social difference? Texts may include novels, short stories, manifestos, drama, manga, poetry, social media, newspapers, protest art, film, food, and pop culture.

title: Native American Literature
description: (H/DSJ) A survey of a wide spectrum of Native American verbal art, from traditional narratives and song to contemporary poetry, fiction, and film. Emphasis on cultural contexts and continuity.

title: British Literature: 19th Century
description: Humanities A survey of Romantic and Victorian British Literature, covering the approximate years 1800 to 1900. Authors and works vary, but may include Wordsworth, Coleridge, Shelley, Keats, Austen, Brontë, Tennyson, Eliot, Browning, Dickens, Thackeray, and Wilde. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with grade of 2.0 or better OR ENGL& 111 with grade of 2.0 or better.

title: British Literature: 20th and 21st-Century
description: Humanities A survey of modernism, postmodernism, and postcolonialism in British Literature from 1900 to the present day. Authors and works vary, but may include Conrad, Joyce, Woolf, Auden, Achebe, Rhys, Ballard, Smith, and Rushdie. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with grade of 2.0 or better OR ENGL& 111 with grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Creative Writing: Creative Nonfiction
description: Humanities Introduction to the reading, writing, and revising of creative nonfiction. Literary terms and techniques will be presented and applied to original student work and constructive review of original work will provide practical application. This course will include significant critical reading of professional works in the genre. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or better, or ENGL 170 with a grade of 2.0 or better, or instructor permission.

title: Creative Writing: Poetry
description: (H) Introduction to the reading, writing, and revising of poetry. Poetry terms and techniques will be presented and applied to original student work and constructive review of original work will provide practical application. This course will include significant critical reading of professional works in the genre.
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better or ENGL 170 with a 2.0 or better, or Instructor Permission.

title: Creative Writing: Drama
description: Humanities Invention and development of dramatic material: Dialogue, action, stage location, and music. Students and instructor read and critique materials in a workshop setting.

title: Creative Writing: Fiction
description: (H) Introduction to the reading, writing, and revising of fiction. Fiction terms and techniques will be presented and applied to original student work, and constructive review of original work will provide practical application. This course will include significant critical reading of professional works in the genre.
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better or ENGL 170 with a 2.0 or better, or Instructor Permission.

title: Women’s Literature
description: Humanities A study of the distinctive contributions of women to literature.

title: Writing in the Disciplines
description: Theory and practice of writing in various academic disciplines. Prerequisite: Successful completion of ENGL& 102 or ENGL& 235 with a minimum GPA of 2.0.

title: Literature and Medicine
description: Humanities A study of medical themes in literature, exploring Different representations of health, illness, and medical practice in novels, short fiction, and poetry. Literature and Medicine places emphasis on the historical and cultural contexts of both the literary works and their medical subjects, as well as how the methods of literary studies contribute to knowledge in the field of medicine. Authors, time periods, and nations covered may vary, but will typically include texts from the 19th and 20th centuries in Britain and America. Prerequisite: Students must be accepted into a bachelor’s program at Olympic College or have instructor permission. ENGL& 101 or the equivalent with a 2.0 or better.

title: Composition II
description: A continuation of ENGL& 101 with emphasis on argumentation, research, and documentation. Formerly ENGL 102 Prerequisite: Successful completion of ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better or its equivalent.

title: Intro to Literature
description: Humanities A study of major literary forms and methods of interpretation. Formerly ENGL 140

title: Introduction to Fiction: Studies in Literature
description: (H/DSJ) Introduction to methods for close reading and interpreting fiction which may include short stories, novellas, and/or novels. Introduction to diverse authors and how the fictional works are represented, understood, and/or influenced by social, cultural, and historical contexts. Application of social justice and/or diversity theories to fictional texts such as intersectionality, race theory, queer theory, feminist theory, etc.).

title: Intro to Poetry
description: Humanities The course covers a selection of poets writing in English. The nature and development of their poetry and its distinguishing features. Also considers several schools of literary criticism. Formerly ENGL 143 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 .

title: Intro to Shakespeare
description: Humanities Studies in several major dramas and sonnets. Formerly ENGL 269

title: British Literature I
description: Humanities A survey of the development of English Literature from its beginnings through the later middle ages. Formerly ENGL 266

title: British Literature II
description: Humanities Historically, Renaissance through Enlightenment England saw cultural and technological evolution, religious and political revolution, and colonial expansion amidst international tensions. Beginning with the rediscovery of classical texts, the Renaissance ushered in humanism and its emphasis on reason and agency, ideas developed throughout the Enlightenment inspiring revolution. This course explores Europe’s plunge towards modernity through many of England’s writers (such as Marlowe, Shakespeare, Donne, Milton, Behn, Finch, Swift, Leapor, Burney, Equiano, Sancho, etc. who contributed literature during this time span). Engl&227 addresses the spark of change in their works, shifting away from Christian themes and writing in the vernacular, demarking what it meant to be British. Formerly ENGL 267 .

title: Technical Writing
description: Problem-solving strategies, information literacy, and research for professional and technical writing settings. Prerequisite: Successful completion of ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better or its equivalent.

title: American Literature I
description: Humanities, DSJ Colonization, revolution, expansion, war–early America (1600-1865) was a dynamic period filled with exciting new ideas about individual rights and democratic ideals. But what did this new nation think of itself and its promises? This course explores the establishment of an American identity through its literature. We will examine these narratives in the context of their historical periods, their literary movements, their analysis of culture, and their throughlines to today. Formerly ENGL 260
prerequisites: English&101 as a pre-requisite OR co-requisite

title: American Literature II
description: (H/DSJ) After the Civil War, the push and pull of events and beliefs continued to shape America: Reconstruction and Redemption, immigration and migration, industrialization and war, the American Dream and consumer culture. The tension between tradition and modernization permeated 20th and 21st century America. But what did those traditions represent and what does change mean? English&245 is a literary journey through the American landscapes as a variety of authors examine American culture through various literary movements, genres, and lenses. Formerly ENGL 261
prerequisites: English&101 as a pre-requisite OR a co-requisite.

title: Introduction to Environmental Science
description: (NS) Ecological principles, including the interdisciplinary studies of the physical, biological, chemical, and geological principles to understand ecosystems. Focuses on the relationships and connections between humans and their environment, and solutions to environmental problems. Recommended for non-science majors. Community service requirement (12 hours). ,

title: Introduction to the Fashion Industry
description: An in-depth look at the structure and the interrelationships between the consumer and the primary, secondary, and auxiliary market segments within the fashion industry.

title: Visual Merchandising & Promotion
description: A comprehensive study of merchandising which includes: presentation, analysis, experimentation and research of the merchandise presentation environment. Learn the practical application of store image, color, texture and display theories of visual merchandising techniques in the fashion industry. Field trips included. ,

title: History of Fashion
description: An overview of costume history in Western culture from ancient civilizations to the present. Examine cultural, social, and historical events and analyze their effect on the history of costume and apparel, including the influence of historical costume on fashion today.

title: Fashion Styling
description: An overview on the art of styling and how to master it. Gain a unique insight as to what it takes creatively to become a fashion stylist by: understanding how to dress Different body types; identify marketing strategies from a styling perspective; and by developing a final project incorporating style, image and identity using visual and written presentation. ,

title: Store Operations
description: Learn the steps needed to develop a retail operation, how to analyze business situations and apply solutions based on sound management theory, and how to examine the processes involved in maintaining a successful retail establishment. ,

title: Fashion Trends and Forecasting
description: Students will develop an understanding of the process, methods and influence of trend analysis and forecasting for the fashion business used for developing a merchandising plan for a retail business.
prerequisites: None.

title: Event Planning
description: Event planning offers the opportunity to develop and present an idea, product or brand from the original idea, through the public relations process, in order to engage with consumers in new and exciting ways. The final project is the actual event.
prerequisites: None.

title: Merchandising in Fashion Capitols
description: Students, under faculty supervision, visit a major fashion capitol to study the factors involved in fashion marketing, design and the international customer’s attitudes about fashion. Career opportunities research is included. ,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Practicum
description: Course can be Offered as: FILM 199/299. Practical application in the working world of the basic theories studied in dramatic arts.

title: Documentary Filmmaking I
description: In this hands-on course students will engage in the development and pre-production stages of a documentary film project. Students will research and find a great story, developing their idea into a workable idea for their non-fiction film project. They will create a pitch deck and reel to secure the necessary funding for their project. They will create a budget for their documentary film and learn how to pitch to buyers, production companies, investors and grantors. Students will practice the art of archival research to collect the necessary photos, videos, headlines and graphics for their story, how to assemble the right crew, and outline their story as they enter the production phase. Prerequisite: FILM 290 FILM 292 (or FILM 285 ).

title: Documentary Filmmaking II
description: In this hands-on course students will immerse themselves in the production (principal photography) phase of their documentary film project. Students will practice all aspects of documentary production, including but not limited to: The art of the interview (both in terms of shooting and how to conduct the interview itself), proper lighting and composition, scheduling, shooting re-creation and b-roll footage to accompany their story, and how circular workflow will benefit their documentary film project.
prerequisites: Documentary Filmmaking I FILM 200

title: Documentary Filmmaking III
description: In this hands-on course students will immerse themselves in the post-production and distribution phases, applying circular workflow to construct the story of their documentary film project. Students will apply act structure as needed in the documentary form to effectively and emotionally tell their story. Students will use visual effects and find their unique style while assembling their film. Students apply music and sound design to their documentary film project, complete their finished film with sound mixing and color correction. Once the student’s documentary film project is completed, they will explore the various options for distribution of their film in the current industry. Prerequisite: FILM 201 - Documentary FIlmmaking II

title: Acting for the Camera I
description: An applied study of the camera actor’s craft. Topics include feature film, daytime drama and television series performance styles. Formerly DRMA 240

title: Acting for the Camera II
description: An intermediate applied study of the camera actor’s craft. Topics include feature film, daytime drama and television series performance styles. Formerly DRMA 241 Prerequisite: FILM 240 .

title: Acting for the Camera III
description: An advanced applied study of the camera actor’s craft. Topics include feature film, daytime drama and television series performance styles. Formerly DRMA 242 Prerequisite: FILM 241 .

title: Acting for the Camera IV
description: An advanced and professional applied study of the camera actor’s craft. Topics include feature film, daytime drama and television series performance styles. Formerly DRMA 243 Prerequisite: FILM 242 .

title: Screenwriting I
description: This introductory screenwriting class provides students with the tools, models, and techniques to practice the art and craft of screenwriting at the beginning level. Emphasis is placed on recognizing unique storytelling rather than genre-specific story structure. This class is organized as a lecture and workshop, whereby students can feel enthusiastically inspired to share and shape their story in a constructive and creative environment, receiving peer and instructor feedback. Formerly DRMA 245

title: Screenwriting II
description: This intermediate screenwriting class provides students with the tools, models, and techniques to practice the art and craft of screenwriting at a professional level. Emphasis is placed on developing a unique voice and style in story development and scene execution. This class is organized as a lecture and workshop, whereby students can feel enthusiastically inspired to share and shape their story in a constructive and creative environment, receiving peer and instructor feedback. Formerly DRMA 246
prerequisites: FILM 245 or instructor permission.

title: Screenwriting III
description: This advanced screenwriting class provides students with the tools, models, and techniques to practice the art and craft of screenwriting at a professional level. Emphasis is placed on perfecting unique voice and style in story development, expression, and scene execution. This class is organized as a lecture and workshop, whereby students can feel enthusiastically inspired to share and shape their story in a constructive and creative environment, receiving peer and instructor feedback. Formerly DRMA 247
prerequisites: FILM 246 or instructor permission.

title: Screenwriting IV
description: This advanced screenwriting class provides students with the tools to practice the art and craft of screenwriting at a professional level. Emphasis is placed on practicing unique voice and style in relation to genre-specific story structure development and execution. This class is organized as a lecture and intensive workshop, whereby students can feel enthusiastically inspired to share and shape their emerging and/or ongoing narrative work in a constructive and creative environment, receiving both peer and instructor feedback. Formerly DRMA 248
prerequisites: FILM 247 or instructor permission.

title: Screenplay Rewriting
description: Students gain knowledge and hands-on practical experience of rewriting a previously completed draft of a screenplay through guided next steps with the goal of improving the work. Emphasis is placed on peer and instructor critical analyses and addressing substantial constructive notes and feedback to successfully complete a second or successive draft of a screenplay.
prerequisites: FILM 248 or instructor permission. Students must have a completed screenplay in order to enter the class.

title: Film Producing I
description: This class provides students with an overview and practical application relating to the role of producer during the stages of development, pre-production, and production of a film. Emphasis is placed on providing knowledge and understanding about the business of motion pictures, TV and online industry as it relates to independent film producing. Formerly DRMA 270

title: Film Producing II
description: Students will examine the role and responsibilities of the film producer during the stages of post-production and distribution of an independent film. Topics will include effective strategies for crowdfunding and the traditional and emerging distribution platforms, marketing and distributing independent films. Students will develop practical working knowledge by forming production companies and applying knowledge to real-world scenarios. Formerly DRMA 271 Prerequisite: FILM 270 - Film Producing I ,

title: Film Directing
description: Introduces the professional practices and techniques of feature film directing including pre-visualization, storyboarding, film language, staging, lighting, editing, camera angles and framing composition. Formerly DRMA 280

title: Film Directing II
description: Practical application and intermediate techniques of feature film directing including pre-visualization, film language, staging, lighting, camera angles, framing composition and key frame methodology. Formerly DRMA 281

title: Film Production
description: This course is an opportunity for the serious beginner to learn the fundamentals of filmmaking. In this introductory class, students will learn the basics of filmmaking from pre-production through principal photography, post-production, and delivery, fulfilling the roles and responsibilities of crew members on short film projects. Formerly FILM 285 Digital Filmmaking I

title: Post Production
description: This course introduces students to the various industry-standard applications and techniques used in non-linear digital film post-production workflow. Combining lecture, demonstrations, discussion, and hands-on practice in non-linear editing, color correction, and VFX students will then apply this knowledge to pre-existing digital footage. Formerly FILM 286 Digital Filmmaking II
prerequisites: FILM 285 or instructor permission.

title: Cinematography
description: In this hands-on course, students explore theoretical and practical elements of digital cinematography with an emphasis on camera techniques, lighting, and cinematographic language. Theoretical and hands-on technical topics include operation and characteristics of cameras, lenses, accessory camera equipment, lighting, composition, digital compression, and exposure (in-camera tools like histograms, as well as light meters). Formerly FILM 287 - Digital Filmmaking III Prerequisite: FILM 285 or instructor permission.

title: Audio Production & Post Production
description: This hands-on course will introduce students to the technical, aesthetic and artistic concerns that arise with production and field recording/mixing and designing/mixing audio for image-based media including film and video. Instruction centers on the use of professional-quality tools and techniques for selecting microphones for recording and mixing during production, and editing, designing, mixing, and Foley use and creation during post production. Combining lecture and discussion of the technical aspects of sound and acoustics, the course then progresses to their application in real-world situations. Formerly FILM 288 Digital Filmmaking IV Prerequisite: FILM 285 or instructor permission

title: Short Film Workshop
description: In this hands-on collaborative workshop course, students engage fully with the production process by planning and implementing production on a narrative short digital film project. Students receive individualized instruction within their primary crew role(s) and often work independently in this project-based class. Projects considered for this class must already have a fully developed and shot-listed short film screenplay written by themselves in a screenwriting class, or permission to use another’s, in-hand by the first day of the class. Formerly FILM 289 Digital Filmmaking V

title: Editing with DaVinci Resolve
description: This step-by-step course prepares students for the certification test in the latest version of the DaVinci Resolve non-linear editing software. Students will explore hands-on, high-speed editing on the revolutionary new Cut page, which is designed for fast turnaround work, use the traditional Edit page for more sophisticated projects, along with the DaVinci Resolve’s legendary color correction, fusion VFX and fairlight audio tools. Perfect for both new editors and anyone switching to DaVinci Resolve, this course teaches students how to import and organize media, edit and trim video, add transitions, effects, titles and more.
prerequisites: Students must have a good working knowledge of computers, operating systems, using a mouse, keyboard, standard menus and commands. Students must be able to open, save and close applications and files, as well as navigate to other folders.

title: Field Production
description: Students will create high-quality, short film content to support messaging needs for OC entities and/or local organization, preparing students for the real-world experience of working with clients. This course introduces students to industry-standard timelines and workflows as they create media projects. Students will gain actionable experience developing a pitch, preparing a creative deck, and organizing a measurable pre-production plan. Working through crew roles, this class will benefit new and seasoned student filmmakers as they learn, apply, and practice producing actual media content. Ultimately, this course will prepare students for the process of how to pitch and deliver a successful media project.

title: Directing Actors in a Film Performance
description: This course examines highly effective film directing styles and techniques that inspire actors to create real behavior and authentic emotion in an on-camera performance.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Cinematography
description: This course focuses on developing a critical eye, creating an individual approach to cinematography and successful collaboration to create an original, adaptable cinematic look for a digital film. Students will learn advanced camera techniques and movements, the importance and uses of professional lenses, the exclusive and inclusive nature of frame composition, and the impact of lighting on the overall look and feel of a film.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Film Directing
description: In this class, we will analyze in the classroom, and explore hands-on, the elements needed to develop a personal voice and style in filmmaking. Students will combine advanced techniques in script analysis, actor staging and blocking, and carefully designed camera movement as elements of visual style with detailed analysis of subject, theme, and point of view as elements of voice.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Scene Study for Film Actors I
description: This emotionally challenging class explores the actor’s method of creating a personal reality within an imaginary scene that spontaneously generates real behavior and authentic emotion.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Film Producing
description: This class is a survival guide to understanding what it takes to develop a feature film, TV, or new media project from script to screen without sacrificing its creative elements or vision due to budgetary constraints.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Post-Production Techniques
description: Students will use industry-standard applications to develop an advanced understanding of commonly used post-production techniques used in digital filmmaking workflow. Combining analysis and hands-on practice in non-linear editing, color correction, sound design and mixing, students will then apply this knowledge to pre-existing digital footage.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Master Storytelling Workshop
description: The emphasis of this advanced class and workshop is on providing students the opportunity to evolve their unique voice and style in their screenwriting by utilizing a variety of in-depth approaches to plotting, characterization, and scene design. This class explores practices of identifying the central dramatic question, finessing character arcs, tracking reveals, revision process, locking drafts, colored drafts, and shooting scripts.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: History of Film
description: This course is an overview of the history and theory of global film art from the early silent era to our modern age of digital filmmaking.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Psychology of Film
description: This class provides students with an advanced examination of topics, theories and practices relating to psychology and their practical application to visual storytelling and film analysis. Students will gain invaluable insight and a dynamic working knowledge of the process of analyzing conventional films and screenplays, as well as student work in film and/or screenplay formats while developing innovative methods for improvement.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Emerging Technologies in Filmmaking
description: Students will explore the ever-changing landscape of storytelling, evolving production techniques, and post-production workflow for cutting edge technologies such as Virtual Reality (VR) and other interactive media. This class provides an innovative analysis and breakdown of storytelling methods, camera, lighting, directing, and blocking techniques applicable to emerging technologies in digital film, and examines their impact ethically and globally from both creative and business perspectives.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Advanced Scene Study for Film Actors II
description: In this psychologically demanding course students explore advanced scenes furthering the refinement and development of their emotional availability and creative imaginations.
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Production Workshop I
description: This advanced class focuses on the evolution and groundwork of senior projects during Development and Pre-Production in preparation for FILM 440: Production Workshop II. Students will develop a practical application of shepherding projects through pre-writing, writing, colored revisions, constructive feedback, locking, and formulating a shooting draft and script breakdown, preliminary budget, and shooting schedule during pre-production. Formerly FILM 340 Advanced Film Producing
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course.

title: Production Workshop II
description: In this advanced course students, working individually and in groups, will immerse themselves in the pre-production and principal photography stages of making a thesis film project. This class will provide innovative hands-on experience and practical real-world application with outlining key role deliverables, location scouting, budgeting, casting and crewing up, creative financing, principal photography, coverage versus creativity, and other production-related issues. This course operates in a dynamic, flexible and often self-paced working environment, whereby students will address real-world challenges associated with finalizing pre-production, commencing and completing principal photography for a thesis film project. Formerly FILM 440 Production Workshop I
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program acceptance or instructor approval is required to register for this course. Completion of FILM 440 is required to take this course.

title: Production Workshop III
description: In this advanced course students, working individually or in groups, will immerse themselves in the post-production phase of making a thesis project. This class will provide innovative hands-on experience and practical real-world application with outlining key role deliverables, organizing reshoots, editing a thesis project, editing a trailer, performing color correction, and sound editing, design and mixing, producing professional-level deliverables, providing insightful and usable feedback, and other post-production-related duties and issues. This course operates in a dynamic, flexible and often self-paced working environment, whereby students will address real-world challenges associated with finalizing production, and commencing and completing post-production for a thesis project. Formerly FILM 450 Production Workshop 2
prerequisites: BAS in Digital Filmmaking Program Acceptance or Instructor Approval is required to register for this course. Completion of FILM 450 is also required.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: French I
description: Humanities First course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the French-speaking countries. Formerly FLFRN 101

title: French II
description: Humanities Second course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the French-speaking countries. Prerequisite: FRCH& 121 or instructor permission

title: French III
description: Humanities Third course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities, and exploration of the geography and culture of the French-speaking countries. Prerequisite: FRCH& 122 or instructor permission

title: Physical Geography with Lab
description: Natural Science Basic physical elements of the environment and their regional and global distribution. Topics address processes within the atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, biosphere, and cryosphere. Areas of study include seasons, weather, climate, landscape formation, distribution of plants and animals. Includes laboratory and field studies. Formerly GEOG 102
prerequisites: Previous science class recommended.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Social Justice in Geography
description: (SS/DSJ) Analysis of the geographical distribution of power, resources, access, and opportunities on a global, national, and local scale with a focus on cultural geography, population migration, the environment, and economic growth.

title: Earth from Space
description: Natural Science A study of Earth remote sensing: history; instruments; satellites; and data uses including agriculture, forestry, disaster management, geology, archaeology, oceanography and ice. Formerly GEOG 250
prerequisites: A previous science class is recommended.

title: The World of Maps
description: NS - Natural Sciences A study and evaluation of maps centering on their significance to human communication and influence on everyday life. Investigation into their cognitive function, use, history, spatial framework, data collection (instruments, aerial survey, satellites), anatomy, composition, and modern applications.

title: Introduction to Geography
description: (NS/SS) Survey of Geography including cartography and remote sensing, physical geography, human geography, regional geography and Human impact on Earth. Formerly GEOG 101

title: Human Geography: Culture and Places
description: (H/SS) Cultural human geography focusing on geographical concepts, migration, folk and popular culture, language, religion, ethnicity, development and resource issues. (Formerly GEOG 103).
prerequisites: English 99 is recommended.

title: Economic Geography
description: (SS) Economic geography is concerned with the distribution of economic activity, the use of the world’s resources, and the spatial organization and expansion of the world economy.

title: Geography of the Pacific Northwest
description: Social Science A lecture/field course introducing the physical, economic and cultural geography of the Pacific Northwest. Formerly GEOG 120 Prerequisite: ENGL 099 is recommended. Students will need to provide their own transportation to field study sites that are within a maximum radius of 60 miles from the OC Bremerton campus. Field study visits are required once a week.

title: Geologic Hazards
description: Natural Science This course investigates a number of geologic hazards such as earthquakes, tsunami, volcanism, floods, landslides, and coastal hazards. Historic examples are used as case studies.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Survey of Earth Science
description: Natural Science The interplay of the solid Earth, the atmosphere, and the hydrosphere. Global climate change, ozone depletion, and loss of biodiversity are major focal points. Prerequisite: MATH& 107 or equivalent.

title: Intro Physical Geology
description: Natural Science An introduction to Earth’s materials, processes, and landscapes and how they were formed; labs parallel lecture content. Optional field trips. ,

title: Historical Geology
description: Natural Science Topics are geologic history of earth since its formation, plate tectonic theory, organic evolution as interpreted in the fossil record, and the geologic time scale. ,

title: Environmental Geology
description: Natural Science Geologic resources and processes important to Human welfare: Volcanoes, earthquakes, slope stability, rivers and flood management, groundwater, soils, mineral and energy resources. Formerly GEOL 102 ,

title: Geology of the Pacific Northwest
description: Natural Science Understand the geologic origins of the rocks and landscapes in Washington and neighboring parts of the Pacific Northwest. Formerly GEOL 108 ,
