description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: German I
description: Humanities First course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the German-speaking countries.

title: German II
description: Humanities Second course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the German-speaking countries. Prerequisite: GERM& 121 or instructor permission

title: German III
description: Humanities Third course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the German-speaking countries. Prerequisite: GERM& 122 or instructor permission

title: Modern Asia
description: Social Science Survey of common heritage and historical events that crafted Asia: events since 1800 from different perspectives, major societies in the region, interactions among societies and with larger world.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Films in American Culture
description: Humanities/Social Science The history and culture of America as seen in 20th Century American film. Prerequisite: Completion of ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above is strongly recommended.

title: History of U. S. Immigration
description: (SS/DSJ) This course examines immigration to the United States from colonization to the present. We will explore immigration patterns, laws, and debates, while placing them in historical context. We will read primary sources and first-hand accounts to better understand the diversity of immigration processes and experiences. Prerequisite: Completion of ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above is strongly recommended.

title: Western Civilization I
description: Social Science Introduction to development of Western Civilization from its earliest beginnings up to 1300 AD examining the major political, economic, religious, and social trends. Formerly HIST 101

title: Western Civilization II
description: Social Science Introductory course in development of Western Civilization from 1300-1815 AD analyzing major political, religious, economic, and social trends. Formerly HIST 102

title: Western Civilization III
description: Social Science Introductory course in development of Western Civilization from 1815, analyzing the major political, religious, economic, and social trends of this era. Formerly HIST 103

title: World Civilizations I
description: (SS) This course explores the social, economic, political, intellectual, and artistic achievements of world civilizations from pre-history to ca. 700 C.E.

title: World Civilizations II
description: (SS) This course explores the social, economic, political, intellectual, and artistic achievements of world civilizations from the 8th to 18th century.

title: World Civilizations III
description: (SS) This course explores the social, economic, political, intellectual, and artistic achievements of world civilizations from the 18th century to the present.

title: US History 1
description: Social Science Survey of the political, social, economic, and intellectual forces involved in the foundation and development of the U. S. from pre-Columbian America through the Civil War. Formerly HIST 104 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: US History 2
description: Social Science Survey of the political, social, economic, and intellectual forces involved in the development of the United States from Reconstruction to the present. Formerly HIST 105 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Pacific NW History
description: Social Science The Pacific Northwest, from earliest times to the present, with emphasis upon political, economic, social, and cultural developments. Formerly HIST 250 Prerequisite: Completion of ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above is strongly recommended.

title: Women in US History
description: (SS/DSJ) Explores the social, political and economic roles of women, pre-contact to the present. Comparative approach illustrates the variety of experiences among women of diverse races, social and economic classes, and ethnic groups. Formerly HIST 240 Prerequisite: Completion of ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above is strongly recommended.

title: Native American History
description: (SS/DSJ) Explores the histories and experiences of Native American nations from 1600 to the present, with a focus on the impacts of U.S. Indian policy from 1789 onward. Emphasis on diverse perspectives and political struggles of Indigenous peoples, highlighting both the consequences of colonization and the resilience, resistance, and self-determination. Formerly HIST 235 Prerequisite: Completion of ENGL& 101 with a grade of 2.0 or above is strongly recommended.

title: Introduction to Emergency Management
description: Provides the groundwork on which students can build a strong foundation for disaster and emergency management for homeland security in the 21st century. Addresses issues, policies, questions, best practices, and lessons learned through recent years; standards on emergency management and business continuity, and exposure to new and developing theories, practices, and technology in emergency management.
prerequisites: This is a required first course to enter the HSEM degree program. May take other HSEM courses concurrently.

title: Concepts, Policy & Doctrine for Tribal Emergency Management
description: This course provides the groundwork on which Tribal Emergency Management can build a strong foundation of preparedness, response, recovery and mitigation for their communities.
prerequisites: None (HSEM 102 or 105 required as prerequisite or corequisite to other program classes)

title: Basic Incident Command System/National NIMS
description: This course describes the history, features, principles, and organization structure of the Incident Command System (ICS). It also explains the relationship between ICS and the National Incident Management System (NIMS).

title: Tribal Incident Management Systems
description: This course introduces the incident management structures commonly utilized in Indian Country. It explains the relationship between various incident management structures and the National Incident Management System (NIMS) and affords students an opportunity to share with other Tribal Emergency Management professionals structures they have experienced or would like to explore.
prerequisites: HSEM 102 (or 105) prior or concurrent

title: All Hazards Emergency Planning
description: This course is designed to introduce students to developing an effective emergency planning system. This course offers training in the fundamentals of the emergency planning process, including the rationale behind planning. Emphasis will be placed on hazard/risk analysis and planning team development. Other topics, such as Continuity of Operations (COOP), Emergency Support Functions, National Response Plan, Washington State Comprehensive Emergency Management Plan and contingency planning for areas such as Special Needs (Vulnerable Populations) or Animal Sheltering are included. Prerequisite: EM 102 completed or concurrent or program advisor approval.

title: Planning for Tribal Emergency Management
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts, systems, and processes that guide and support effective emergency management planning in Indian Country, including the history and rationale behind planning. Emphasis will be placed on hazard/risk analysis, planning as an integral component of the National Preparedness System (NPS) and Tribal emergency management, and planning with the whole community, including functional and access needs populations.
prerequisites: EM 102 or EM 105 with a 2.0 or greater, or permission of instructor or program chair.

title: Technology in Emergency Management
description: This class provides a detailed overview of the technology used and applied in the field of emergency management. Students will learn how to utilize technology in emergency planning, response, recovery, and mitigation efforts and they will identify key elements that must be in place for technology to enhance the emergency management process. Prerequisite: EM 102 or EM 105 or concurrent enrollment.

title: Public Information
description: This course will prepare students to support emergency management public information operations, including integration with a Joint Information System, use of a Joint Information Center, coordination with on-scene public information officers, use of alert and warning systems, emergency and routine information distribution (including media and social media), and ensuring appropriate messaging for the whole community.
prerequisites: HSEM 102 prior or concurrent

title: Emergency Response Awareness to Terrorism
description: Provides current and relevant information about terrorism, terrorist behavior, homeland security policies and dilemmas, and how to deal effectively with threats and the consequences of attacks. Student will gain insight into the key players involved in emergency management, local and state issues, particularly as they need to interact and work with FEMA and other federal agencies. Course components include identifying terrorism, causes of terrorism, preventing terrorist attacks, responding to terrorism attacks and avoidance in communication and leadership collapse. Prerequisite: EM 102 or EM 105 with a 2.0 or higher.

title: Public Administration
description: This course provides an overview in the structure and issues of public service. Course participants will examine the context of public administration: the political system, the role of federalism, bureaucratic politics and power, and the various theories of administration that guide public managers today. Course components include public administration, personnel, budgeting, decision-making, organizational behavior, leadership, and policy implementation. Lessons will be drawn from the most current applications of public administration today, such as Hurricane Katrina efforts and Homeland Security. Prerequisite: EM 102 completed or concurrent or program advisor approval.

title: Homeland Security Emergency Management Special Topics
description: Special topics will be developed for areas outside the usual course offerings in Emergency Management. Topics developed will focus on a specific current issue or concept in the areas of emergency management. Prerequisite: EM 102 or EM 105 and EM 110 or EM 111 with at least a 2.0 grade or department permission

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Emergency Operations Center
description: This course provides the student with skills and knowledge to manage an Emergency Operations Center (EOC), acquire and control resources, and interface with on-scene responders within Incident Management Systems. Prerequisite: EM 110 and EM 102 with a 2.0 grade or higher, or concurrent enrollment.

title: Cultural Heritage Preservation and Traditional Knowledge
description: This course will explore the importance of protecting, preserving, and restoring both tangible and intangible cultural heritage in the face of disasters and emergencies, and will describe how traditional knowledge can be applied to disaster planning.
prerequisites: EM 105

title: Exercise Design and Evaluation
description: This course provides participants with the knowledge and skills to develop, conduct, evaluate and report effective exercises that test a community’s operations plan and operational response capability. Throughout the course, participants will learn about topics including exercise program management, design and development, evaluation, and improvement planning. It also builds a foundation for subsequent exercise courses, which provide the specifics of the Homeland Security Exercise and Evaluation Program (HSEEP) and the National Standard Exercise Curriculum (NSEC). Prerequisite: EM 102 and EM 120 with 2.0 grade or better or Program Coordinator approval.

title: Tribal Emergency Management - Grants
description: This course will explore the importance and role of grants in Tribal Emergency Management. Students will research various emergency management grants (federal, tribal, state, and local) and their potential impacts on communities predominately populated by indigenous peoples.
prerequisites: HSEM 105

title: Developing and Managing Volunteer Resources
description: This course will focus on methods and procedures for involving affiliated and spontaneous volunteers in emergency management programs, with the goal of maximizing the effectiveness of volunteer resources.
prerequisites: HSEM 102 prior or concurrent

title: Disaster Response and Recovery
description: The purpose of this course is to enable students to understand and think critically about response and recovery operations in the profession of emergency management. Students will utilize problem based learning by analyzing actual disaster events and applying the theories, principals, and practice of response and recovery. In addition, students will learn about the issues faced by special populations and how to address these special needs in natural disaster response and recovery. Prerequisite: EM 102 and EM 120 with grade of 2.0 or better or program coordinator approval.

title: Emergency Management Work-Based Learning
description: Provides students practical experience in emergency management. Students learn to work withing time constraints and are exposed to appropriate workplace behaviors, while applying core skills they have learned in the program. Prerequisite: EM 249 with at least a 2.0 grade and department approval.

title: Professional Development 1
description: This course prepares the student for the Emergency Management AAS-T Work-based Learning experience. Students will learn how to search for jobs and internships, prepare resumes and job applications, prepare for job and internship interviews, and create an appropriate professional portfolio. Course should be taken 2 terms prior to EM 240. Formerly Enrollment in EM AAS-T program with at least 48 credits completed.

title: Homeland Security Law and Policy
description: This course is designed to give the student an overview of various statutes, regulations, constitutional law, and common law associated with Homeland Security Emergency Management. Students will be introduced to the legalities and ethics relevant to organizing for counterterrorism, investigating terrorism and other national security threats, and crisis and consequence management.
prerequisites: HSEM 102 or HSEM 105 or concurrent enrollment.

title: Introduction to Healthcare Emergency Management
description: Provides the groundwork on which students can build a strong foundation for healthcare related disaster and emergency management. Addresses issues, policies, questions, best practices, and lessons learned through recent years; standards on healthcare emergency management and business continuity, and exposure to new and developing theories, practices, and technology in healthcare emergency management.
prerequisites: HSEM 102 or HSEM 105 or concurrent enrollment.

title: Intro to Hospitality Industry
description: Intro to Hospitality is a comprehensive tour of the fascinating and challenging fields of the hospitality industry: travel and tourism, lodging, food service, meetings, conventions and expositions, leisure and recreation.
prerequisites: Instructor signature.

title: Dining Room Supervision
description:
The secret to success in the hospitality industry is SERVICE or more precisely EXCELLENT SERVICE… and the secret to providing excellent service is the training provided to the waitstaff by the supervisor. This course will provide the potential supervisor with the knowledge and skills required to insure EXCELLENT SERVICE. Prerequisite: CULIN 104 : Dining Room Service.
,

title: Elements of Hospitality Management
description: This course offers an overview of the characteristics and attributes of leaders and compares Different leadership styles. The functions of management are detailed and the distinction between leadership and management is made. Prerequisite: Instructor signature, students must have a valid Kitsap County Food Handler’s permit to provide to instructor, taken and passed HMGMT 102 with at least a 2.0 grade.

title: Beverage Management
description: This course covers the fundamental areas of beverage operations: the planning of the bar, bar staffing, legal factors to consider, drink costing, purchasing, receiving and storage, and beverage production methods.
prerequisites: Instructor signature.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Professional Development
description: This course is 10 planned hours and prepares students for a career in healthcare. This class will cover topics including seeking opportunities to improve knowledge, skills, and abilities. Using supervision effectively.

title: Medication Management
description: This class is 15 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in healthcare. This class will cover topics including understanding substance use disorder, pharmacological actions of alcohol and other drugs and assessment and screening tools.

title: Working with Others
description: This course is 10 planned hours and prepares students for a career in healthcare. This class will cover topics including engaging in active and reflective listening, utilizing non- judgmental words and behaviors. Learn skills to communicate with the client’s family and significant others (with client consent). Using “person centered/person first” language. Building positive relationships, the value of communication, and supportive and collaborative teamwork.

title: Motivational Interviewing
description: This course is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in behavioral healthcare. This class will cover topics including showing respect and concern for others through words and actions through the use of strength-based, evidence-based practices.

title: Providing Services
description: This class is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in healthcare. This class will cover topics including collaborating with care team to help implements the plan of care. Monitor client’s condition, behavior, and progress on a continuous basis to document and share with care team.

title: Professional & Ethical Conduct
description: This course is 10 planned hours and prepares students for a career in healthcare. This class will cover topics including fulfilling responsibilities and commitment, practicing ethically, obtaining client consent, maintaining confidentiality and privacy, managing stress, and maintaining personal health.

title: Documentation
description: This course is 10 planned hours and prepares students for a career in the healthcare industry. This class will cover topics that include completing all required documentation based on agency protocols. Producing documentation that is objective, accurate, and legible with correct language, grammar, and spelling. Completing documentation in a timely manner.

title: De-escalation and Crisis Management
description: This class is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. An introduction to the various types of crisis events that Behavioral Health Providers may encounter. The course will offer an introduction of crisis intervention theories and skills and techniques to assist the student in recognizing concepts of crisis management.

title: Cultural Competency and Individualized Care
description: This class is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. An introduction to topics including cultural diversity including people with disabilities and implications for treatment. HIV/AIDS and infectious disease brief risk intervention for people with substance use disorder. Becoming equipped with how to deliver culturally relevant services that as Behavioral Health Providers. The course will offer an introduction of crisis intervention theories and skills and techniques to assist the student in recognizing concepts of crisis management.

title: Nursing Assistant for Behavioral Health Tech. Apprentices
description: Introduction to the role of the nursing assistant in caring for clients in a variety of settings but specifically for Behavioral Health Technician apprentices. This course prepares Behavioral Health Tech. apprentices to perform basic technical, personal care, and restorative skills for care of clients at treatment facilities, behavioral health clinics and institutions.

title: Trauma Informed Care
description: This class is 25 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in healthcare. Students will gain knowledge of impacts of trauma, develop a trauma informed lens to connect clients to services and to better understand the correlation of behavioral health issues and trauma. Please note that this course has been developed to create an awareness about the impact of trauma. Concerns may arise as we begin to explore this topic. Please notify your instructor immediately should you need assistance exploring self care options during or upon completion of this course.

title: SUDP Apprenticeship Program Understanding Substance Use Disorder & Medication Management
description: This class is 60 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. It covers topics including understanding substance use disorder, pharmacological actions of alcohol and other drugs, substance misuse and substance use disorder treatment methods, and addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment’s (CSAT) Addiction Counseling Competencies (TAP 21). Students completing this course will become familiar with medical and pharmacological resources. This class is also relevant to many fields of study from social services to criminal justice and the medical field. The following competencies, listed in WAC 246-811-030 (from a-w), which outlines the minimum education requirements for substance use disorder professional certification, are covered in this class: a. Understanding addiction b. Pharmacological actions of alcohol and other drugs c. Substance abuse and addiction treatment methods

title: SUDP Apprenticeship Ethical Framework for Counseling
description: This course is 40 planned hours and prepares students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. It addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment’s (CSAT) Addiction Counseling Competencies (TAP 21). This class will cover topics including professional and ethical responsibilities. The following competencies, listed in WAC 246-811-030 (from a-w), which outlines the minimum education requirements for chemical dependency professional certification, are covered in this class: e. Cultural diversity including people with disabilities and its implications for treatment g. HIV/AIDS brief risk intervention for the chemically dependent r. CD confidentiality s. Professional and ethical responsibilities

title: SUDP Apprenticeship Program Documentation 1
description: This course is 60 planned hours and will prepare students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. It addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment’s (CSAT) Addiction Counseling Competencies (TAP 21). This class will cover topics including understanding substance use disorder placement, continuing care, and discharge criteria, including ASAM criteria. Substance use disorder clinical evaluation using DSM-5 (screening and referral to include comorbidity). Substance use disorder individual service planning. Documentation to include screening, intake, assessment, individual service plan, clinical reports, clinical progress notes, discharge summaries, and other client related data. The following competencies, listed in WAC 246-811-0303 (from a-w) - which outlines the minimum education requirements for Substance Use Disorder Professional certification -are covered in this class: c. Substance abuse and addiction treatment methods d. Understanding addiction placement, continuing care, discharge criteria, ASAM f. CD clinical evaluation (screening and referral to comorbidity) g. HIV/AIDS brief risk intervention for the chemically dependent h CD treatment planning q. Documentation to include screening, intake, assessment, treatment plan, clinical reports, clinical progress notes, discharge summaries, other client related data.

title: SUDP Apprenticeship Program Documentation 2
description: This course is 60 planned hours and prepares students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. description: This course is 40 planned hours and prepares students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. It addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment’s (CSAT) Addiction Counseling Competencies (TAP 21). This class will cover topics including understanding health equity issues in the delivery of healthcare encounters and the role of cultural competence in the field of healthcare. The following competencies, listed in WAC 246-811-030 (from a-w) - which outlines the minimum education requirements for chemical dependency professional certification - are covered in this class: (c) Substance abuse and addiction treatment methods (g) Cultural diversity including people with disabilities and its implications for treatment. (e) HIV/AIDS brief risk intervention for the chemically dependent

title: SUDP Apprenticeship Program Trauma Informed Care
description: This class is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in substance use disorder treatment services. This class covers topics including describing, selecting and appropriately using strategies from accepted and culturally appropriate models for counseling and addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Center for Substance Abuse Treatment’s (CSAT) Addiction Counseling Competencies (TAP 21). Students will gain knowledge of impacts of trauma, develop a trauma informed lens to connect clients to services and to better understand the correlation of behavioral health issues and trauma. Please note that this course has been developed to create an awareness about the impact of trauma. Concerns may arise as we begin to explore this topic. Please notify your instructor immediately should you need assistance exploring self care options during or upon completion of this course. The following competencies listed in WAC 246-811-030 (from a-w) -which outlines the minimum educational requirements for substance use disorder professional certification are covered in the class: (f)Substance use disorder clinical evaluation (screening and referral to include comorbidity) (o)Developmental Psychology

title: Introduction to Behavioral Healthcare
description: This course introduces students to the field of Behavioral Health. In this course, students gain an understanding of the historical underpinnings of the field. Students are also introduced to the work of the behavioral health provider and the milieu in which client services are provided. An exploration of ethics, values, and self-understanding as these apply to the behavioral health worker also comprises an important element of the course. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 300
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Practicum 2
description: Through supervised clinical experience, the student begins hands-on training in a behavioral health/human service field. Skills focused upon are: intake interviewing, case management, assessment of client/consumer needs and intervention strategies to meet those needs, individual and group counseling, outreach activities, recording procedures, appropriate use of community referral and networking resources, staff relations, and inter-agency communication. One hundred and twenty hours of recorded clinical experience is required. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course.
prerequisites: 1. Admission to BASBH program and specific foundational course work based on area of study.

title: Social & Cultural Diversity in Behavioral Health
description: This course examines how cultural, biological, and social diversity affect behavioral health and healthcare. It presents current theories and practices for working with diverse populations in the behavioral health field and fosters the understanding and application of cultural diversity, cultural humility, self-awareness, social justice, and advocacy. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 320

title: Ethics in Behavioral Healthcare
description: A broad understanding of ethics, legal standards, and professional responsibilities in behavioral health with an emphasis on counseling ethics. Explore behavioral health professionals’ responsibilities to themselves, clients, colleagues, and society. Gain an understanding of ethical standards, ethical decision-making, professional boundaries, confidentiality, and federal and state laws pertaining to specific populations and situations in behavioral healthcare. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 330
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Professional Development
description: This course prepares students for practicum and employment. Activities may include identifying professional goals, identifying relevant positions, self-assessment, application preparation, and preparation for supervision and consultation. Students must complete this course prior to enrolling in BH& 491 Practicum 1 or BH& 471 Capstone Courses. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 340
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Behavioral Neuroscience
description: Building on the study of human biology, this course introduces the structures and processes of the human brain. Designed for non-science majors, the course emphasizes the relationships among biology, emotions, thoughts, and behavior. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 350
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Case Management
description: This course is an overview of case management within the context of healthcare and human services. Students develop observation, problem-solving, documentation, and relationship building skills through the exploration of the case management process which includes client engagement and assessment, care planning, resource acquisition, and care coordination. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 400
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Assessment of Mental Health Disorders
description: This course introduces the skills necessary to screen clients for common behavioral health conditions, conduct comprehensive biopsychosocial assessments, and partner with clients to develop effective plans to achieve client-identified goals. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 420
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Trauma Informed Care
description: This course offers an overview of the various types of trauma and the impact they have on individuals, couples, families, and communities. Students who complete the course will gain the knowledge, skills, and dispositions required by behavioral health professionals to utilize trauma-informed intervention and treatment principles and successfully assist in a time of crisis. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 430
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Family Counseling
description: The purpose of the course is to introduce students to the theory, assessment techniques and intervention strategies used in the practice of family systems counseling. The focus of the course will be on building a theoretical base for understanding and applying family systems counseling strategies and techniques. Didactic and experiential learning will provide students with the opportunity to apply and integrate knowledge and skills gained through reading, lectures and videos. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 440
prerequisites: Admission to BASBH program

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques
description: This course provides a comprehensive survey of the major contemporary theories of counseling, as well as their implications for practice. Core topics such as historical background, key concepts, the counseling process, counseling techniques and procedures, multicultural perspectives, and evaluation are examined for each theory. Students are given the opportunity to conceptualize selected case studies, decide on appropriate interventions, and practice a variety of techniques that are commonly used in counseling practice. Formerly BASBH 450
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Behavioral Healthcare in Primary Care
description: This course is an advanced practice class that builds on the student’s knowledge of behavioral health assessment, treatment planning, documentation, and evaluation. Students will learn advanced applications of this knowledge within an integrated healthcare model of treatment. This course will emphasize practice skills designed to enhance effective communication across disciplines to prepare students for a collaborative healthcare treatment approach. Practice skills learned in class promote consumer engagement, motivation, and empowered decision-making among clients.
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Capstone Project
description: In the capstone course, students synthesize, apply, and extend content learned throughout the Behavioral Health degree program. In consultation with faculty, students will propose, complete, evaluate, and present a project that demonstrates their mastery of a specific component of their Behavioral Health coursework relevant to the field and to their own academic and professional goals. Formerly BASBH 471
prerequisites: Admitted to the BASBH Program.

title: Practicum 1
description: Through supervised clinical experience, the student begins hands-on training in a behavioral health/human service field. Skills focused upon are: intake interviewing, case management, assessment of client/consumer needs and intervention strategies to meet those needs, individual and group counseling, outreach activities, recording procedures, appropriate use of community referral and networking resources, staff relations, and inter-agency communication. One hundred and twenty (120) hours of recorded clinical experience is required. A grade of 2.0 is required to pass this course. Formerly BASBH 470
prerequisites: 1. Admission to BASBH program and specific foundational course work based on area of study.

title: Intro to Addictive Drugs
description: Social Science An introduction to substance abuse and dependence focusing on the dynamics of addiction and its economic, psychological, and pharmacological impacts. Formerly HSSA& 101 Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better.

title: Intro to Human Services
description: (SS/DSJ) A survey of the key concepts and guiding principles in Human services theory and practice. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with 2.0 or better.

title: Diversity, Ethics & the Law
description: Explores the ethical issues of confidentiality, duty to care, duty to warn and other related issues for counselors and therapists. Includes 4 hours of AIDS prevention education. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0.

title: Case Management
description: Assessment, case management, and documentation for Substance Use Disorder Professionals. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0

title: SUDP Individual Counseling
description: Survey of accepted one-on-one counseling modalities, techniques and methods for treating substance use dependency. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0

title: SUDP Group Counseling
description: Survey of accepted group counseling modalities, techniques and methods for treating substance use dependency. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0.

title: Adolescent Addiction Treatment & Prevention
description: Survey of accepted counseling modalities, techniques and methods for assessing and treating chemically dependent adolescents. This course meets topic area U in the WAC 246-811030 (minimum education requirements for a chemical dependency professional credential). Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0

title: Relapse Prevention and Family Counseling
description: Review of the stages of relapse, relapse prevention, the development of refusal skills, and methods and strategies of integrating significant others into the treatment process. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0

title: Suicide Risk Assessment & Management
description: An exploration of the theoretical foundation in suicide risk assessment and management with a special emphasis on epidemiology of US suicide, mental illness and substance abuse, and evidenced-based risk and protective factors. Role play and skill development exercises included. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better.

title: Co-Occurring Disorders
description: An overview of guiding principles and core components of co-occurring disorders treatment using lecture and experiential learning methods. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 and HS 101 with a minimum grade of 2.0, and PSYC& 220 .

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Human Services & SUDP Practicum 1
description: Practicum offers opportunities for students to demonstrate competency in work settings such as Human services agencies and substance use disorder treatment facilities.
prerequisites: Completion of all core requirements for Human Services or Substance Use Disorder Professional Degree Certificate Programs. A grade of 2.0 or higher is required for successful completion of all HS prefix courses.

title: Human Services & SUDP Practicum 2
description: Practicum offers opportunities for students to demonstrate competency in work settings such as Human services agencies and substance use disorder treatment facilities.
prerequisites: Completion of core requirements for Human Services or Substance Use Disorder Professional Degree or Certificate Programs. A grade of 2.0 or higher is required for successful completion of all HS prefix courses. Instructor permission required before enrolling.

title: Cross Cultural Partnerships
description: This class is 50 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in peer support. An introduction to topics including cultural diversity including children, young adults, families, BIPOC, Veterans and people with visible and invisible differing abilities and implications for recovery.

title: Ethics and Boundaries
description: This course is (50) planned hours and prepares students for a career in behavioral health peer support. It addresses ethical considerations and core competency foundations established by Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) and the Washington State Healthcare Authority. This course addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by the Washington State Healthcare Authority. Students completing this course will become familiar with the required ethics and boundaries needed to become a Certified Peer Counselor in Washington State. The following highlighted competencies, listed in WAC 246-341-0724 , which outline the minimum duty requirements for Certified Peer Counseling certification, are covered in this class: 1 Peer Support Behavioral health services provide a wide range of activities to assist an individual in exercising control over their own life and recovery process through: a Developing self-advocacy and natural supports; b Maintenance of community living skills c Promoting socialization; d The practice of peer counselors sharing their own life experiences related to behavioral health disorders to build alliances that enhance the individual’s ability to function. 2.a Ensure peer support counselors are recognized by the health care authority as a “peer counselor” as defined in WAC 246-341-0200 ; 2.b Provide peer support services within the scope of the peer counselor’s training and department of health credential The following categories of competencies will be explored: Category I Engages peers in collaborative and caring relationships Category II Provides support Category III Shares lived experiences of recovery Category IV Personalizes peer support Category V Supports recovery planning Category VI Links to resources, services, and supports Category VII Provides information about skills related to health, wellness, and recovery Category VIII Helps peers to manage crises Category IX Values Good Communication Category X Supports collaboration and teamwork Category XI Promotes leadership and advocacy Category XII Promotes growth and development

title: Documentation
description: This course is (35) planned hours and will prepare students for a career as a Certified Peer Counselor. This course addresses substance use disorder, mental health and co-occurring disorder documentation in the peer support field. It addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), Washington State Healthcare Authority (HCA) and National Association of Peer Supporters (INAPS). This class will cover topics that include describing and identifying documentation basics, practicing documentation, defining peer support in federal and state language, identifying state documentation standards, recognizing and utilization of simple, adaptable documentation formats. This class will also cover how to identify strengths, SMART goals and attending to the golden thread. The student will learn how to document the use of their lived experience within the peer support encounter for Medicaid reimbursable peer support as well as learn how to document in community peer support settings. Peer support behavioral health services provide a wide range of activities to assist an individual in exercising control over their personal recovery journey. WAC 246-341-0724 outlines the scope of requirements for Peer Support (Certified Peer Counseling) and this class will cover the various aspects of documentation as it relates to peer support: a Frequency duration and outcomes b Documentation is a legal document c Exploring the components of an Individual Service Plan and understanding how to change or add to it with or without an approved identified supervisor. d SMART goal creation e Identify state documentation standards and the various accepted forms of documentation f Telling the whole story one progress note at a time

title: Forming Internal and External Partnerships
description: This course is (50) planned hours and prepares students for a career in behavioral health peer support. It addresses overarching concepts of ethics, cultural humility and the importance of forming internal and external partnerships with formal and informal supports. This course addresses transdisciplinary foundations established by the Washington State Healthcare Authority. Students completing this course will become familiar with medical and pharmacological resources. This class is also relevant to many fields of study from social services to criminal justice and the medical field. The following competencies, listed in WAC 246-341-0724 , which outlines the minimum duty requirements for Certified Peer Counseling certification are covered in this class: 1 Peer Support Behavioral health services provide a wide range of activities to assist an individual in exercising control over their own life and recovery process through: a Developing self-advocacy and natural supports; b Maintenance of community living skills c Promoting socialization; d The practice of peer counselors sharing their own life experiences related to behavioral health disorders to build alliances that enhance the individual’s ability to function. 2.a Ensure peer support counselors are recognized by the health care authority as a “peer counselor” as defined in WAC 246-341-0200 ; 2.b Provide peer support services within the scope of the peer counselor’s training and department of health credential

title: Supervision Utilization
description: This course is 15 planned hours and prepares students for a career in behavioral health peer support. This class will cover topics including seeking opportunities to improve knowledge, skills, and abilities, using supervision effectively.

title: Utilizing Lived Experience
description: This class is 10 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in behavioral health peer support. This is an introduction to storytelling and the power of vulnerability in using one’s own story in the workplace. The course will offer an introduction of the history of peer support and explore skills and techniques to assist the student in recognizing concepts of how to ethically use your story in your work as a Certified Peer Counselor.

title: Crisis Intervention and De-escalation
description: This class is 20 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in behavioral health peer support. An introduction to the various types of crisis events that peer support providers may encounter. The course will offer an introduction of crisis intervention theories and skills and techniques to assist the student in recognizing concepts of crisis management.

title: Trauma Informed Perspective
description: This class is 25 planned hours and aims to prepare students for a career in peer support. Students will gain knowledge of impacts of trauma, develop a trauma informed lens to connect clients to services and to better understand the correlation of behavioral health and trauma.

title: Language & Culture of the Middle East
description: Humanities/Social Science An introduction to the language and culture of the Middle East, with special emphasis on Islam.

title: Politics and Literature
description: (H/SS) An examination of the central issues and concepts of politics through the perspective provided by great literature. Included will be the questions of authority, responsibility, freedom, and power.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Introduction to the Art of Film
description: Humanities An introductory study of the narrative, visual and aural elements of film, including the cultural and social forces that create the variety of film styles.

title: Literature and Film
description: Humanities A survey of literary and film techniques and a comparison of verbal and visual languages.

title: Introduction to Western Religions
description: Humanities Introduction to the study of religions, emphasizing the western religious traditions, including Judaism, Christianity and Islam.

title: Introduction to Eastern Religions
description: Humanities A study of the major religions of Asia, emphasizing India, China, and Japan.

title: Pacific Northwest Voices
description: (H/DSJ) This course offers students a chance to explore diversity in our own backyard! We will consider how the interwoven histories of the Salish Sea (the Puget Sound), the growth of Seattle into a world-class city, and the tensions of the environmental and economic forces of the Kitsap Peninsula (including the region’s military significance) inform and underpin fiction, poetry, and non-fiction narratives. Students will explore how narratives of family, community, local history, and even the Pacific Northwest bioregion affect their own narratives of identity and construction(s) of knowledge. A reading-, writing-, and exploration-intensive course, Pacific Northwest Voices includes a field trip component as well as a cumulative class project. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with grade of 2.0 or better or Instructor Approval.

title: Labor and Film
description: Humanities/Social Science Using a combination of labor films and labor history, this course examines the role of unions in the United States and their trajectory of struggle for workers’ rights and welfare.

title: Writers/Writing as Activism
description: (H/DSJ) Writers/Writing as Activism will immerse students in writing and actions to persuade and to enact social change. It will invite students to do in depth research into relevant topics and to choose from a variety of forms – written and oral, and others to be determined (conference, play, etc.) – to create documents that seek to articulate the reasons for social change and what kind of change might be beneficial. Students will also collaborate and present work to the Olympic College and Bremerton-area community.

title: Major Film Directors and Works
description: Humanities A study in-depth of one film director’s style, or selected major works by different directors. This is a writing course.

title: Rock’N Roll: Music and Ideas
description: Humanities A historical overview of the Rock ‘N Roll roots in the post-war world (1945-present), with an emphasis on its role in social/cultural evolution and its contributions to American and world cultures.

title: New Waves in Cinema
description: (H) What defines a “New Wave?” Turn on any streaming platform and audiences are bombarded by content ranging from big studio films to home-made videos. In this class we’ll explore the break-away trends in cinema, categorized by country and region, their influences, their differences, and the similarities that define and unify them as a movement. This course examines the origins of the “New Wave” in 1950s France and the proliferation of new waves in world cinema through present day. Focusing on cinemas from various countries and regions, the course will explore the cultural and technological contexts that generated a rupture in dramatic arts and the studio system, creating the “new independent cinema” and “new media” that classifies the environment in which the majority of today’s cinema is made. Students will examine the influence of digital cinema on the body of work produced under the rubric of “New Cinemas” and trace its development through to the present. Formerly DRMA 291 5 hrs Lecture

title: Women’s and Gender Studies-Advanced
description: (H/SS) This is an interdisciplinary upper divisional introduction to the scholarly field of Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies. It explores gender and sexuality in history, literature, culture, media, and various theoretical perspectives including, but not limited to, Feminist Theory, Queer Theory, and Intersectionality. The course aims to give students a solid intellectual foundation for the further study of gender as it operates in multiple social, cultural, political, and geographic locations. Prerequisite: Students must be accepted into a bachelor’s program at Olympic College or have instructor permission. Students must have completed ENGL& 101 or the equivalent with a 2.0 or better. Students who are not in a bachelor’s program at Olympic College should take AEGS 205 . HUMAN 320 is not a continuation of AEGS 205 .

title: IS Foundations
description: This course forms the cohort and prepares students with the foundational topics used throughout the BAS program. It creates the required learning platform by focusing on four subject areas: SQL, Web, Programming, and Networking/Security. Students will work collaboratively to construct a working website using industry standard programming tools and install secure working windows and linux based servers and workstations.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the BAS program

title: Information Systems Integration
description:
Students work collaboratively using divergent technologies to create a secure, self-hosted, database-driven website as a means to develop and hone skills, showcase incoming talents, and construct a working community for future projects used throughout the BAS program. Portfolio is introduced. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the BAS program and co-enrollment in IS 300 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Scripting for Automation
description:
Students apply scripting languages to automate tasks, including those performed by applications, web pages, operating system shells, and embedded systems. General purpose and popular languages are explored and practiced for creating wrapper programs and custom commands, and performing networking tasks and unified queries. Prerequisite: IS 300 with 2.0 or better or permission of instructor.

title: Leading in the Digital World
description: Students will examine how leadership intersects with digital technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI) and industry-standard, cloud-based systems. The course covers the leveraging of AI tools, the use of automation as a driver for organizational efficiency, and data analysis to inform leadership decisions.
prerequisites: Must be admitted into a BAS program

title: Database & Data Analysis
description:
Plan and design relational databases. Mine and analyze data using Structured Query Language (SQL) with real-world applications. Topics covered include: data modeling, data normalization and integrity, advanced queries, data manipulation, data analytics and functions, and tabular and graphical representation of analysis findings. Overview of data analytics, including issues of privacy and security. An introduction to NoSQL databases is included. Prerequisite: IS 300 with 2.0 or better or permission of instructor.
,

title: Information Assurance I
description: The student will develop and apply knowledge and skill in planning, designing and evaluating the structural components and procedures of organizational security and information assurance.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the BAS program.

title: LAN Administration IV
description:
Students will focus on upper-administrator planning tasks for Windows Server 2008, choose the appropriate Windows Server solution for a design requirement, and perform domain- or forest-wide server administration tasks. Prerequisite: IS 302 with 2.0 or better.
,

title: Project Management I
description: Students learn the main phases, strategies and tools that support effective project management processes. The course covers all the stages of the project life cycle: including selecting projects, project planning, and risk assessment through execution, monitoring and control. Through case studies students have the opportunity to assess and apply best project management practices in the context of real-world scenarios.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the BAS program.

title: IS Reading and Research
description: Independently or in small teams, and mentored by a faculty member, students do intensive and self-directed research that results in an original scholarly paper or other product that can be formally presented. Students set goals and objectives that help form their own professional development strategy, and that clearly define the research project, reflect original research question(s), and deepen technical knowledge in specific area of interest.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS program.

title: Informatics and Analytics
description:
Studies the roles of informatics and analytics in today’s business environment and explores the trends that are related to big data. Uses data to support effective decision-making process in a wide range of business contexts. Topics include research methods in informatics; big data management and analytics; predictive analytics; recognizing data patterns and trends; and information ethics, law and policy. Through the use of case studies, students collaborate to research and present data-driven solutions to real-world problems. Prerequisite: IS 330 with 2.0 or better.
,

title: Information Assurance II
description:
In this course students will explore current issues and advanced topics in network security and digital forensics. Prerequisite: IS 337 with a 2.0 or better.
,

title: Project Management II
description:
Students learn to apply project management strategies to information systems development and implementation projects. Covers computer systems life cycle management: including requirement identification and analysis, proposal evaluation and development, project planning, management and control, cost and risk analysis, project documentation and legal and licensing requirements. Also includes discussions of current IS project management practices and trends and the role of the development team and its internal and external stakeholders and partners. Prerequisite: IS 350 with 2.0 or above.
,

title: Enterprise Systems
description:
Students form work-based teams to apply significant knowledge, skills and abilities in developing an enterprise-level environment, taking on roles as network administrators, software developers, web database designers and project managers. Teams produce professional documentation to include auditable security plans, policies, procedural manuals, network diagrams, and wireframe schematics. Throughout the course, each team works closely with a faculty member to ensure that their project yields high quality results. Prerequisite: IS 346 with a 2.0 or better.
,

title: Senior Internship I
description: This course provides the student with an opportunity to identify, locate, and perform practical and meaningful learning through real-world work experiences. These work experiences will assist the student to become part of a wider IT community and apply learned knowledge, skills, and understanding in their chosen career.

title: Senior Internship II
description: This course is a continuation of IS 480 - Senior internship I and is for those students to continue with their work to identify, locate, and perform practical andmeaningful learning through real-world work experiences. These work experiences will assist the student to become part of a wider IT community and apply their learned knowledge, skills, and understanding in their chosen career.

title: Senior Project I
description: Students work with faculty facilitator on individually-selected advanced-level project or goals that demonstrate mastery of program outcomes and relevant skills. Students will prepare formal written proposals detailing project or goal activities, and will refine their respective portfolios for professional presentation.
prerequisites: Overall program 2. 5 GPA.

title: Senior Project II
description: This course is a continuation of IS 490 - Senior Project I and is for those students who will continue to work with a faculty facilitator on individually selected advanced-level projects or goals that demonstrate mastery of program outcomes and relevant skills. As part of their initial work for IS 490- Senior Project I, students will have previously prepared and have approved their formal written proposals detailing project or goal activities and will present their outcomes during a professional presentation.

title: Internship Preparation
description: Internship Preparation is a course designed to prepare students for applying to internships in their chosen program of study. Students will learn how to search for internships, build their resumé, write job specific cover letters, networking strategies, interview techniques, professional etiquette, and building a strong online presence. Gain confidence and readiness to secure and excel in internship opportunities. Formerly CO-OP 111
prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in INTRN 121, 122, 123, 124 strongly recommended.

title: Orientation to Manufacturing
description: Overview of the manufacturing sector, including career exploration and local manufacturer presentations.

title: Applied Fundamental Skills
description: Manufacturing and trade-related concepts, math skills, language skills, academic success strategies, interpersonal skills, and career planning specific to manufacturing careers.

title: Manufacturing Methodologies
description: An introduction and survey of the concepts used in manufacturing, such as Lean, Green, and Just in Time.

title: Machine Tools/Precision Measurement
description: Precision measurement methods and tools, identification and use of hand and machine tools, and industrial safety practices. ,

title: Machining Operations and Procedures
description:
Identify and practice common machining operations. Develop and expand machining knowledge, skills, and abilities. Perform process planning, quality assurance inspections, and lean manufacturing. Prerequisite: MANU 130 with a grade of 2.0.
,

title: Intro to Computer Numerical Control
description:
Concepts/capabilities of Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machining. Basic programming, speeds, feeds, General & Misc. (G&M) codes, store and edit data. Prerequisite: MANU 130 with Grade 2.0 or better, Concurrent Enrollment in MANU 130 is allowed.
,

title: Advanced Computer Numerical Control
description:
Expand knowledge, skills, and abilities in CNC operations. Identify the applications and capabilities of Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM) software. Begin using and navigating the Mastercam software package. Prerequisite: Introduction to CNC ( MANU 150 ) with a grade of 2.0.
,

title: Computer Aided Manufacturing I
description:
Entry level course for the use of Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM) software as it pertains to the programing and operation of CNC machine tools. Prerequisite: MANU 160 with a grade of 2.0.
,

title: Manufacturing Materials Fundamentals
description:
Survey of materials typically found in aerospace, recreational, automotive, medical, and construction industries, and how their mechanical, physical, and, chemical properties impact design, manufacturing, and performance requirements. Hands on labs will supplement the lectures giving students a broader understanding of materials and how they behave under Different loading (thermal, mechanical, and environmental) conditions. Prerequisite: Successful completion of TEC-D 145 (or equivalent) with a grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Capstone Project
description: Capstone course that allows students to integrate knowledge from previous classes and demonstrate this in a collaborative, team based, multi-discipline project, in which they design and document a product, and test that design through manufacturing when appropriate and a final project report.
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Essential Mathematics
description: Concepts, calculations, and applications of arithmetic; use of a calculator. Formerly MATH 990A
prerequisites: None.

title: Prealgebra
description: Prepares students for study of algebra. Includes signed numbers, variables, linear equations, area and perimeter, the metric system, and applications. Formerly MATH 090B

title: Brief Math Review
description: Topics from MATH 090 , MATH 091 , MATH 094 , or MATH 099 appropriate to student level. (Pass/No Credit)

title: Elementary Algebra
description: First course in the sequence of Elementary Algebra and Intermediate Algebra. Basic algebraic concepts, first-degree equations, polynomials, integer exponents, roots and radicals, word problems. Prerequisite: MATH 091 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Math Orientation
description: Math Orientation course. Overview of the various Olympic College Math Pathways and study skills content. Multiple placement assessments and individualized mathematics content designed to prepare students to reach the next course level. Upon successful completion students may place up to and including first quarter calculus.

title: Essentials of Intermediate Algebra
description: An intermediate algebra course for non-STEM majors. Algebraic expressions, rational and negative exponents, radical and rational expressions and equations, linear and quadratic equations and graphs, proportional reasoning, applications. See MATHI 098 for 3-credit integrated co-requisite offering. Formerly Math 98I
prerequisites: Math 091 within the last six years with a 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score

title: Intermediate Algebra
description: Second course in the sequence of Elementary Algebra and Intermediate Algebra. Understanding functions and function notation; graphing linear and quadratic functions; systems of equations; rational and radical expressions; solving a variety of non-linear equations such as quadratic, rational, and radical equations; rational exponents. A scientific calculator is required. Prerequisite: MATH 094 within the last six years with a 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Applied Math
description: Integrated presentation of topics in arithmetic, algebra and geometry; problem-solving, estimation, use of right triangle relationships; applications of math in practical workplace related problems. Prerequisite: MATH 094 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Applied Trigonometry
description: Plane trigonometry for technical programs including trigonometric functions, inverse functions, right and oblique triangles, radians, identities and graphing trigonometric functions. Prerequisite: MATH 100 or MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Mathematics and the Environment
description: Natural Science Real-life data relating to environmental issues are studied using linear, exponential and power functions, and elementary statistics. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Precalculus I & II
description: Natural Science An accelerated combination of MATH& 141 and MATH& 142 , allowing the better prepared student to complete the precalculus preparation in one quarter rather than two. Topics include polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions, vectors and parametric equations. A graphing calculator is required. Students completing MATH 143 may not receive graduation credit for MATH& 141 and/or MATH& 142 . Formerly MATH& 144
prerequisites: Satisfactory placement test score.

title: Business Algebra
description: Natural Science First in a two-quarter sequence of algebra and calculus focusing on applications in business and economics; functions, including exponential and logarithmic and their graphs; financial formulas, systems of equations, linear programming. Requires use of a graphing calculator. Formerly MATH 156 Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor.
