title: Introduction to Discrete Mathematics
description: Natural Science Basic logic, number theory, sequences and series, induction. Counting: permutations, combinations, probability, and binomial theorem, graphs and trees. Prerequisite: MATH& 142 or MATH 143 with grade of 2.0 or better.

title: Differential Equations I
description: Natural Science A first course in differential equations covering ordinary differential equations and initial value problems. Includes first order equations, second order linear equations, the Laplace transform, and series solutions. Modeling with emphasis on physics and engineering applications. Prerequisite: MATH& 163 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Differential Equations II
description: Natural Science A second course in Differential equations including systems of 1st order linear equations, nonlinear equations, partial Differential equations, Fourier Series, boundary value problems. Prerequisite: MATH 221 and MATH 250 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Linear Algebra
description: Natural Science Systems of linear equations, vector spaces and subspaces, inner product spaces, orthogonality, least squares, determinants, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, linear transformations, and applications including systems of 1st order linear Differential equations and linear operators on the plane. Prerequisite: MATH& 163 with a grade of 2.0 or above, or permission of instructor.

title: Math in Society
description: Natural Science Topics relevant to Liberal Arts majors, including the following: Mathematical Models (Linear and Exponential) as tools for solving real world problems. Probability as a tool for making informed decisions. Basic descriptive statistics as an introduction to statistical thinking. Consumer Mathematics (loans, annuities, etc.) as a life skill. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 or MATH 098 or MATHI 098 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score, or co-enrollment in MATHI 098

title: Mathematics for Elementary Education 1
description: Natural Science First course for elementary teachers. Emphases: math reasoning, problem solving, sets, real number system, number theory. Scientific calculator: fraction ability/statistical operations required. Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Math for Elem Educ 2
description: Natural Science Second in a sequence for elementary teachers. Topics: geometry, probability, statistics. Emphases: representations, concepts, spatial reasoning. Calculator with statistical operations required. Formerly MATH 167 Prerequisite: MATH& 131 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Precalculus I: Algebra
description: NS - Natural Sciences Using numeric, analytic and graphical methods, linear, polynomial, rational, exponential, and logarithmic functions are studied. A graphing calculator is required. Formerly MATH 119 Prerequisite: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.5 or above, or satisfactory placement test score, or co-enrollment in MATHI 099 .

title: Precalculus II: Trigonometry
description: NS - Natural Sciences Using numeric, analytic, and graphical methods, trigonometric functions and polar and parametric equations are studied. (Graphing calculator is required.) Formerly MATH 120 Prerequisite: MATH& 141 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Introduction to Statistics
description: Natural Science Application of statistics in the context of various fields; descriptive statistics, linear correlation and regression, probability, sampling, the Normal Distribution, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing.
prerequisites: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 or MATH 098 or MATHI 098 within the last six (6) years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Business Calculus
description: Natural Science Second in a two-quarter sequence of algebra and calculus focusing on applications in business and economics; Differentiation and integration, optimization, applications; partial derivative. Requires use of a graphing calculator. Formerly MATH 157 Prerequisite: MATH 147 or MATH& 141 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Calculus I
description: Natural Science Functions, limits and continuity; the derivative, definition, rules and applications; implicit Differentiation; antiderivatives; optimization. Formerly MATH 124 Prerequisite: MATH& 142 or MATH 143 within the last 6 years with a grade of 2.0 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Calculus II
description: Natural Science Definite integrals, techniques of integration, numerical approximation, applications of integration, Differential equations: Separable, growth and decay applications. Formerly MATH 125 Prerequisite: MATH& 151 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Calculus 3
description: Natural Science Series, functions of two variables and their graphs, contour diagrams, vector algebra, dot and cross products, multivariable functions, partial differentiation. Prerequisite: MATH& 152 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Calculus 4
description: Natural Science Gradients, directional derivatives, optimization, local and global extrema, multiple integrals, vector fields, line integrals, flux integrals, calculus of vector fields, divergence and curl of a vector field, Stokes’ Theorem, Green’s Theorem, The Divergence Theorem. Formerly MATH 220 Prerequisite: MATH& 163 with a grade of 2.0 or above.

title: Integrated Essentials of Intermediate Algebra
description: Only offered as part of an integrated co-enrollment course option that includes MATH& 107. Algebraic expressions, rational and negative exponents, radical and rational expressions and equations, linear and quadratic equations and graphs, proportional reasoning, applications. Formerly MATH 098 (3-credit version), MATH 098I Prerequisite: MATH 091 with a grade of 3.5 or above, or MATH 094 with a grade of 2.0 or above within the last six (6) years, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Integrated Intermediate Algebra
description: Only offered as part of an integrated co-enrollment course option that includes MATH& 141. Graphing linear and quadratic functions; systems of equations; rational expressions; radical expressions, and rational exponents. A scientific calculator is required. Formerly MATH 099 (3-credit version), MATH 99I Prerequisite: MATH 094 with a grade of 3.5 or above within the last 2 years, or MATH 099 with a grade of 2.0 or above within the last two (2) years, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: AHA Basic Life Support for Healthcare Providers and First Aid
description: This course is designed for healthcare professionals and other personnel who need to know how to perform CPR and first aid basics for the most common first aid emergencies. Included is how to recognize emergencies, call for help, and perform lifesaving skills in a variety of settings. American Heart Association BLS for Providers and Heart Saver First Aid Training certifications awarded upon successful completion.

title: Pathophysiology for Medical Assisting
description: The etiology, symptoms, diagnostic procedures and treatment of common disease systems as they relate to the medical assistant or other ambulatory healthcare employees.
prerequisites: Completion of BIOL& 241 and BIOL& 242 with a 2.0 or higher.

title: Medical Law and Ethics
description: Overview of medical law, ethics and bioethics as related to the ambulatory healthcare setting. Topics include scope of practice, diverse populations, credentialling and confidentiality requirements.

title: Coding/Alternative Health Settings
description: Introduction to specialized billing and coding rules that apply to alternative settings such as dental offices, home health, hospice, long term care and chemical dependency facilities. Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in MEDA 205 .

title: Computers in the Medical Office
description:
Using medical administrative software, students will learn to complete various administrative tasks necessary for working as medical billers and/or coders. Prerequisite: MEDA 120 and BSTEC 110 with a 2. 5 or higher. Accuplacer scores must place student above MATH 094 or students must complete MATH 094 with at least a 2.0.
,

title: Healthcare Customer Service
description: Customer service skills and their application to working with patients and others in a medical facility.

title: Ten-Key Skills
description: Using a web based 10-key program, students will learn to use the numeric keypad with speed and accuracy.

title: Electronic Health Records for Ambulatory Healthcare Settings
description: Introduction to Electronic Health Records (EHR) for Ambulatory Healthcare Settings, including the use of clinical tools to accurately document the patients’ visits in a medical assistant’s role. ,

title: Medical Administrative Procedures
description:
Introduction to medical administrative software, data entry, appointment scheduling, accounts receivable, correspondence, professionalism, and record management in the medical office. Prerequisite: Ability to type 30 wpm with 90% accuracy and concurrent enrollment in MEDA 162 , or completion of MEDA 162 with a minimum grade of 2.5.
,

title: Mathematics and Pharmacology for Medical Assistants
description: Pharmacology and math as used in ambulatory healthcare settings. Dosage calculations, reference ranges, temperature conversions, growth charts and use of the metric system. Overview of drug therapy and theory relative to the medical assistant’s scope of practice.
prerequisites: Placement Test score that places student in Math 099, or completion of Math 094 witha 2.0 or higher, and concurrent enrollment in MEDA 151.

title: Anatomy, Physiology & Pathophysiology I
description: Principles of Anatomy and Physiology, along with the Pathophysiology as it relates to ambulatory healthcare settings. This is the first of two classes of Anatomy, Physiology & Pathophysiology. Body structure, organization, and physiology, effects of aging along with signs, symptoms, etiology, diagnostics, and treatment modalities for diseases will be examined. Prerequisite: Placement test results placing student into ENGL 101 or completion of ENGL 099 /101 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Anatomy, Physiology & Pathophysiology II
description: Principles of Anatomy and Physiology, along with the Pathophysiology as it relates to ambulatory healthcare settings. This is the second of two classes of Anatomy/Physiology & Pathophysiology. Body structure, organization and physiology, effects of aging along with signs, symptoms, etiology, diagnostics, and treatment modalities for diseases will be examined. Basic nutrition concepts will be examined as it relates to the ambulatory healthcare setting. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 130 with a grade of 2. 5 or higher

title: Exam Room Techniques for Medical Assistants
description:
Introduction to basic examination room techniques required for a medical assistant in an ambulatory setting. Patient preparation and skills required for physician assistance with common examinations and procedures, physical environment safety and maintenance of supplies and equipment in the medical office. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 130, MEDA 151, and MEDA 162 with a grade of 2.5 or higher.
,

title: Laboratory Procedures for Medical Assistants
description: Basic Introduction to the clinical laboratory for the ambulatory setting. Phlebotomy techniques will be reviewed. Commonly utilized laboratory and cardiopulmonary testing seen in ambulatory settings, as well as patient preparation for common diagnostic procedures. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 136 and MEDA 168 with a grade of 2.5 or higher. ,

title: Medical Receptionist Skills
description: Oral, written and telephone skills development appropriate to a medical receptionist setting. Emphasis on professional attributes and job search readiness.

title: Medical Receptionist Externship
description: Students are placed in approved medical facilities for a supervised, unpaid front Office experience as final preparation for working as a Medical Receptionist.
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Professional Preparation  for Medical Assistants I
description: Discussion of the personal attributes, work setting, skills and responsibilities of a Medical Assistant. Investigation of the scope of practice defined by state and national regulations. Introduction to time management theory, therapeutic patient interaction techniques and critical thinking skills. This is the trigger course for the Medical Assisting Program. Formerly MEDA Professional Preparation I
prerequisites: Instructor permission

title: Professional Preparation for Medical Assistants II
description: Continued exploration of therapeutic communication in the healthcare setting with an emphasis on patients with mental health disorders. Professional telephone communication in a healthcare setting. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 151 with a minimum grade of 2.5 or higher.

title: Professional Preparation for Medical Assistants III
description: Study of employment opportunities in the Medical Assisting field with emphasis on job search readiness and the professional responsibilities of Medical Assisting. Prerequisite: MEDA 152 with a minimum grade of 2.5 or higher

title: Medical Terminology
description: The roots, suffixes, prefixes, abbreviations, and combining forms used in medical terminology and their application to all body systems. Prerequisite: Accuplacer test scores which place the student into ENGL& 101 or higher. Alternatively, ENGL 099 with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Medical Insurance Billing
description: Introduction to medical insurance, billing and coding for outpatient settings. Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in MEDA 162 .

title: Coding in Outpatient Settings
description: Medical coding for various outpatient settings, including coding from reports and application of coding guidelines for third-party payers. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 162 ; completion of MEDA 205 or concurrent enrollment.

title: Medical Assisting Invasive Procedures
description:
Introduction to invasive procedures necessary for Medical Assistants. Includes concepts of asepsis, venipuncture, skin punctures and injections. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 130 , MEDA 151 , and MEDA 162 with a grade of 2.5 or higher.
,

title: AIDS/HIV/Blood Borne Pathogens
description: Meets WA State requirement for professional license in health occupations and AIDS Omnibus Bill 1988 components for 7 hour education on HIV/AIDS and OSHA Blood Borne Pathogens standard. (Pass/No Credit)

title: Medical Reimbursement Issues for Ambulatory Settings
description: Introduction to billing principles which are key to maximum reimbursement. Focuses on Medicare regulations and how they impact physician payment, auditing of claims’ documentation, analysis of denied claims, and how to appeal claims denial. Current billing practices will be reviewed. Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment or completion of MEDA 163 with a grade of 2.5 or higher.

title: Medical Claims and Coding
description:
Coding for insurance claims, preparing claims forms, obtaining prior authorization, documentation required to support CPT codes, and HCPCS coding. Prerequisite: MEDA 163 with a grade of 2.5 or higher.
,

title: Exit Testing for Medical Assistants
description: Demonstration of entry level skills for Medical Assisting externship and a comprehensive theory examination. Prerequisite: Completion of MEDA 119 , MEDA 123 , MEDA 131 , MEDA 136 , MEDA 152 , MEDA 163 , and MEDA 168 with a grade of 2.5 or higher. Completion with a grade of 2.5 or higher or concurred enrollment in MEDA 112 MEDA 120 MEDA 137 , MEDA 153 MEDA 205 MEDA 209 . ,

title: Medical Office Emergencies
description: Emergency preparedness for the outpatient medical provider including AHA Healthcare Provider CPR and Basic First Aid, infection control and medical asepsis, personal safety precautions, HIV/AIDS and bloodborne pathogens training and disaster preparedness.
prerequisites: Completion of, or concurrent enrollment in MEDA 151 with a 2.5 or higher.

title: Practicum Externship for Medical Assistants
description: Students are placed in approved medical facilities for a supervised, unpaid office experience as a final preparation for employment as a Medical Assistant. Prerequisite: Completion of all required Medical Assisting program courses with a grade of 2.5 or higher, and concurrent enrollment in MEDA 211 , and instructor permission. Clinical documentation completion required prior to the start of the course. 18 hours practicum

title: Human Relations in the Medical Office
description: Discussion, problem-solving and evaluation of the clinical and administrative experiences gained in MEDA 210 . Prerequisite: Concurrent enrollment in MEDA 210

title: Externship for Billing and Coding
description: Students are placed in approved medical facilities for a supervised office experience as a final preparation for working as a Billing/Coding Specialist.
prerequisites: Completion of all required Medical Billing and Coding program courses with a 2.5 or higher, and concurrent enrollment in MEDA214, and instructor permission. Externship documentation completion required prior to the start of the course.

title: Human Relations for Billing/Coding
description: Discussion, problem-solving and evaluation of the experience gained in MEDA 213 . Prerequisite: The student must have completed all other required medical assisting courses with a minimum cumulative grade point average of 2.0 in these courses. All required courses must be taken within the previous three years. Concurrent enrollment in MEDA 213 and instructor permission is required.

title: Introduction to Phlebotomy
description: Basic introduction to the phlebotomy techniques and safety in the laboratory setting. Anatomy and physiology of the circulatory system and customer service in the healthcare setting.
prerequisites: Completion of MEDA 162 with a 2.0 or higher OR concurrent enrolment.

title: Advanced Phlebotomy
description: Further emphasis on theory regarding skills and safety procedures required to successfully perform blood collection techniques. Theory regarding sample processing, various testing methods that require a variety of sample types, advanced sample collection requirements, electrocardiogram procedures and legal and ethical issues impacting phlebotomists.
prerequisites: Completion of PHLEB 101 with a 2.5 or higher; or

title: Phlebotomy Techniques Lab
description: Skill demonstration and practice of phlebotomy procedures completed in various healthcare settings, sample processing, various testing methods that require a variety of sample types, and electrocardiogram procedures.
prerequisites: Completion of PHLEB 101 with a 2.5 or higher; or current Medical Assistant licensure; or Licensed Practical Nurse licensure; or completion of the Medical Billing and Coding Certificate of Specialization AND concurrent enrollment in PHLEB 102.

title: Practicum for Phlebotomy
description: Students are placed in approved medical facilities for a supervised, unpaid laboratory experience as a final preparation for employment as a Phlebotomist.
prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in PHLEB 103

title: Weather and Atmosphere
description: Natural Science Study of atmospheric components, processes, and weather phenomena. Attention to measurement instruments, maps, and satellite images, including those on the Internet. Prerequisite: ENGL 099 and MATH 094 must be passed with a 2.0 or better.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Fundamentals of Music
description: Humanities The study of basic elements of music theory, to include but not limited to notation of music, key signatures, chords, scales, rhythms.

title: History of American Popular Music
description: Humanities Through readings, recordings, video, lecture, and live performances, the genres presented are Spirituals; Minstrelsy; Delta, Classic, and Urban Blues; Tin Pan Alley songs; Broadway Shows; Early New Orleans Combo Jazz; Gospel Music; Folk Music; Swing; Country Music; Bluegrass; Rhythm & Blues (R&B); numerous styles of Rock and Roll; Soul Music; Motown; Popular Vocalists; Jazz-Rock Fusion; EDM; and Hip-Hop.

prerequisites: Audition.

description: Humanities/Skills Performance In this course, students will be introduced to Vocal Theater, including opera, musical theater, operetta, and other relevant theatrical vocal styles. Students will learn about the genres, as well as how to prepare a role and how to audition. The course will culminate with a final performance. Formerly Opera Production ,

description: Humanities/Skills Performance Group instruction in guitar techniques. Music theory and elementary repertoire from various playing styles will be utilized. Student provides own instrument.

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Humanities/Skills Performance Rehearsal and performance of chamber/wind literature from classical through contemporary mediums. Open to all students and community members, based upon ability. ,

title: Voices of Resistance
description: (H/DSJ) “We shall overcome!” This course is a survey of music used in American resistance and protest movements with a focus on 20th and 21st Century movements. Explores the social, cultural, and historical contexts of various notable movements in U.S. history, and examines the respective music and its impact. The course “text” will draw from articles, books, visual media, and of course… music!

title: Music in Film and Television
description: Humanities This course is an exploration of music in film and television. Students will develop and demonstrate methods for analyzing what they hear, leading to the recognition of the unique compositional styles of various film and television music composers. Students will trace the evolution of music in film and television to the present day encompassing Hollywood films, Independent, and International films and respective composers.

title: Introduction to World Music
description: Humanities An exploration of traditional and urban ethnic music of selected cultures of the world.

title: Introduction to Jazz History
description: Humanities A survey of the ethnic sources of jazz and influences on art and pop music of the U. S. and the world.

title: Jazz Musicianship I
description: Humanities Extensive study of the basic elements of jazz music and performance. Jazz scales and their use, chord structure, rhythmic structure, and aural skills will be covered.

title: Jazz Musicianship II
description: Humanities Extensive study of the basic elements of jazz music and performance. Jazz scales and their use, chord structure, rhythmic structure, and aural skills will be covered. Prerequisite: MUSC 239 .

title: Music Appreciation
description: H - Humanities Music Appreciation is a survey and exploration of the role of music throughout history. In this course, students study the many roles that music has played in various cultures and contexts. No prior musical experience is necessary. Open to all students.
prerequisites: none

title: Music Theory I
description: Humanities A thorough overview of the fundamentals of music, pitch, harmony and rhythm. Formerly MUSIC 180

title: Music Theory II
description: Humanities Extensive study of the basic elements of music and performance, chord structure, scales, harmonic analysis, rhythm-meter, and aural skills. Formerly MUSIC 181 Prerequisite: MUSC& 141 .

title: Music Theory III
description: Humanities Extensive study of chromaticism in the diatonic structure of music and performance, chord structure, scales, harmonic analysis, rhythm-meter, and aural skills. Formerly MUSIC 182 Prerequisite: MUSC& 142 .

title: Music Theory IV
description: Humanities Discovery of chromatic harmony in the common practice period through analysis, composition, and performance. Formerly MUSIC 280 Prerequisite: MUSC& 143 .

title: Music Theory V
description: Humanities Discovery of style of the 18th Century baroque through analysis, composition and performance. Formerly MUSIC 281 Prerequisite: MUSC& 143 .

title: Music Theory VI
description: Humanities Discovery of style of the 20th and 21st Century through analysis, composition, and performance. Formerly MUSIC 282 Prerequisite: MUSC& 143 .

title: Research Skills for Evidence Based Nursing
description: Preparing the health-care provider to identify, effectively search for, and appropriately use evidence-based research throughout OC’s nursing program and in preparation for patient care. Formerly This course has been called PubMed/CINAHL Database Search Skills

title: Professional Role Development I
description: Introduction to the professional concepts of nursing including the history of nursing, the role of the student nurse, professional associations, WAC/RCW laws, documentation, and legal issues.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA pathway

title: Professional Role Development II
description: Examines professional nursing concepts including the role of the nurse, inter-disciplinary relationships, and the nursing process.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Nursing Communications
description: An introduction to communication concepts related to the nurse/client relationship, with an emphasis on theories of communication, principles of interviewing, role and responsibility in nursing informatics, and techniques for communicating with diverse clients, populations, and interdisciplinary team members.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Associate in Technical Arts (RN) pathway.

title: Nursing Ethics I
description: DSJ Beginning concepts of ethical reasoning, including the values, principles, on which nurses base ethical decision-making.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Nutrition for Professional Nursing
description: DSJ Healthcare Professional’s role in nutritional assessment, client education, and dietary requirements for wellness and modifications for physical conditions throughout the lifespan. Prerequisite: ENGL 099 , MATH 090 all with a grade of 2.0 or better OR equivalent assessment (Accuplacer) test scores in these areas. Recommended: BIOL& 160 , BIOL& 175 , CHEM& 121 .

title: Clinical Applications Lab I
description: Students learn, demonstrate, verbalize, and document nursing procedures and skills. Successful completion of module includes successful completion of skill performance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Clinical Applications Lab II
description: DSJ This course prepares students to perform the necessary skills for the care of clients in acute and long-term care settings. Same As VLBSN 142
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Physical Assessment
description: The course provides foundational concepts and principles in conducting health assessments of individuals across the lifespan. Proper assessment techniques, critical thinking, and interpretation of findings are emphasized.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA pathway.

title: Nursing Care of the Older Adult
description: Introduces students to the expected progression of growth and development with common age-related changes of the older adult, health promotion, disease prevention, maintenance of common chronic conditions to optimize health, and palliative care. Ethical and legal aspects, the influence of social determinants of health, as well as appropriate communication for a diverse geriatric population will be incorporated.
prerequisites: NURSE 146 - Nursing care of the older adult

title: Dosage Calculations
description: ​​Mathematical computations used for medication administration and intravenous therapy in clinical practice.
prerequisites: MATH 099 or MATHI 099 with a 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology
description: Examines the basics of clinical pharmacology.
prerequisites: BIOL& 241 with 2.0 or higher.

title: Nursing Foundations
description: Introduces nursing students to the conceptual underpinnings for developing a personal and professional nursing theoretical framework, focusing on maximizing the health potential of clients in their environment.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA pathway

title: Clinical Nursing Practice I
description: Students develop professional relationships, critical thinking, and nursing assessment abilities. Emphasis on verbal/written documentation using appropriate medical language/theory.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA pathway

title: Nursing Therapeutics
description: DSJ Introduces concepts for promoting healthy physiological responses in clients. A nursing process framework will be utilized to foster critical thinking in the nursing role. Formerly Clinical Nursing Therapeutics Same As VLBSN 158
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Clinical Nursing Practice II
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of care for clients in long-term care and perioperative settings. This course emphasizes health promotion, safety, and applying the nursing process to patient-centered care of the older adult.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Mental Health Theory
description: Prepares students to care for clients who, as a result of a mental illness, have alterations in mood, personal identity, and coping. Same As VLBSN 172
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathways.

title: Mental Health Clinical
description: Prepares students to care for clients who, as a result of a mental illness, have alterations in mood, personal identity, and coping. Same As VLBSN 174
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathways.

title: Nursing Care of Pediatric Clients
description: Prepares students to care for pediatric clients, focusing on the promotion and maintenance of family health, related to the physical, psychosocial, and emotional development of children. Same As VLBSN 176
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Nursing Care of Pediatric Clients Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of pediatric clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance of family health. Same As VLBSN 177
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Reproductive and Sexual Health
description: Explore physiologic, psychosocial, and pathophysiologic aspects of sexual and reproductive health across the lifespan. Same As VLBSN 178
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Reproductive and Sexual Health - Clinical
description: DSJ Application of physiologic, psychosocial, and pathophysiologic aspects of sexual and reproductive health across the lifespan. Same As VLBSN 179
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Nursing Program.

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing I
description: Prepares students to care for adult medical surgical clients across the continuum of healthcare environments. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient centered care. Covers common geriatric conditions. Same As VLBSN 180
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing I Clinical
description: (DSJ) Provides students with opportunities to apply theoretical concepts learned in NURSE 180 and to utilize the nursing process primarily with adult clients in an acute care setting. Same As VLBSN 181
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Nursing program

title: Chronic Health Problems in the Elderly
description: Links pathophysiological changes related to particular disease entities common in the elderly adult and nursing care involved in facilitating optimal health.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Medical-Surgical and Mental Health Nursing Clinical
description: This clinical course prepares students to care for adult clients primarily in an acute care setting, addressing both medical-surgical conditions and mental health concerns. The focus includes clients experiencing alterations in mood, personal identity, and coping due to mental illness, as well as those managing acute and chronic medical conditions. Students will apply theoretical concepts and utilize the nursing process to provide comprehensive, patient-centered care across both physical and mental health needs.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Nursing Program

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Professional Role Development III
description: This course will examine concepts of leadership and management applicable to the roles of the registered nurse in patient care coordination and decision-making.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Clinical Applications III
description: Prepares students to perform advanced nursing care procedures based on critical analysis of patient needs in the management of clients with diverse therapies involving high-risk treatment modalities and equipment. A nursing process framework is utilized. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to apply critical thinking and theoretical content into practice. Same As VLBSN 202
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA pathway.

title: Nursing Ethics II
description: DSJ In this course, the nursing student will apply ethical theory, concepts, and decision-making processes to client case studies.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Nursing Practice Application
description: DSJ Lab class emphasizing deliberate practice of psychomotor skills while addressing the psycho-social needs of the client (sterile dressings, IV pumps, urinary catheter insertion, nasogastric tubes, tracheostomy suctioning).
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (RN) or ATA (LPN - RN) pathway.

title: Medical Surgical Nursing Theory II
description: Links pathophysiological changes related to disease entities and client care needed to facilitate positive adaptation in the client’s response. The course emphasizes a review of anatomy and physiology, pathophysiology, and management of acute and chronic conditions related to neurology, hematology, nephrology/urology, oncology, and shock. Same As VLBSN 208
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Clinical Nursing Practice III
description: Integration of previous learning and application of theoretical concepts to clinical practice with an emphasis on critical thinking and the nursing process. Same As VLBSN 210
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Professional Role Development Seminar
description: Seminar focuses on topics to aid transition from student to registered nurse role
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway

title: Professional Role Development/Preceptorship
description: Utilizing a preceptorship program, this course prepares students to manage care for clients in the role of a professional nurse in a variety of direct patient care clinical experiences. Formerly Professional Role Development/Mentorship
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Pharmacology Review
description: A review to enhance the student’s clinical nursing practice application of pharmacology.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in ATA (LPN-RN) or ATA (RN) pathway.

title: Preparing for the RN-NCLEX
description: This course provides nursing students with tools that promote success in completing the NCLEX-RN Next Generation licensure exam.
prerequisites: Completion of NURSE 208 and continued enrollment in the ATA/TADN pathways; or completion of VLBSN 208 and continued enrollment in the VLBSN pathway of the Nursing program.

title: Nursing Assistant
description: Introduction to the nursing assistant’s role in providing holistic and person-centered care for clients in various settings. Utilizing the Washington State’s Board of Nursing’s Nursing Assistant Curriculum Framework, this course prepares nursing assistant students to perform basic nursing, personal care, and restorative skills for care of clients across the lifespan. Formerly NA 105 and NA 115
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Nursing Assistant pathway.

title: Nursing Assistant Practicum
description: ​​In this course, students apply knowledge and basic technical, personal care, and restorative skills in the role of a nursing assistant for care of clients in a clinical setting. Prepares students for obtaining certification as a nursing assistant in the state of Washington. (64 hours of clinical experience are completed at an approved care facility, 16 hours of guided lab practice are completed on campus in the skills lab setting.)​ Prerequisite: Course grade of 2.0 in NA 104 . Complete all required health & safety documentation, including passing a DSHS background check, by week three of the quarter. 8 hours practicum

title: Nursing Assistant Practicum: Employed NAR
description: ​​​​In this course, Nursing Assistant Registered (NAR) students hired by an approved care facility apply knowledge and basic technical, personal care, and restorative skills while caring for clients under the supervision of a licensed nurse. Prepares students for obtaining certification as a nursing assistant in the state of Washington. (64 hours of clinical experience are completed at the approved care facility, 16 hours of guided lab practice are completed on campus in the skills lab setting.)​ Prerequisite: Proof of employment at an approved sponsoring facility, NAR credential, and permission of Program Director. Course grade of 2.0 in NA 104 . Complete all required health & safety documentation, including passing a DSHS background check, by week three of the quarter.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Human Nutrition
description: Natural Science Basic principles of nutrition emphasizing the physiological requirements for proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, vitamins, and minerals; their sources; their relationship to metabolism, nutritional status, and common health problems. Prerequisite: ENGL 099 , MATH 090 all with a grade of 2.0 or better OR equivalent assessment (Accuplacer) test scores in these areas. Recommended: BIOL& 160 , BIOL& 175 , CHEM& 121 .

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Intro to Oceanography
description: Natural Science Quantitative and descriptive study of the oceans and their physics, chemistry, geology, and biology. Laboratory includes extensive field work. ,

title: Leading/Managing Tech Prof. and Org.
description: Covers critical leadership issues, developing a leadership orientation, core functions of management; individual and interpersonal effectiveness in view of leading and managing the total enterprise.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program.

title: Workplace & Environmental Safety
description: Fundamentals of workplace/environmental safety management. Course is designed to increase appreciation for workplace safety, expand knowledge of potential hazards, and identify the necessary steps to conduct an environmental audit. Elements of safety management and basics of a safety program.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the OLTM BAS program

title: Business/Leadership-Digital Economy
description: Students explore the impact of digital technologies on business processes to understand their leadership role in organizations and professional-technical fields. Topics will focus on how leadership skills and practices that support the implementation of digital technologies in various business contexts, and how leadership, digital technologies and innovation intersect in the evolving business environment. Students will research and analyze how converging technologies shape business functions.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the BAS program.

title: Business Ethics and Policy
description: Explores contemporary ethics and policy issues in business. Examines fundamental components of an ethical organization. Use of professional codes of ethics and decision-making models. Essential elements of organizational policy.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program.

title: Conflict and Negotiation
description: Explore themes and skills related to conflict and negotiation. Examines nature, sources, and strategies to manage conflict. Basics of interest-based negotiation. Course blends concepts and skills with theory and practical application.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program.

title: Leading High Performance Teams
description: Fundamental concepts, theories, and techniques for guiding teams to achieve organizational outcomes and maximize Human potential. Examine principles of high performance teamwork by analyzing interrelated practices and underlying group dynamics.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program.

title: Quality Management and Process Improvement
description: Quality management and process improvement methods within a technical environment. Explore drivers, components, and challenges of process improvement initiatives.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program.

title: Leading Change
description: Focus on change management within a technical environment. Key leadership challenges, skills, and competencies from effectively planning, leading, executing, and sustaining change in a complex environment. An emphasis on change dynamics and models.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS Program.

title: A Leader’s Introduction to Human Resource Management
description: Students will explore key human resource functions within organizations. Examine current issues, trends, practices, and processes in Human Resource Management. Students will identify how human resources is integral to employee performance management, company culture and overall organizational effectiveness.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the OLTM program

title: Senior Capstone I
description: Students will create an implementation plan for the change project created as a part of the OLTM program requirements. An emphasis will be placed on determining potential barriers, overcoming resistance as well as identifying metrics to determine success or failure of a change management plan. Students will be introduced to the portfolio assignment that will conclude during OLTM 490 (Senior Capstone II).
prerequisites: Acceptance into the OLTM program

title: Senior Capstone II
description: Students will review, analyze and present the outcomes of their change management plan created and implemented as part of the OLTM program requirements. In addition, a leadership portfolio will be created as a culmination of student coursework and experience through the OLTM program.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS OLTM Program and successful completion of OLTM 480.

title: Child Guidance & Development
description: Group discussions, Internet research and instructor guidance, about child growth and development, guidance and discipline, and building family relationships. (Pass/No Credit).

title: Child Development & Parenting
description: Group discussions, Internet research and instructor guidance, about child growth and development, parenting styles and building family relationships.

title: Discipline Strategies for Parents
description: Group discussions, internet research and instructor guidance about positive discipline methods, parenting styles and building family relationships.

title: Strengthen Parent/Child Relationship
description: This course explores the importance of the parent-child relationship and its impact on the healthy growth and development of the child.

title: Parent Education Cooperative Preschools
description: Parents enrolled in a local cooperative preschool will learn about child development and positive guidance techniques by participating in a lab school setting. Formerly ECED 115/116/117 Same As PARED 116 & 117
prerequisites: Child enrolled in OC Parent Child Co-op Preschool or OC Child Care, or permission of instructor.

title: Confidently Parenting Your 2-Year-Old
description: Designed to assist parents of 2-year-olds with support for the early years in order to parent with confidence and build strong family relationships for the future.

title: Confidently Parenting Your 3-Year-Old
description: Designed to assist parents of 3-year-olds with support for the early years in order to parent with confidence and build strong family relationships for the future.

title: Confidently Parenting Your 4-Year-Old
description: Designed to assist parents of 4-year-olds with support for the early years in order to parent with confidence and build strong family relationships for the future.

title: Transitioning to Kindergarten
description: Overview of the kindergarten transitioning process for parents including school readiness and building partnerships with schools.

title: Coparenting Together
description: This course provides parents with resources and information related to co-parenting, parallel parenting and building positive relationships with children within a co-parenting family structure.

title: Skills for Parenting Special Needs Children
description: This course is designed to provide parents and caregivers with the knowledge and practical skills necessary to successfully parent children with special needs. Topics include understanding developmental disabilities, effective communication, emotional support, navigating educational systems and fostering positive family environments.

The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)

title: Intro to Ethics
description: Humanities/Social Science Course will examine the development of moral philosophy, representing a broad range of some of the key ideas and thinkers as they have come to influence moral and ethical choices.

title: Intro to Philosophy
description: Humanities/Social Science Introduction to philosophical questions that deal with rational inquiry in the various areas of philosophical study: metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, religion, and other areas.

title: Critical Thinking
description: Humanities/Social Science Emphasis on analyzing, evaluating, and constructing thought in a clear logical fashion with application to other fields. A non-symbolic approach to logic and critical thinking. Formerly PHIL 115

title: Symbolic Logic
description: Natural Science/Social Science Introduction to symbolic logic with focus on sentence logic with proofs and predicate logic with quantifiers and proofs. Formerly PHIL& 106
prerequisites: Intermediate algebra course work (high school or college) with a grade of 2.0 or higher.

title: Golf
description: Course emphasizes the fundamental skills of golf, proper equipment usage, etiquette, and rules necessary to play golf as a recreational sport.

title: Self Defense
description: Course emphasizes the fundamental skills necessary to defend yourself and/or others in the event you are confronted by an attacker assailant.

title: Karate
description: Emphasizes the fundamental skills of karate and develops an understanding of karate as an art form.

title: Tai Chi
description: This course will be an introduction to the study of Tai Chi focusing on the philosophy and postures of this martial art.

title: Brazilian Jiu Jitsu
description: N/A Covers basic knowledge and skills needed (such as movements, positions, and concepts) in a progressive skill building approach. Emphasizes proper technique, mobility, pressure, and leverage awareness. Intended for those interested in establishing a foundational knowledge of BJJ.
prerequisites: none

title: Racket & Paddle Sports I
description: Learn and apply the fundamental rules and skills of badminton, pickleball, and table tennis.

title: SCUBA Diving
description: Course provides the skills and knowledge of SCUBA diving necessary for safe diving in local or tropical waters. Certification arrangements may be made upon satisfactory course performance.
prerequisites: 16 years of age and pass swimming test.

title: Basketball
description: Emphasizes the fundamental skills of basketball, team strategies of offense and defense and rules necessary to play basketball as a recreational sport.

title: Volleyball
description: Course emphasizes the fundamental skills of volleyball, offenses, defenses, and rules necessary to play volleyball as a recreational sport.

title: Yoga I
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of yoga as a form of exercise, relaxation and improved posture. This course will help provide a foundation for sound physical and emotional health.

title: Yoga II
description: Provides further exploration into the practice of yoga. Additional postures and exercises designed to achieve strength, flexibility, and proper body alignment will be presented.

title: Zumba Fitness
description: Zumba Fitness is a combination of Latin music and dance patterns which create a dynamic and effective fitness program. Routines include interval training of fast and slow rhythms.

title: MixxedFit
description: MixxedFit is a people-inspired dance fitness program that is a mix of explosive dancing and bootcamp toning.

title: Total Body Blast
description: High-intensity interval training utilizing the Tabata protocol of intense exercise followed by short rest. effective speed, agility and flexibility drills to improve your fitness.

title: Fast Fitness
description: Self-paced fitness class incorporating both resistance training and cardiovascular exercise.

title: Jogging
description: Exploration of concepts of improving lifetime aerobic fitness. Students will jog a variety of distances and courses with sufficient stimulus to produce aerobic fitness.

title: Ice Skating I
description: Course for beginner-advanced ice skaters. Instructional emphasis will be placed on building a fundamental understanding of ice skating skills and their application as a lifelong sport. Students will be introduced to basic use and care of equipment and safety implications.

title: Aerobic Walking
description: Self-paced walking class in which students walk a variety of distances to improve aerobic fitness. Course can be offered as: PEFSP 178/ PEFSP 278 .

title: Weight Management and Exercising
description: Learn about the principles of successful weight management including healthy eating behaviors, dietary guidelines, and appropriate exercise choices and technique. ,

title: TRX Suspension Training
description: Explores the concepts of improving strength, flexibility, balance and mobility utilizing the body’s own weight.

title: Introduction to Kettlebells
description: Course emphasizes safe and effective use of kettlebells. Students will be introduced to exercises including the swing, goblet squat and get-up.

title: Weight Training I
description: N/A Introduction to the basic principles of weight training. Students will learn how to use free weights safely and effectively, as well as learn how to incorporate them into a comprehensive workout program.
prerequisites: none

title: Weight Training II
description: Course incorporates principles of both strength and conditioning. Students engage in musculoskeletal and cardiorespiratory activities with the goal of improving overall fitness

title: Athletic Conditioning I
description: Prepares pre-season student athletes competing in intercollegiate sports at Olympic College through cardiovascular and flexibility training.

title: Athletic Conditioning II
description: Prepares student athletes competing in intercollegiate sports at Olympic College through general and sport specific strength development.

title: Athletic Conditioning III
description: Prepares student athletes competing in intercollegiate sports at Olympic College through sport specific movement and speed development.

prerequisites: Permission of instructor.
