title: Weight Training II
description: Course incorporates principles of both strength and conditioning. Students engage in musculoskeletal and cardiorespiratory activities with the goal of improving overall fitness.

title: Introduction to Physical Therapy
description: An introduction to the profession of physical therapy. Content includes: history of physical therapy, professional conduct, role of the PTA, communication skills and psycho-social aspects of practice.
prerequisites: Admission into the Physical Therapist Assistant Program.

title: Medical Terminology For PTA
description: An in-depth introduction to medical terminology. Each body system will be examined individually.

title: Documentation for the PTA
description: Presents issues relating to documentation in physical therapy.
prerequisites: Admission into the Physical Therapist Assistant Program.

title: Current PT Trends & Issues
description: The course will discuss current issues relevant to physical therapy that may be controversial or that may have recently been introduced to the profession.
prerequisites: Passing grade in all previous Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Kinesiology and Functional Anatomy
description: A study of movement emphasizing functional components of the musculoskeletal and neuromuscular systems with a two hour palpation lab.
prerequisites: Admission to the Physical Therapist Assistant Program.

title: Pathology
description: A basis for the understanding of etiology and progression of disease processes. Basic concepts of inflammation followed by a systems-based approach to the body systems.
prerequisites: 2.0 or better in all previous Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Human Growth and Development
description: Examination of normative development across the lifespan from infancy through the aging adult, including the impact of disease/disability on the normative development and function.
prerequisites: Passing grade in all previous Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Orthopedic Conditions
description: An in-depth view of musculoskeletal and orthopedic conditions encountered in physical therapy. Sections on each joint or region and common orthopedic surgical procedures and associated rehabilitation are described.
prerequisites: Passing grade in all previous Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Neuroscience for the PTA
description: The course presents an in-depth view of neurological and neuromuscular conditions encountered in physical therapy. Review and development of functional neuroanatomy and pathology for each condition.
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: PTA Procedures - Basic Skills
description: The course provides an introduction to basic skills utilized in physical therapy. Students will be introduced to the concepts of aseptic technique; wound care; bed mobility and transfer training skills; body mechanic techniques; monitoring of vital signs; client positioning and draping techniques; and treatment area preparation. Laboratory content is presented concurrent to lecture material.
prerequisites: Admission into the Physical Therapist Assistant Program.

title: PTA Procedures: Postural Control and Gait
description: The course provides the student with skills for posture, balance, and gait assessment along with instruction in corrective measures for each. Gait training education including use of assistive and adaptive devices is presented. Functional components relating to ADL’s, self-care and home, community and work environments are addressed at a basic skill level. Laboratory components are presented concurrent to lecture material.
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: PTA Procedures - Orthopedics
description: N/A The course prepares the student to perform assessment and intervention techniques regarding orthopedic and musculoskeletal conditions. Non-operative and post-operative conditions are covered, as well as preventative care. Extensive instruction with lab practice including therapeutic exercise in orthopedics. Formerly PTA 122 - PTA Procedures III - Orthopedic Procedures
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: PTA Procedures - Physical Agents
description: N/A The course presents comprehensive coverage of physical agents used in physical therapy, including thermal agents, hydrotherapy, traction, compression, ultrasound, electrical currents and electromagnetic radiation. Physiological effects, indications and contraindications are defined. Concurrent lab instruction and practice in all physical agents. Formerly PTA Procedures IV - Physical Agents
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: PTA Procedures -Tests and Measures
description: N/A Students will learn to perform data collection and assessment techniques relating to common practice of orthopedic and neurologic conditions. Formerly PTA Procedures VI - Tests and Measures
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior PTA courses.

title: PTA Procedures - Therapeutic Exercise
description: N/A Students will learn fundamental knowledge about exercise theory and technique as well as specific exercises relating to strength, range of motion, flexibility, proprioception and aerobic exercise. Formerly PTA Procedures VII - Therapeutic Exercise
prerequisites: Passing grade in all previous PTA courses

title: Clinical Affiliation I
description: Students will be placed in clinical facilities for 10 weeks; 12 hours per week. 120 total hours of clinical education under the direct supervision of a physical therapist/PTA. Formerly Clinical “Experience” I
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Ethics and Administration
description: The course presents principles related to management structure, ethics and legal aspects within physical therapy including sections on conflict of interest, risk management, liability, reimbursement, and advocacy.
prerequisites: Successful completion of prior PTA courses

title: PTA Procedures - Neuromuscular Rehabilitation
description: N/A The course prepares the student to perform assessment and intervention techniques regarding neuromuscular conditions. Theoretical and practical knowledge regarding intervention for head injury, spinal cord injury, stroke/CVA, Parkinson’s, multiple sclerosis, and cerebral palsy. Specific treatment interventions discussed include sensory stimulation, neurodevelopmental techniques, and proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation. Laboratory components are presented concurrent to lecture material. Formerly PTA Procedures V - Neuromuscular Rehabilitation
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: PTA Procedures - Fundamentals of Rehabilitation
description: N/A The course provides the student with skills for assessing proper wheelchair fitting and instruction in wheelchair mobility; advanced skills relating to ADL’s, self-care and home, community, and work environments; prosthetic, orthotic, and adaptive device management; and cardiopulmonary rehabilitation. Laboratory components are presented concurrent to lecture material. Formerly PTA Procedures VIII - Functional Rehab
prerequisites: Passing grade in all previous PTA courses

title: Clinical Affiliation II
description: This course is the student’s second exposure to clinical education. Students will be placed in clinical facilities for 3 weeks, full-time, with an expected 40-hour work week. 120 total hours of clinical education will be fulfilled under the direct supervision of a physical therapist and/or physical therapist assistant. Formerly PTA 251 - Clinical Experience II
prerequisites: Passing grade in all prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses.

title: Clinical Affiliation III
description: This course is the terminal clinical education experience. Students will be placed in a clinical facility for 8 weeks. 320 total hours of clinical education will be fulfilled under the direct supervision of a physical therapist and/or physical therapist assistant. Students will be expected to be practicing at an entry level status by the end of this clinical rotation. Formerly PTA 252 - Clinical Affiliation II
prerequisites: All prior Physical Therapist Assistant courses with a passing grade and successful passing of lab practical exit exam.

title: Professional Integration Seminar
description: Provides a basis for developing clinical problem solving and critical thinking skills while concurrently enrolled in the final clinical education course. Students will develop skills necessary for licensure application and examination, including taking practice licensure examination, and resume writing/job application skills.
prerequisites: Enrollment in PTA program and successful completion of all previous course work.

title: Everyday Physics
description: (NS) A conceptual overview of physics for the non-science major. The course discusses the principles of nature governing phenomena that we see and experience in our everyday lives. Prerequisite: ENGL 099 and MATH 098 or MATHI 098 (or higher) with a grade of 2.0.

title: Introduction to Physics
description: Natural Science Metric System, Velocity and Acceleration, Vector’s, Newton’s Laws, Work and Energy, Momentum, Rotational Motion. Satisfies lab science requirements for direct transfer degree.
prerequisites: Math 099 (Intermediate Algebra) with a grade of 2.5 or above, or satisfactory placement test score.

title: General Physics I
description:
Natural Science Mechanics: Fundamental quantities, vectors, one and two dimensional motion, statics, Newton’s Laws, gravitation, work and energy, impulse and momentum, and rotational motion. Formerly PHYS& 121/PHYS& 131 Prerequisite: MATH& 141 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: General Physics II
description:
Natural Science Electric fields, forces and potential, direct current and resistance, capacitance and dielectrics, magnetism, induction, electromagnetic waves, reflection, refraction, polarization, and interference phenomena. Formerly PHYS& 122/PHYS& 132 Prerequisite: PHYS 114 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: General Physics III
description:
Natural Science Simple harmonic motion, fluids, sound waves, heat, thermal equilibrium, Ideal Gas Law and laws of thermodynamics, lenses and optical instruments. Quantum, atomic and nuclear physics as time allows. Formerly PHYS& 123/PHYS& 133 Prerequisite: PHYS 114 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Engineering Physics I
description:
Natural Science Mechanics: fundamental units, vectors, statics, laws of kinematics, linear and rotational motion, work, energy, momentum, impulse, equilibrium, inertia, and rocket propulsion. Formerly PHYS& 221/PHYS& 231 Prerequisite: MATH& 163 and PHYS 110 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: Engineering Physics II
description:
Natural Science Electricity and Magnetism: Coulomb’s Law, Gauss’s Law, electric and magnetic fields, capacitors and resistors in circuits, electrical instruments, and Kirchoff’s rules. Formerly PHYS& 222/PHYS& 232 Prerequisite: PHYS 254 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: Engineering Physics III
description:
Natural Science Fluids, Oscillations and Waves; Temperature Scales, Heat Measurements, Thermal Properties of Matter, First and Second Law of Thermodynamics; Light, Reflection, Refraction, Lenses, Mirrors, Image Formation, Interference, Diffraction and Polarization. Formerly PHYS& 223/ PHYS& 233 Prerequisite: PHYS 254 with a grade of 2.0 or above.
,

title: State and Local Government
description: Social Science Political and legal foundations of state and local governments, including legislative functions, distribution and execution of power, administrative organizations, political parties and voter behavior, and state and local policy making.

title: Politics of Middle East
description: Social Science A study of the historical and current politics of the Middle East. We will study the role of war, terror, oil, religion, ethnic cultures and the significance of these dynamics to the world.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Practicum
description: A practical application in the working world of the basic theories studied in the above program or discipline.

title: Environmental Policy, Politics, and Sustainability
description: (SS) This course introduces learners to United States Environmental Policy and politics. It reviews the historical development of Federal environmental policies, institutions, and actors from the 1970s to the present. It analyzes how various institutions and actors, including state and local governments, political parties, non-government interest groups, private corporations, and markets influence environmental policymaking. It prepares learners for future academic and career opportunities in environment-related fields.

title: U. S. Health Care Crisis
description: Social Science Controversies and challenges of U. S. health care including accessibility and costs. Formerly POLS 300 (Same as BNURS 323

title: Intro Political Science
description: Social Science Introduction to the principles and problems of the study of politics and government with focus on history and philosophy and systems used in the field.

title: Intro to Political Theory
description: Humanities/Social Science Introduces major classical and modern thinkers, theories, and ideologies that shape the Western political tradition. Explores how political theory informs and engages contemporary political cultures and institutions and encourages active citizenship. Formerly POL-S 185

title: United States Government
description: Social Science Survey of the origin and functions of the government of the United States. Examines the Articles of Confederation, The Constitution, and the creation and purpose of the federal system. Analyzes the functions of Congress, the Presidency and the Courts. Other topics include political socialization, ideology, interest groups, political parties, the role of the media in politics, and elections.

title: International Relations
description: Social Science This course introduces students to the field of International Relations (IR). It will focus on basic concepts such as nations and nationalism, the nature of the interstate system, the United Nations (UN), power, international conflict and war, and prospects for peaceful conflict resolution. Students will also be introduced to various approaches, theories, and concepts used in the study of International Relations. Formerly POL-S 240

title: Physical Assessment in Nursing
description: This course provides the foundation for the performance of physical assessment, the basis of nursing decisions and actions for the practical nurse.
prerequisites: Admission to the Practical Nursing program. Co-requisite: PNURS 107, PNURS 112, PNURS 114, PNURS 122

title: Practical Nursing Lab II
description: This course prepares practical nursing students to perform nursing skills for care of the med-surg client.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Practical Nursing Skills Lab I
description: This course prepares Practical Nursing students to perform foundational nursing skills for care of clients across the lifespan.
prerequisites: Admission to the Practical Nursing program. Co-requisite: PNURS 101, PNURS 112, PNURS 114, PNURS 122

title: Medical Terminology
description: An introduction to word roots, combining forms, suffixes, prefixes, spelling and pronunciation guidelines using a body systems approach.

title: Foundations of Practical Nursing
description: Introduction to the role of the practical nursing student and practical nurse. Prerequisite: Admission into the Practical Nursing Program and concurrent enrollment in, or completion of PNURS 101 , PNURS 107 , PNURS 114 and PNURS 122

title: Fundamentals I: Nursing Care of the Older Adult
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to basic nursing care across the lifespan. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care. Prepares students to care for clients in the long-term care setting.
prerequisites: Admission into the Practical Nursing Program.

title: Fundamentals II: Medical-Surgical Nursing I
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to nursing care of the medical-surgical client. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care.Prepares students to care for clients in the medical-surgical care setting
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Long-Term Care Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of long-term care clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance. Prerequisite: Admission to Practical Nursing Program and concurrent enrollment in, or completion of PNURS 101 , PNURS 107 , PNURS 112 , and PNURS 114 .

title: Medical-Surgical Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of medical-surgical care clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Transition to PN Theory
description: Introduction of military medics and corpsmen to the role of the Practical Nurse (PN), focusing on patient-centered care across the lifespan and incorporating social determinants of health.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Medic-Corpsman PN Bridge pathway

title: Transition to PN Lab
description: This course prepares military medics and corpsmen to perform foundational and medical-surgical nursing skills for care of clients across the lifespan.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Medic/Corpsman PN Bridge pathway

title: Practical Nurse Role Development
description: This course prepares Practical Nursing students to enter the workforce and to perform delegation and supervisory nursing skills for clients across the lifespan.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Fundamentals III: Mental Health
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to mental health care across the lifespan. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care. Prepares students to care for clients with mental health disorders
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing Program

title: Fundamentals III: Pediatrics
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to nursing care of the pediatric client. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care. Prepares students to care for pediatric clients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Fundamentals III: Reproductive Health
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to nursing care of the reproductive health care client and neonate. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care. Prepares students to care for reproductive health care clients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Fundamentals IV: Medical-Surgical Nursing II
description: Introduction to nursing concepts and theories related to care of the complex medical-surgical client. Builds clinical reasoning by using the nursing process and integrating pathophysiology into patient-centered care.Prepares students to care for clients with complex medical-surgical disorders.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Pediatric Clinical
description: ​​Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of pediatric clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance.​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing (PN or CM-PN) pathway and concurrent enrollment in PNURS 204.

title: Reproductive Health Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of reproductive healthcare clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance. Formerly PNURS 208 - Pediatric/Reproductive Health Clinical
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Practical Nursing (PN or CM-PN) pathway and concurrent enrollment in PNURS 205.

title: Mental Health Clinical Experience
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of mental health care clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing program.

title: Advanced Medical Surgical Clinical
description: Application of knowledge regarding physiologic, pathophysiologic, and psychosocial aspects of complex medical-surgical care clients, focusing on promotion and maintenance. Formerly PNURSE 210 - Clinical Capstone
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nursing pathway.

title: Professional Role Development/Preceptorship
description: ​​Utilizing a preceptorship program, this course prepares students to manage care for clients in the role of a professional nurse in a variety of direct patient care clinical experiences. ​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Practical Nurse pathway

title: Psychology of Adjustment
description: Social Science Systematic presentation of scientific psychological principles and procedures for the resolution of Human problems and relationships.

title: Psychology in Film
description: (SS) In contemporary culture, movies play a significant role in influencing our understanding of ourselves, those around us, and the interpersonal culture we live in. This introductory psychology class provides students with an exploration of topics, theories, and practices relating to the depiction of psychology in narrative film and its application to self-development. Emphasis is placed on examining how motion pictures depict psychologically relevant issues such as the portrayal of disorders and mental illness, human behavior, social identity, gender, the male gaze, archetypes, etc. Students gain critical insight and working knowledge of analyzing conventional films in terms of theme, characterization, and plot and their psychological impact on the audience. The modality used in the approach to subject matter in this class is Postmodern Narrative Therapy.
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 and FILM 245 encouraged but not required.

title: Introduction to Community Psychology
description: (SS/DSJ) Community psychology integrates social, cultural, economic, political, environmental and international influences to promote positive well-being, increase empowerment, and prevent the development of systemic problems within communities, groups, and individuals. Community psychologists view social problems as a result of an unequal distribution of resources, which can result in issues like poverty, homelessness, substance abuse and crime. As a result of this worldview, their theories are based on core values and principles that involve a commitment to social justice for marginalized groups, creating a more equitable distribution of resources across groups, and an appreciation for diverse world views.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Biopsychology
description: (Social Science, Natural Science) Biopsychology is focused on the structure and function of the nervous system. The fundamental principles of neuroscience will be the starting point of this course, offering the foundation for understanding and discussing complex psychophysiological inquiries. This course will cover the perspectives, questions, and techniques of brain cell structure and function, neural communication, neurotransmitters, neurological disorders and treatment, brain development, and neural systems responsible for motivation, learning, emotion, stress, sleep, sensation, perception, and consciousness. Formerly PSYCH 220, PSYC 240 Prerequisite: PSYC& 100 with a grade of 2.0 or better. BIOL& 160 or BIOL&175 are highly recommended

title: Criminal Psychology
description: Social Sciences This course will introduce students to the principles and theories of delinquent and criminal behavior from a developmental, cognitive-behavioral, and biological perspective. The course will also examine the risk and protective factors that are believed to influence delinquency and crime in both juvenile and adult offenders.
prerequisites: Successful completion of ENGL&101 with a 2.0 or higher, or its equivalent.

title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
description: Social Science An introduction to professional issues in clinical and counseling psychology and major therapeutic techniques used in the professions. Prerequisite: Either PSYC& 100 or SOC& 101 or instructor’s approval.

title: Industrial/Organizational Psychology
description: Social Science Industrial and Organizational Psychology studies the psychology of people in organizations and the workplace. The focus is on applying the principles derived from the study of Human behavior and applying this knowledge to the solution of problems at work. Areas of focus will include: Job/task analysis, recruitment, selection, performance management, talent development, teamwork/group dynamics, organization design, change management, and culture development.
prerequisites: Acceptance into BAS program or permission of instructor

title: General Psychology
description: Social Science Survey of basic topics in psychology including methods, biological basis of behavior, sensation, perceptions, learning, memory, motivation, emotion, and clinical psychology. Formerly PSYCH 101
prerequisites: ENGL& 101 recommended.

title: Lifespan Psychology
description: Social Science Introduces the development of different stages in physical, cognitive, personality, and socio-emotional changes over the life span. Formerly PSYCH 224
prerequisites: PSYC& 100 recommended.

title: Psychological Disorders
description: Social Science A survey of psychopathology, specifically a study of abnormal Human behavior, its description and explanation from several theoretical perspectives and an overview of therapies to modify abnormal behavior. Formerly PSYC& 220 - Abnormal Psychology

title: Introduction to Radiologic Technology
description: ​​This course is an introduction radiologic technology. This class explores not only the practical aspects but also the historical, ethical, and legal dimensions of the field.​
prerequisites: Acceptance into the Radiologic Technology Program

title: Radiologic Patient Care
description: ​​This course covers key elements essential for professionalization in radiologic technology. Through a combination of lectures and hands-on lab experiences, students gain proficiency in safe techniques applicable across diverse environments. Topics such as patient evaluation and infection control practices are thoroughly explored, equipping students with the knowledge and skills necessary to excel in their practice while prioritizing patient safety and well-being.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the Radiologic Technology Program

title: Positioning I
description: ​​This course introduces students to radiographic technique, thoroughly examining the process of evaluating procedures. The focus is on clinical communication skills essential for effective interaction with patients. Students are instructed in precise positioning techniques tailored for commonly encountered examinations.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the Radiologic Technology Program

title: Positioning II
description: This course extends further into radiographic technique, the evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, clinical communication with patients, and the instruction of positioning techniques for commonly performed examinations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology Program

title: Positioning III
description: This course delves deeper into radiographic technique, the evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, clinical communication with patients, and the instruction of positioning techniques for commonly performed examinations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology Program

title: Positioning Lab I
description: This course extends further into radiographic technique, the evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, clinical communication with patients, and the instruction of positioning techniques for commonly performed examinations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Positioning Lab II
description: ​​In this course students will learn to assess radiographic images to determine if they meet appearance standards and to correct those that do not. Through analysis of images, students gain an understanding of the technical, procedural, and clinical factors that influence image appearance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology proram

title: Positioning Lab III
description: In this course students develop skills in assessing radiographic images to determine adherence to appearance standards and correcting those that fall short. Through comprehensive exploration, students gain insight into the technical, procedural, and clinical factors influencing image appearance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Image Analysis I
description: In this course students assess radiographic images to determine if they meet appearance standards and to correct those that do not. Through analysis of images, students gain an understanding of the technical, procedural, and clinical factors that influence image appearance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Image Analysis II
description: In this course students develop skills in assessing radiographic images to determine adherence to appearance standards and correcting those that fall short.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology Program

title: Image Analysis III
description: ​​In this course students further develop their ability to assess radiographic images, identifying those that meet appearance standards and learning techniques to correct those that do not. Throughout the course, students gain knowledge of the technical, procedural, and clinical factors that influence image appearance.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Additional Concentrations
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of different imaging concentrations, emphasizing the key concepts and educational prerequisites for each specialty.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical I
description: This initial competency-based clinical course integrates skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical II
description: This is a continuation of competency-based clinical courses integrating skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical III
description: This is the third competency-based clinical course integrating skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical IV
description: Clinical experience in radiologic technology provides a structured pathway for individuals to gain practical experience, develop essential skills, such as positioning, and explore career opportunities in the dynamic field of medical imaging and radiology.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Radiation Physics
description: ​​This course examines the fundamental concepts of radiation, teaching the principles behind x-ray production, x-ray circuitry, fluoroscopy, and quality control measures.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Image Production
description: This course provides a detailed exploration of exposure factors, image acquisition techniques, and computer processes in radiologic technology.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Cross-Sectional Anatomy
description: ​​This course demonstrates techniques for identifying anatomical structures on CT, MRI, and ultrasound scans across various planes. Students explore the diverse anatomical structures of the human body, their spatial relationships, and appearances across different imaging modalities.​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Positioning IV
description: ​​This course covers positioning techniques tailored for less commonly encountered examinations. Radiographic technique, evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, and effective clinical communication are emphasized.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Positioning V
description: This course focuses on positioning techniques tailored specifically for trauma examinations.​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Positioning Lab IV
description: ​​This course extends knowledge of radiographic technique, evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, clinical communication, and positioning techniques for uncommon performed examinations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Positioning Lab V
description: This course expands on radiographic technique, the evaluation of radiographic orders and procedures, clinical communication with patients, and the instruction of positioning techniques for uncommonly performed examinations.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical V
description: This is the fifth competency-based clinical course integrating skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Clinical VI
description: This is the sixth competency-based clinical course integrating skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Clinical VII
description: This is the seventh competency-based clinical course integrating skills to diagnostically image patients.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Radiation Biology
description: ​​This course explores cellular biology and the effects of radiation on living organisms. Techniques to assess radiation exposure and effective radiation protection measures are emphasized.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program

title: Radiographic Pathology
description: In this course, students will become acquainted with essential medical terminology and the underlying causes of various diseases. They will explore radiologic pathology, gaining insight into how different pathologies manifest in radiographic images. Additionally, students will learn about radiographic procedures tailored to diagnose and assess different pathological conditions.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Radiologic Pharmacology
description: This course examines the basics of raiologic pharmacology.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Registry Review
description: This course will concentrate on preparing students for the American Registry of Radiologic Technologist (ARRT) exam.​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology program.

title: Professional Development
description: Focuses on transition from student to ARRT-registered radiologic technologist role.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Radiologic Technology Program.

title: Introduction to Science
description: Natural Science Overview of sciences taught at Olympic College. Scientific methodology through inquiry, observation, experiment, and communication of science concepts in chemistry, biology, physics, and others. No prior science background required. Prerequisite: MATH 094 and ENGL 099 or equivalent placement scores.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Crime and Society
description: (SS/DSJ) This introductory course provides students with a foundational understanding of the American criminal justice system. Students will learn about the empirical reality of crime as well as the primary actors, institutions and processes involved in the criminal justice system. Crime and reactions to crime will be examined as part of the social and institutional life in contemporary American society. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with Grade of 2. 4 or better is recommended.

title: Sociology of Aging
description: (SS/DSJ) An introductory course on aging focusing particularly on the social and emotional dimensions of the aging process.

title: The Family
description: (SS/DSJ) This course covers the institution of the family and other intimate relationships. Emphasis is placed on mate selection, gender roles, sexuality, communication, power and conflict, parenthood, diverse lifestyles, divorce and remarriage, and economic issues. Upon completion, students should be able to analyze the family as a social institution and the social forces which influence its development and change. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: U. S. Race & Ethnicity
description: (SS/DSJ) An examination of America’s diverse ethnic and cultural traditions, with an emphasis on global and comparative perspectives. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Criminology
description: SS - Social Sciences Why do people commit crimes? How should the United States deal with criminal activities? This course is designed to give students an opportunity to critically think, read, and write about the sociology of crime. This course covers the history and theory of what causes or contributes to crime, covering the major issues that confront the criminal justice system and studying the social organizations that create and enforce definitions of deviance and crime. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Sexuality and Gender
description: (SS/DSJ) This course provides an introduction to the theoretical and empirical literature on the sociology of sex and gender. The readings cover theory and methods, feminism, masculinity, intersectionality, international/comparative perspectives, gender roles, and recent sociological research in this area. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Social Deviance
description: SS - Social Sciences In this course students will focus on the sociological analysis of deviance in American society, with a specific emphasis on social power. Through the use of empirical research, students will explore competing definitions of deviance, the development and consequence of deviant identities and careers, and the organization of deviant activities. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Sociology Through Literature
description: Social Science Through the use of contemporary and historical literature, students will study selected historically marginalized populations and communities with a focus on culture, values, social oppression, diversity, and social functioning. Prerequisite: ENGL& 101 with a 2.0 or better.

title: Sociology of the Digital World
description: Social Science This course explores the social impacts of digital technology, online networks, and online communities. Topics include virtual identity development, online interaction, cyber communities, the digital divide, as well as social change and problems related to digital technology. Prerequisite: SOC& 101 .

title: Intro to Sociology
description: (SS/DSJ) Introduces the subject matter, theories and methods of sociology. Examines the social forces and processes that shape our personalities, institutions, culture, and society. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Social Problems
description: (SS/DSJ) This survey course studies major social problems in contemporary society, their nature, development, social causes, and possible solutions with an emphasis on the intersection of social identities including, but not limited to race, class, gender, and sexuality. Various topics include the impact of poverty, crime, drug addiction and prejudice on the individual and society. Prerequisite: Accuplacer placement in ENGL& 101 strongly recommended.

title: Spanish for Health Professionals
description: This course introduces students to Spanish in the context of health care. Grammar, vocabulary, and cultural aspects are presented in a communicative approach.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Spanish I
description: Humanities First course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the Spanish-speaking countries.

title: Spanish II
description: Humanities Second course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the Spanish-speaking countries. Prerequisite: SPAN& 121 or instructor permission

title: Spanish III
description: Humanities Third course of a three-course sequence. Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening abilities with an introduction to the geography and culture of the Spanish-speaking countries. Prerequisite: SPAN& 122 or instructor permission

title: Spanish IV
description: Humanities The principles of Spanish syntax, lexicon, and grammar. Explores the history, geography, and culture of Spanish speaking countries. Prerequisite: SPAN& 123 or permission of instructor.

title: Intro to Surgical Technology
description: ​​This course introduces the surgical technology profession including the history of surgical technology, and the role of the surgical technologist. Other topics introduced include education, certification, and associations of the profession, communication and teamwork, medicolegal aspects of surgical technology, health care facility structure and environment, and supporting the psychosocial needs of the patient. ​
prerequisites: Acceptance into the Surgical Technologist Program.

title: ​​Skills Lab I​
description: This course prepares students to preform basic preoperative skills. Learners will also gain knowledge of diagnostic and assessment procedures, environmental hazards, and infection control techniques.
prerequisites: Acceptance into the Surgical Technologist Program.

title: ​​Surgical Instrumentation​
description: ​​This course introduces students to surgical instrumentation found in the operating room within a variety of specialties. Students will learn how instruments are manufactured, instrument grades, instrument names, instrument categories, instrument functions, how to pass instruments, how to troubleshoot instruments, and the concepts behind decontamination, sterilization, and disinfection. ​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in the Surgical Technoligist Program.

title: ​​Skills Lab II ​
description: This is the second of four skills lab courses within the Surgical Technologist program. Students are introduced to preoperative and intraoperative skills, including draping sterile furniture, setting up the sterile field, surgical counts, medication labeling and handling, receiving, and labeling, blade and suture loading, urinary catheterization, surgical skin prep, draping the surgical patient, participating in the surgical TIMEOUT (safety pause), as well as pass instruments and sharps. Students will also learn about energy sources in surgery, moving, handling, and positioning the surgical patient, surgical skin preparation and patient draping.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: Surgical Procedures I
description: ​In this course students will learn concepts, terms, theory, equipment, instrumentation, supplies, anatomy, and procedures pertaining to minimally invasive surgery, robotic-assisted surgery, general surgery, gynecological and obstetrical surgery, and genitourinary surgery.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Skills Lab III​
description: This is the third out of four skills lab courses within the Surgical Technologist program. Students will build on previously learned intraoperative skills within different surgical specialties. Students will gain insight on postoperative care and skills including cleaning the surgical wound and applying dressings and drains. Students will further their skills in maintaining aseptic technique and correctly addressing breaks in technique, properly communicating with all surgical team members. Students will also learn about death and dying.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: Surgical Procedures II
description: ​In this course students will learn concepts, terms, theory, equipment, instrumentation, supplies, anatomy, and procedures pertaining to ophthalmic surgery, surgery of the ear, nose, pharynx, and larynx, oral and maxillofacial surgery, plastic and reconstructive surgery, and orthopedic surgery.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Perioperative Pharmacology​
description: ​​In this course students will learn the theory and concepts behind basic pharmacology, medication development, regulation, and resources, medication administration, antibiotics, diagnostic agents, diuretics, hormones, medications that affect coagulation, ophthalmic agents, fluids and irrigation solutions, antineoplastic chemotherapy agents, preoperative medications, patient monitoring and local and regional anesthesia, general anesthesia, and emergency situations. ​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: Surgical Procedures III
description: ​​In this course students will learn concepts, terms, theory, equipment, instrumentation, supplies, anatomy, and procedures pertaining to vascular and microvascular surgery, thoracic and pulmonary surgery, cardiac surgery, pediatric surgery, neurosurgery, and emergency trauma surgery. ​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Skills Lab IV​
description: This is the final skills lab course in the Surgical Technologist program. In this course learners will demonstrate proficiency as a surgical technologist student entering clinical externship by highlighting preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative skills acquired through the previous didactic and skills lab courses.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Intro to Clinical Externship​
description: This course is an introduction to surgical services. Students will gain in person experience of what it is like to work in sterile processing, and the operating room department. Students will observe preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative skills from professional operating room teams.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Professional and Certification Preparation I​
description: This course will assist in preparing students for their future career as Surgical Technologists. In this course students will design a resume and cover letter and prepare to sit for the national certification exam. NBSTSA certification prep includes study aids through AST and other professional resources.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: Intermediate Clinical Externship
description: Students will gain experience working in the perioperative environment through our clinical affiliates. Students will work alongside professional preceptors in the second and first scrub role. Students must maintain aseptic technique, communicate professionally with team members, assist with setting up the surgical field, draping, passing instruments and supplies, handling medications, handling specimen, dressing wounds, and assisting with turnovers. Between this course and ST215, students must reach at least 120 surgical cases by the end of the program.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Professional and Certification Preparation II​
description: ​​This course will assist in preparing students for their future career as Surgical Technologists. In this course students will practice mock interviews and prepare to sit for the national certification exam. NBSTSA certification prep includes study aids through AST and other professional resources.​
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: ​​Advanced Clinical Externship​
description: Students will gain experience working in operating rooms through our clinical affiliates. Students will collaborate with professional preceptors in the second and first scrub role. Students must maintain aseptic technique, communicate professionally with team members, assist with setting up the surgical field, draping, passing instruments and supplies, handling medications, handling specimen, dressing wounds, and assisting with turnovers. Students are responsible for reaching at least 120 surgical cases by the end of the program.
prerequisites: Continued enrollment in Surgical Technologist Program pathway.

title: LPN to ADN Transition
description: This course provides Licensed Practical Nurses (LPN) a transition to the Associate Degree of Nursing (RN) role. Competency in performing psychomotor and physical assessment skills of clients in acute and long-term care facilities will be addressed. Formerly Introduction of licensed practical nurses to the role of the RN, focusing on patient-centered care across the lifespan and incorporating social determinants of health.
prerequisites: ​​​​Admission to the ATA (LPN-RN) pathway.

title: Independent Study
description: Allows the student to pursue topics not offered in the College Catalog through in-depth coursework under the direction of an instructor. This course may include directed readings, coverage of special topics, and other independent study. The topic and scope of study, learning objectives, work required, methods of evaluation, and academic level (195 versus 295) will be determined in conference between the student and instructor. (can be offered in all subjects)
prerequisites: Instructor permission.

title: Oxyacetylene Welding
description:
Oxyacetylene welding and brazing in the flat, horizontal and vertical positions on mild steel plate; Oxy fuel cutting and plasma arc cutting processes. Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in WELD 106 or by permission of instructor.
,

title: Arc Welding I
description:
Shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) in the flat and horizontal positions on low carbon steel plate using E6010 and E7018 electrodes; Arc cutting with compressed air (CAC-A). Prerequisite: Completion of or concurrent enrollment in WELD 106 or by permission of instructor.
,

title: Arc Welding II
description:
Shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) making sound groove welds and fillet welds in all positions using E7018 electrodes on plain carbon steel. Prerequisite: WELD 101 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Arc Welding III
description:
Producing sound groove welds in the 2G and 3G positions on mild steel welded with the shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) process using E7018 electrode classification. Prerequisite: WELD 102 and completion of WELD 106 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Gas Tungsten Arc Welding
description:
Students develop skills required for Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (TIG) in the flat, horizontal and vertical positions on mild steel, stainless steel and aluminum plate. Prerequisite: WELD 100 and 107 or concurrent enrollment in WELD 107 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Gas Metal Arc/Flux Cored Arc Welding
description:
Students develop skills required for Gas Metal Arc (MIG) and flux cored arc welding in the flat, horizontal and vertical positions on mild steel and aluminum plate. Prerequisite: WELD 107 or concurrent enrollment in WELD 107 or permission of instructor.
,

title: Welding Technical Orientation I
description: Beginning welding theory with emphasis on safety, WELD processes of oxyacetylene welding (OAW), oxyfuel cutting (OFC), shielded metal arc welding (SMAW), carbon arc cutting with compressed air (CAC-A), electrical fundamentals and blue print reading.

title: Welding Technical Orientation II
description: Advanced welding, cutting, and manufacturing processes and American Welding Society WELD symbol reading and interpretation. Prerequisite: WELD 106 or permission of instructor.

title: Welding Metallurgy
description: A study of the metallurgy of welding and joining both ferrous and nonferrous metals, primarily as used in building and repair.

title: Pipe Welding I
description:
Producing sound open root groove welds in the 2G, 5G, and 6G positions on mild steel pipe welded with the shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) process using E6010 and E7018 electrode classification. Prerequisite: A grade of 3.0 or higher in WELD 103 and WELD 106 or instructor approval.
,

title: Pipe Welding II
description:
Producing sound open root groove welds in the 2G and 5G positions on mild steel pipe welded with the gas tungsten arc welding (GTAW) process using ER 70 S-2 filler metal and E7018 electrode classification. Prerequisite: A grade of 3.0 or higher in WELD 103 , WELD 104 , WELD 107 , and WELD 111 or instructor approval.
,

title: Applied Problem Solving
description: Uses math concepts and models in a lecture/ discovery format to enhance problem-solving skills required in the workplace. (Same as TEC-D 145 ) Prerequisite: MATH 091 with a grade of 2.0 or above or satisfactory placement test score.

description: The fabrication and manufacture of welded projects selected by the student or assigned by the instructor. Includes designing, cost analysis, ordering materials, and fabrication of projects.
prerequisites: Advanced standing with instructor permission.
